Classify these NIH grants by SCIENTIFIC CONTENT (not administrative mechanism).

These are F/K/T awards that were previously auto-classified as "training" based on activity code.
Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 11066462
activity_code: K43
title: Development of a Next-generation Rapid Phenotypic Assay for Drug-Resistant M. tuberculosis
org_name: STELLENBOSCH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Drug-resistant tuberculosis is a major health concern which requires updated and improved diagnostic tools to
inform individualised and standardised treatment protocols. There is a particular need for tests that can provide
rapid results in high-throughput laboratories. This project will provide such tools in the form of microvolume,
arrayed tests based on growth detection by means of standard methods and fluorescent probes.

application_id: 11242177
activity_code: F31
title: Functional Interrogation of Germinal Center Independent Memory B Cells in Head and Neck Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immunotherapy focuses on killing tumor cells by stimulating and reinvigorating cell-mediated immunity in
cancer patients without considering the impact of humoral immunity. Our work will contribute to future cancer
treatments by investigating the role of B cells in antitumor immunity, which will improve targeting of humoral
immunity to amplify the immune response to cancer.

application_id: 10996200
activity_code: K43
title: Integration of Mental Health Services in HIV Care for Adolescents and Youth in Kenya
org_name: KENYATTA  NATIONAL  HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative This research aims to inform integration of mental health care within routine HIV services for adolescents and youth in Kenya. We will develop, pilot and cost implementation strategies for integrating assessments for common mental disorders among adolescents and youth with HIV ages 15 to 24 years, and effective referrals to specialists. Dr. Mugo seeks to build expertise in advanced implementation science methods, qualitative and mixed methods, stakeholder engagement, and skills in scientific leadership in order to transition to an independent research career that advances high quality mental health care for adolescents and youth with HIV.

activity_code: K08
title: Cellular and Molecular Response to Gentamicin-Induced Injury in Underdeveloped Kidneys
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Humans born preterm have low nephron number and are at risk for acute kidney injury and chronic kidney disease. We plan to use a mouse model of low nephron number to study the cellular and molecular basis of kidney disease in this population. Our goal is to discover prevention and treatment strategies for acute kidney injury and the development of chronic kidney disease in humans born preterm.

application_id: 11071477
activity_code: F31
title: A Novel Immune Tolerization Strategy for Investigating the Trade-offs between Oncogene Pathogenicity and Immunogenicity.
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed project aims to investigate early and critical immunoediting events in cancer pathogenesis.
Employing an innovative immune-tolerization strategy capable of uncoupling oncogene-tumorigenicity and
immunogenicity, this research seeks to elucidate distinct immune evasion mechanisms in the presence and
absence of immune pressure. Overall, this study stands to advance our understanding of early immune evasion
mechanisms in cancer tumorigenesis while potentially revealing new therapeutic targets for early disease
intervention.

application_id: 11070711
activity_code: F31
title: Deciphering the role of N6-methyladenosine (m6A) on mRNA during adaptive stress
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Prostate cancer cells can activate adaptive pathways in response to oncogenic stress, allowing for therapy
resistance, cell survival, and cancer progression. This proposal seeks to identify the mechanism by which cancer
cells dynamically adapt to treatments through mRNA regulation with the goal of determining the best ways to
inhibit this process. In identifying this regulation and recognizing ways to inhibit this adaptive stress response,
potential therapeutic opportunities to prevent metastasis in prostate cancer can be identified.

application_id: 11129881
activity_code: K43
title: A social media mHealth intervention to improve retention in care for adolescents and young adults with HIV in Uganda
org_name: MBARARA UNIVERSITY/SCIENCE/ TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project narrative
Adolescents and young adults with HIV (AYWH) aged 15-24 years are the fastest-growing population of people
living with HIV worldwide. AYWH have worse outcomes along the HIV continuum of care than adults with HIV;
HIV/AIDS is the second leading cause of mortality and the fourth leading cause of disability among AYWH
worldwide. This study will develop an adolescent-tailored, mobile health (mHealth) intervention to improve
retention in care and thus has the potential to improve AIDS-related mortality and morbidity among AYWH and
decrease new onward transmission.

application_id: 11084518
activity_code: K43
title: Use of Pharmacoepidemiology to Understand Predictors and Impact of Low-level Viremia in Persons with HIV in West Africa
org_name: NIGERIAN INSTITUTE OF MEDICAL RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Persistent low-level viremia (LLV) in persons with HIV who are on antiretroviral therapy (ART) is a barrier to
achieving the goal of zero transmission and eradication of HIV, but not much is known about the impact of
LLV on attaining the ART goal of viral suppression in West Africa. The proposed research will use
pharmacoepidemiologic methods to assess the prevalence of LLV and impact on ART outcomes. The study
will also use mathematical modeling to predict future LLV with adherence to ART measured by innovative
pharmacological techniques.

application_id: 11182515
activity_code: K43
title: Unravelling the role of gene therapy on spinal disc mediated low back pain
org_name: THE FEDERAL UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY, AKURE, NIGERIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Intervertebral disc degeneration (IVDD) is a multifactorial process regarded as the primary cause of low back
pain that exerts an enormous public health burden and disability on millions of individuals with current conserva-
tive surgical options having limited treatment efficacy. This study evaluates the modulating role of gene therapy,
an unleveraged therapeutic intervention, on pyroptosis-mediated pathways in a rat model of IVDD progression
over 12 weeks. Uncovering the potential of gene therapy as an alternative treatment option for low back pain
and its role in pyroptosis proteins aligns with the National Institute of Health’s mission and Fogarty International
Center’s vision statements to reduce disease burden and disability, improve health, and extend longevity for...

application_id: 11163959
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating evolutionary antagonism between tumors and lymphocytes
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this proposal is to investigate the relationship between clonotypic/phenotypic diversity between
tumors and lymphocytes in the context of cancer metastasis. We will evaluate mechanisms of clonal and
phenotypic selection and diversiﬁcation of tumors and lymphocytes, identifying evolutionary mechanisms utilized
by either compartment to evade or overcome the other. The resulting insights into coevolution of tumors and the
immune system will provide an integrative model of tumor evolution and immune dysfunction during metastasis,
informing the development of therapies that target these evasion mechanisms, such as by enhancing the
diversity of the lymphocyte compartment to overcome limited diversity.

application_id: 11166079
activity_code: F31
title: Determining the spatial contexts that modulate immune cell phenotypes in colorectal cancer
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to understand how spatially organized multicellular communities in colorectal cancer promote
or hinder the anti-tumor immune response. The goals are to identify molecular interactions that influence
immune cell activity and to identify markers that can predict which patients will benefit from immune checkpoint
inhibitors. The findings from this project could ultimately extend eligibility for immune checkpoint inhibitors to
more colorectal cancer patients and identify targets for new forms of immunotherapy.

application_id: 10977243
activity_code: K43
title: Mycobacterial Epigenetics: Investigating the impact of DNA methylation on antibiotic tolerance and persistence
org_name: STELLENBOSCH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mycobacterium tuberculosis population heterogeneity, including persister cell formation, can prevent effective therapeutic treatment, resulting in extensive treatment duration and recurrent tuberculosis infection. Triggered persister formation in response to environmental stress may be facilitated through DNA methylation of the M. tuberculosis genome. We aim to demonstrate a role for DNA methylation in the M. tuberculosis stress response and in promoting population heterogeneity, including the formation of a persister sub-population.

application_id: 10918287
activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating the tumor suppressive effects of the sirtuin, SIRT1, in triple-negative breast cancer
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The expression level of SIRT1 is often reduced in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), an aggressive subtype of breast cancer that is difficult to treat and metastasizes with high frequency. The proposed research strategy will investigate the effects of decreased SIRT1 activity and expression in breast cancer progression and the regulatory mechanism that decreases SIRT1 expression in TNBC. Understanding the important tumor suppressive role of SIRT1 will provide new therapeutic strategies for the treatment of TNBC.

title: Development of a smart transformable nanotherapeutic against cancers with acquired resistance to EGFR-targeted therapies caused by MET amplification
org_name: KHON KAEN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The administration of epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) targeted therapies to cancer patients invariably leads to the development of drug resistance, mainly caused by a mesenchymal-epithelial transition (MET) amplification. This innovative research project addresses this issue by rigorously developing smart transformable EGFR/MET-targeting peptide nanoparticles for treatment of cancers with acquired resistance to EGFR-targeted therapies due to MET amplification. The successful completion of this project will not only lead to the creation of new classes of safe and effective targeted nanotherapeutics against cancers but also open avenues for the advancement of functional nanomaterials in biomedical applications.

application_id: 11162350
activity_code: K43
title: Molecular and cellular underpinnings of placental malaria
org_name: MOUNT KENYA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Malaria infection during pregnancy causes red blood cells containing the malaria parasite to attach to the
placenta, the organ vital for development and growth of the baby during pregnancy. Placenta infection with
malaria disrupts placental function and is linked to poor pregnancy outcomes like low birth weight, premature
birth, and stillbirth. This proposal will seek to determine the biological effects through which placental malaria
alters placental function and identify strategies for exploiting this knowledge for the development of placental
malaria interventions as well as tools for placental malaria detection during pregnancy.

application_id: 11244377
activity_code: F31
title: Interrogating Mechanisms of Suppressed Tpex Immunotherapy Response in Metastatic Tumor-Draining Lymph Nodes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Progenitor-exhausted T cells (Tpex) are the primary subset of tumor-reactive CD8 T cells that respond to
immune-checkpoint blockade therapy (ICB) in tumor-draining lymph nodes. The capacity of Tpex to respond to
ICB, however, is restricted in the metastatic lymph nodes (metLN) of patients with head and neck squamous
cell carcinoma (HNSCC); Tpex are less abundant, less likely to egress out of the lymph node into the blood, and
more likely to co-localize with immunosuppressive immune cells. This proposal investigates the mechanisms
by which Tpex responses to ICB are restricted in metLN in a clinically relevant mouse model of HNSCC to
reveal strategies by which to reinvigorate this critical pool of CD8 T cells in patients with advanced-stage
disease.

phr: Peripheral artery disease (PAD), an atherosclerotic disease of the leg arteries, puts patients at high risk for myocardial infarction, stroke, and ischemic limb events. preliminary effectiveness of MI-Cope-Care in a pilot randomized controlled trial. If MI-Cope-Care is found to be

activity_code: K43
title: Adapting evidence-based stigma reduction interventions to address HIV and drug use stigma among family members of people who use drugs
org_name: MUHIMBILI UNIVERSITY/ ALLIED HLTH SCIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE HIV and drug use stigma are a barrier to HIV prevention, care and treatment services access among people who use drugs. Although a family is critical to support people who use drugs to enter and remain in HIV and drug prevention and treatment, globally there are no interventions to address HIV and drug use stigma that people who use drugs face from their family members. This study will address this gap by adapting existing evidence-based health facility HIV and drug use stigma reduction interventions and create a stigma-reduction intervention for family members of people who use drugs that will address both HIV and drug use stigma coming from family members going towards people who use drugs.

activity_code: K76
title: Aging Well with COPD through Geriatrics-Palliative Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative
COPD is the third leading cause of death in older Americans, and older adults with
COPD rarely receive proactive geriatrics-palliative care before the end of life. This study
will refine and pilot test a telehealth, palliative nurse coach-led geriatrics-palliative care
intervention for older adults with COPD and their family caregivers. The results will
support a fully-powered randomized controlled trial that will seek to improve the health
and well-being of older adults with COPD and their family caregivers.

title: Health Care Use After Functional Disability: Opportunities to Improve the Care of Older Adults
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research will use innovative methods and existing data sources to examine the clinical, health system,
and policy factors that impact health care use after functional disability. The population of older adults with
functional disability is growing, and disability results in high levels of potentially burdensome health care use.
Assessing the role of health systems and policies, such as Medicare Advantage, in shaping care for this
population is critical to improving the quality of care for all older adults.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our goal is to train graduate students and postdoctoral Oncology Fellows to become committed cancer researchers who work to together as scholars, experimentalists, and physician-scientists to advance cancer research and treatment. We are particularly proud of this grant because it has allowed us to innovate in our training by developing cutting-edge courses, addressing newly recognized needs of our trainees for their career development, training a more diverse group of students, and simultaneously doing substantive, compelling cancer research.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

benefits and harms, particularly for older adults in whom cancer occurs in a complex setting of multimorbidity
and geriatric conditions. Little is known about the contribution of these geriatric conditions to RT-related
outcomes in older adults with advanced cancer, including how they relate to (1) difficulties completing RT; (2)
changes in function, hospitalization, and mortality after RT; and (3) self-reported toxicities and quality of life.

related adverse outcomes in older adults, results of which will support a future R01 application that tests a

title: Human stem cell derived forebrain model to study persistent neurotoxic effects of trans-placental developmental exposures to environmental pollutants
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pregnant women are exposed to an increasing number of environmental contaminants in their daily life, with
potential harmful effects for the developing nervous system of the fetus. These exposures during early stages of
fetal brain development can cause persistent changes and increase the risk on Alzheimer’s Disease and related
dementia (ADRD) later in life. This project aims to provide training to independence via development of an in
vitro placental-fetal brain model to investigate early-life exposures impact on later-life susceptibility to brain
diseases including ADRD.

application_id: 10909730
activity_code: K76
title: Developing a framework to individualize surgical decision-making for older adults with primary hyperparathyroidism: Administrative supplement to promote research continuity and retention
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To make informed surgical treatment decisions, older adults and the providers who care for them must weigh the long-term benefits of surgery against the short-term health risks for an individual patient. However, counseling on the tradeoffs of surgical intervention too often rely on population average risk rather than individualized estimates of risks/benefits. This research will create a decision support tool for the management of primary hyperparathyroidism in older adults and, in doing so, establish a framework for how to combine individualized risk prediction with stakeholder feedback to develop surgical decision support that will inform treatment recommendations for older adults and reduce long-term morbidity.

title: Interrogating Dynamic Regulation of Pre-Synaptic Release Probability In Vivo
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative:  Release probability (P(r)) of neurotransmitter containing vesicles is a critical component of information transfer between neurons and is dynamically regulated by a variety of molecular and circuit mechanisms. Here I combine an array of novel imaging, electrophysiological, behavioral, and pharmacological methods to interrogate the regulation of P(r) in vivo at multiple different scales. This will lead to a novel understanding of how individual pre-synaptic sites are regulated and bridge a wealth of literature in reduced preparations to the awake, behaving brain.

an innovative clinical decision framework which will incorporate geriatric principles to individualize chronic

application_id: 10904941
activity_code: K01
title: Predictors of Pain Severity and Pain-Related Outcomes in Individuals with Sickle Cell Disease
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Sickle cell disease is a painful inherited disorder that affects approximately 100,000 people in the United States, and more than half of these individuals develop chronic or persistent pain that is often severe and disabling. The factors that predict whether an individual with sickle cell disease will develop severe, disabling pain are unclear. The goal of this project is to identify the factors that predict severe pain outcomes in these patients in order to improve pain management strategies and guide future studies of non-opioid therapies for treatment of their pain.

application_id: 11241774
activity_code: F31
title: Dopaminergic modulation of NMDA receptor signaling in the Nucleus Accumbens
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative
Understanding how dopaminergic signaling regulates neuronal activity is essential to understanding how brain
reward circuits become altered as a result of chronic drug use. Dopamine receptor signaling importantly tunes
the activity of glutamatergic NMDA receptors to regulate neuronal excitability, yet the exact mechanisms
underlying dopamine receptor modulation of NMDA receptors remains unclear. A better understanding of
dopamine-glutamate interactions has the potential to identify new strategies for investigating substance use
disorders.

application_id: 10782608
activity_code: F32
title: Genomic mechanisms of BDNF signaling in the brain
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although neurotrophins, such as brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), play an essential role in neural circuit development and function, little is known about how neurotrophin-regulated transcription influences experience- dependent plasticity in the brain. This study seeks to characterize the genomic mechanisms by which BDNF regulates the maturation of inhibition within the mouse visual cortex. By identifying specific transcription factors and target genes that mediate the genomic response to BDNF in the brain, this proposal will provide novel insights into the etiology of neurological disorders that display a loss of BDNF production, including Alzheimer’s and Huntingtin’s disease.

application_id: 10937625
activity_code: K76
title: Evaluating Sleep Deficiency in Persons with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative Sleep deficiency is common and detrimental in persons with Alzheimer's disease and related dementias (ADRD), but improved sleep measures are needed to understand of the relationship between sleep and neurocognitive function in ADRD. The goals of the proposed research are to characterize sleep deficiency in persons with ADRD using feasible, accurate, and comprehensive measures and to evaluate the cross-sectional relationships between sleep deficiency, markers of neuronal damage/plasticity, and ADRD pathology. The work proposed may elucidate mechanisms linking sleep deficiency and neurodegeneration and establish targets for future sleep- promoting interventions, ultimately helping to slow progression of disease and improve quality of life in persons with ADRD.

application_id: 10917131
activity_code: K76
title: Individualizing Anticoagulant use in Older Adults with Atrial Fibrillation
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Atrial fibrillation is the most common heart rhythm disorder in older adults, and it substantially increases the risk of disabling strokes. Anticoagulant treatment reduces the risk of stroke but increases the likelihood of disabling bleeding events. We lack a way to individualize treatment decisions for older adults—this proposal will use multiple studies to better define the individual risk and benefits of anticoagulants in older adults with atrial fibrillation.

title: An Analysis Of The Multi-Level Factors That Impact Provision Of Emergency Medical Services To Hispanic Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative Emergency medical services (EMS) serve as an important entry point in the United States’(U.S.) healthcare system, especially for Hispanic older adults who have challenges accessing preventive and diagnostic care, and who rely on emergency services to meet their healthcare needs. application_id: 11135484
activity_code: K76
title: Developing and testing implementation strategies to support the STEADI for falls risk management in outpatient rehabilitation
org_name: UNIV OF ARKANSAS FOR MED SCIS
phr: Project Narrative
Falls among older adults are a leading cause of disability, death, and high healthcare costs.
This proposal addresses the National Institute on Aging and Healthy People 2030 objectives to
develop strategies to reduce falls, their consequences, and fall-related deaths by developing
and testing strategies to implement the CDC-developed STEADI (Stopping Elderly Accidents,
Deaths, and Injuries) for falls prevention of all older adults attending outpatient rehabilitation.
Our proposed research will investigate and test how best to incorporate falls prevention into
outpatient physical therapy visits that many older adults already attend and, how to best help
older individuals follow the recommendations they receive about how to prevent falls.

application_id: 10918310
activity_code: R25
title: #MYHealth: Training the Next Generation of Health Scientists Through Participatory Research in Adolescent Health
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The overall objective of this proposal is to train and support underrepresented minority high school students (Black, Latinx, Indigenous, low-income, and future first-generation college students) to be co-researchers in a community-based participatory research program focused on adolescent health. At least 350 minority high school students will collaborate with and be mentored by our faculty team over the duration of the program, increasing drivers of persistence in science. This program will ultimately have a significant and widespread impact on pervasive disparities in the research workforce.

application_id: 10908382
activity_code: K00
title: Muscle and Bone Growth in Aging
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The musculoskeletal system declines with age, largely due to physical inactivity and the depletion of growth factors, which leads to muscle weakness and increased risk of bone fracture. Growth factors in the muscle-bone unit play a role in improving muscle and bone regeneration that are further enhanced when physical activity is resumed; however, little is known about the mechanisms of how muscle interacts with bone and vice versa. The purpose of this proposal is to aid in a smooth transition from pre-doctoral training on the muscle to post-doctoral training on the bone to delineate the biochemical mechanisms involved to increase muscle mass and function along with bone mass and strength, in order to improve the quality of life in the aging community.

activity_code: K76
title: Identifying Unmet Needs Driving Disproportionate and Avoidable Nursing Home Placements among Black and Latino Persons Living With Dementia
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The disproportionate rise in nursing home use among Black and Latino persons living with dementia (PLWD) reflects the inadequacy of existing programs and policies to support aging in place for these most at-risk populations. The goals of this study are to 1) identify the unmet needs that drive Black and Latino PLWD to experience potentially avoidable NH placements at greater rates than their White counterparts using an exploratory sequential mixed methods research design and 2) translate preliminary findings onto the public agenda as a policy issue. This work will inform intervention and policy development aimed at filling gaps in needs significant to Black and Latino PLWD’s ability to remain in the community for their long-term care as long as possible.

title: Metabolic regulation of healthy aging by diet, mTOR signaling, and skeletal muscle
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON

application_id: 11131188
activity_code: K76
title: Hospice Use in Older Adults with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias: Patterns, Policies, and Caregiver Experiences
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: NARRATIVE
Although almost half of the over 1.5 million Medicare beneficiaries who enroll in hospice each year have
Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD), hospice is poorly suited to the needs of people with
dementia. Hospice care for people with dementia is both more expensive and lower quality than for other
populations, such as those with cancer. The proposed study will evaluate how recent changes in the way
Medicare pays for hospice impacts the quality and costs of care for hospice enrollees with dementia compared
to those with cancer, which will help determine how to provide the highest-value hospice care for the hundreds
of thousands of people with dementia enrolled in hospice every year.

title: Optimizing anesthesia to prevent postoperative cognitive and functional decline in older adults
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE 
Postoperative delirium (POD) and postoperative cognitive dysfunction (POCD) are the most common surgical complications experienced by older adults, and they are associated with increased hospital length of stay, early departure from the workforce, increased risk of Alzheimer’s disease, and mortality. Nearly 40% of surgical 
patients are older adults, and as the surgical population ages faster than the U.S. population, anesthesiologists are poised to improve the health of older Americans by identifying strategies to prevent POD and POCD. Completing the proposed research will provide evidence that is readily translatable to clinical practice to aid the 
choice of general anesthetic strategy to minimize the incidence of POD, POCD, and functional decline in older adults and ...

application_id: 11177967
activity_code: K01
title: Optimization and development of extracellular matrix-targeting probes for PET-based assessment of lung fibrosis disease activity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Assessment of active pulmonary fibrosis, a progressive, fatal disease characterized by unrelenting scarring and
stiffening of the lungs, is critical for appropriate therapeutic treatment. This project is relevant to public health as
it aims to determine the capacity of a novel positron emission tomography (PET) probe to accurately detect
pulmonary fibrosis disease activity in mice and chemically optimize it to enable future clinical translation. These
studies will serve as a foundation for future clinical applications of this PET-based probe as a non-invasive
method for assessment of activity for this deadly disease, in order to determine best treatment for patients,
reduce their illness, and lengthen their lives.

title: Decoding hyperexcitability in malignant glioma
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The study of neural-tumor cell interactions and how they contribute to glioma progression represents an emerging research paradigm in brain tumor biology. title: Estrogen's Neuroprotective Effects on the Brain-Barrier in Restrictive Eating Disorders
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Estrogen deficiency is common in R-EDs, which may drive neuroinflammation given the anti-inflammatory effects of estrogen on the brain barrier. application_id: 11098708
activity_code: K76
title: Virtual Reality and Computerized Cognitive Intervention for Mild Cognitive Impairment in Heart Failure
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 This research project is relevant to public health because both heart failure (HF) and mild cognitive
impairment (e.g., trouble remembering and concentrating beyond normal aging) are highly prevalent among
older adults in the United States. Despite advances in health care, there are no effective interventions for treating
cognitive impairment in HF, which if left untreated, leads to suboptimal health, quality of life, and shorter survival.
Therefore, we propose to test the effect of a dual-component intervention comprised of a virtual reality-based
cognitive restoration intervention and computerized cognitive training on cognitive function and overall health
among 172 older adults with HF who experience mild cognitive impairment.

application_id: 11124769
activity_code: K76
title: Establishing and Implementing Pneumonia Diagnosis in ED Older Adults: A Mixed Methods Approach
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pneumonia is diagnosed in over half a million U.S. ED visits by older adults each year and has high mortality.
Currently, emergency physicians lack the tools to reliably diagnose pneumonia and deliver life-saving care to
the correct patients. This proposal seeks to improve diagnostic accuracy thereby improving patient care and
outcomes by: investigating novel serum tests; deriving and piloting a new diagnostic pathway; and applying
group concept mapping to prepare for successful validation and implementation.

application_id: 11088929
activity_code: K76
title: The Role of Hypothalamic Dysfunction in Accelerating Aging in Humans
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative:
With development of preventative and therapeutic interventions, we were able to extend human life, but the
final years or even decades of life are riddled with diseases and disability. Aging is a common risk factor for all
aging-related diseases, therefore, if we could delay aging, we could extend the part of the life that is lived in
good health. Our study aims to discover if broken functioning of the hypothalamus, the brain region that
regulates hormones and metabolism of the entire body, is a reason for faster aging in some people, as these
discoveries could lead to future therapies to delay aging.

title: CoCM-PAL: Adapting Collaborative Care for Older Adults with Serious Illness and Comorbid Depression or Anxiety Receiving Ambulatory Palliative Care
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative: Older adults with serious illness have high rates of anxiety and depression not well addressed by current paradigms of care. This study aims to implement Collaborative Care, a model of mental health care integrated in medical care, in the palliative care setting to improve the treatment of depression and anxiety among older adults with serious illness. This work will lead the way to sustainable, scalable approaches to improving mental health care in the medical treatment of older adults.

title: Tailoring Rehabilitation to the needs of Older Adults with Cognitive Impairment and Dementia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In this study, we will develop a refined rehabilitation toolbox that can be used to improve outpatient physical
therapy service delivery for older adults with Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD). We need
this study because current rehabilitation approaches do not address the unique needs of older adults with
ADRD. Study findings will set a solid foundation for my future research and career as a leader in aging-focused
rehabilitation research.

application_id: 11176285
activity_code: R36
title: Multimodal Assessment and Longitudinal Trajectory of Callous-Unemotional Behaviors in Young Children: The Role of Parental Emotion Socialization
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposal focuses on conduct problems (CP) in young children, particularly the amplifying effects
of callous-unemotional (CU) behaviors and the potential buffering effects of parental emotion
socialization. The study aims to determine the convergence of physiological functioning, observed
empathy, and parent/teacher ratings into a single CU construct, assess within and between person
variability in initial levels of CU and over time, and examine how parental emotion socialization may
potentially mitigate the negative impact of CU on CP severity one year later.

application_id: 11180182
activity_code: K00
title: Elucidating the Mechanisms of Translational Approaches to Enhance Recovery of Aged Muscle
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Impaired recovery from skeletal muscle disuse in aging results in heightened risk of disability and falls contributing to decreased healthspan. During the F99/K00 phases, I will study the mechanisms of novel therapeutic compounds and cellular interactions to promote muscle recovery in aged mice. This proposal will be guided by a panel of muscle aging experts and training experiences across various disciplines and institutions to promote my training in muscle aging research.

application_id: 10914126
activity_code: K00
title: Training in lifespan behavioral, social, and neuroscience research connecting early-life cognitive decline to late-life ADRD
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Findings from the proposed line of research will link accelerated brain and biological aging in midlife to brain aging in older adults with cognitive impairment as well as Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD). These findings may help uncover the origins and risk factors for individual differences in brain aging that could help identify those most in need of intervention in midlife as well as help develop novel preventions to target ADRD before disease onset. The long-term goal of this line of research is to lower the global burden of ADRD by advancing our understanding of individual differences in brain aging.

activity_code: K76
title: Assessing Needs and Advancing Dignity-Conserving Care for Hospitalized, Incarcerated Older Adults: Clinical Data, Patient Experience, and Policy Evaluation
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative The proposed work will address key knowledge gaps about the clinical, geriatric, and ethical dimensions of need for hospitalized incarcerated older adults. We will leverage a unique dataset from a 5-state hospital network, spanning 25 years and thousands of hospital admissions, to define prevalence and trends in factors such as age, delirium, and advance directive completion. We will also conduct interviews to characterize the experience of hospitalization for an incarcerated older adult from multiple vantage points: patient, family, correctional officer, and bedside nurse. Finally, we will evaluate prevalence and contents of state and federal policies pertinent to the hospital care of older adults.

application_id: 10919162
activity_code: F32
title: The Impact of Gossip on Children's Feelings of Belongingness
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE When children feel like they do not belong, it increases their distress and their risk for later anxiety/depressive symptoms and drug use. Feelings of belonging can be undermined or strengthened by gossip (i.e., negative or positive talk about others), yet little is known about the origins of gossip in early childhood. The current proposal uses both experimental methods and naturalistic observations to identify the developmental genesis of gossip as well as the immediate and long-term effects of spreading gossip or being the target of gossip on children’s feelings of belonging and distress in peer groups, which is of critical importance to children’s social and psychological well-being.

Over 1 million older adults with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD) live alone in the US, and
little is known about their use of medications, support for medication management, or how to intervene
effectively to optimize their use of medications. The objectives of this Beeson K76 proposal are to characterize
medication use and medication management supports among older people with ADRD who live alone, to
understand in-depth multifaceted perspectives on their use of medications, and to develop a stakeholder-
informed clinical decision framework to support clinicians and guide future interventions to optimize medication
use in this group. Ultimately, the information learned from this proposal will lead to the development of clinical
interventions to improve qu...

title: Crisis migration and youth mental health: The moderating influences of culture, context, and community

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Crisis migration has surged across the world, with an increasing number of populations forced to flee their home countries to escape violence, persecution, political unrest, and/or socio-economic turmoil, Venezuela, for example, has seen millions of families leave the country to escape an unstable humanitarian crisis and resettle in the United States (U.S.).3 The proposed project seeks to understand the mental health effects of such crisis migration on youth, as well as the extent to which various cultural strengths and community factors may moderate and mitigate these mental health risks. application_id: 10973109
activity_code: K76
title: Estimating the treatment effect of cardiovascular medications to modify the risk for future cognitive decline in older adults
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Older adults frequently cite maintenance of cognitive function and prevention of dementia as their top health priority. This is highly relevant to cardiovascular treatment decisions as the modifiable risk factors for cognitive decline and cardiovascular disease overlap and early data suggest potentially differential treatment effects of cardiovascular therapies on cognitive outcomes. We propose to evaluate the effect of two of the most commonly prescribed cardiovascular therapies (Beta-blockers & Calcium Channel Blockers) on cognitive trajectory and risk for Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias among older adults by leveraging existing longitudinal data from SPRINT MIND and prospective randomized trial data from the PCORI-sponsored LIVEBETTER pragmatic trial.

application_id: 11085515
activity_code: R36
title: Probing the Proteome: Depressive Symptoms, Inflammation and Mitochondrial Perturbations in Acute HIV Infection
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Depressive disorders are three times more prevalent among people with HIV than in the general population and predict worse health outcomes along the HIV care continuum. Thus, there is a need to elucidate the biobehavioral determinants of depressive disorders to catalyze the development of novel pharmacologic and behavioral treatment for this prevalent HIV comorbidity. This project will advance our mechanistic understanding of how HIV-induced pathophysiological alterations beginning in acute HIV infection may amplify risk for depressive disorders by probing the proteome to identify differentially expressed proteins in PWH that have persistent depressive symptoms over 96 weeks despite initiation of suppressive ART.

application_id: 11318298
activity_code: F31
title: Disease Etiology and Structural Proteomics of Jbts17
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The work proposed in this fellowship will deepen our understanding of cilia biogenesis and how the disruption
of ciliary proteins leads to craniofacial ciliopathies. Specifically, mutations to Jbts17 result in Oral-Facial-Digital
Syndrome, Joubert Syndrome, and Meckel-Gruber Syndrome. By directly examining known Jbts17 disease
variants, this proposed work may reveal a new function of this protein during cilia biogenesis and may extend
our knowledge of craniofacial development and disease.

application_id: 11161907
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanistic studies of amino acid deprivation-induced hepatic fibrosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
This study aims to understand how amino acid depletion by PEGylated asparaginase (PEG-ASNase), a drug
used to treat leukemia, causes the activation of hepatic stellate cells (HSCs) and subsequent liver fibrosis. The
application describes a novel mechanism of liver fibrosis involving drug-induced activation of adipose
triglyceride lipase (ATGL) and the induction of the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPAR-γ)
transcription factor, leading to liver injury. The goals of this project are to elucidate how PEG-ASNase activates
fibrosis-causing HSCs and to develop strategies to prevent liver damage in patients receiving this treatment.

title: Adapting the ESTEEM intervention to improve mental health and PrEP uptake among men at risk of HIV

The proposed research is relevant to public health because it focuses on ethical and social

title: Influences of Prenatal Phthalate Exposure on Early Childhood Overweight/Obesity and Potentially Protective Dietary Strategies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DELAWARE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Phthalates are common chemicals in the environment that can disrupt hormones and change how the body accumulates and stores fat. We will investigate how being exposed to these chemicals before birth is linked to childhood obesity, and whether certain diets can help prevent this.

application_id: 11165998
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of exocrine pancreatic innate immunity via microbially induced IL-22
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
IL-22 is a microbe-induced cytokine that promotes tissue repair but at excessive levels also inflammation.
While these actions are best studied in the gut, here we aim to investigate the regulation of IL-22 signaling in
the pancreas and propose that gut microbes are key modulators of pancreatic IL-22. Therapeutically targeting
IL-22 may be beneficial for pancreatic diseases including pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

application_id: 11189664
activity_code: K99
title: Examining the Relationship Between Ongoing Alcohol Use, Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors and Related Constructs, and Behavioral Economic Decision-Making
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Alcohol use and suicidal thoughts and behaviors are strongly related, but the exact relationship between
alcohol and suicide risk is poorly understood. Behavioral economic decision-making, such as rapid devaluing
of delayed rewards (i.e., delay discounting), appears to be a common factor for increased alcohol use and
suicidal thoughts and behaviors. The proposed research is to better understand how behavioral economic
decision-making differs between individuals who use alcohol and those who use alcohol and have suicidal
thoughts and behaviors.

application_id: 11085643
activity_code: K99
title: Alcohol metabolism potentiates HIV-induced lung and liver multimorbidity via inter-organ crosstalk
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Heavy alcohol use is associated with increased respiratory symptom burden in people living with HIV (PLWH), and alcohol increases the risk of sepsis and multimorbidity of the lungs and liver in PLWH. In this proposal, the PI will determine if alcohol and HIV induce apoptosis of lung immune cells, alveolar macrophages, and promote the release of extracellular vesicles which impair alveolar epithelial barrier function, causing lung leak, and potentiate liver injury; thereby exacerbating lung injury. The findings from the study will not only identify potential treatment strategies for PLWH with AUD but also raise awareness of the impact of alcohol misuse on HIV-induced multi-organ damage.

activity_code: F31
title: Leveraging in vivo protein tracking to identify novel immune markers associated with obesity driven disease
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE.
The intestinal epithelium, responsible for transmitting signals from nutrient and toxin intake, plays a piv-
otal role in interorgan communication during the development of obesity. In this grant, we will determine
how obesity impacts intestinal protein secretion, and how adipose tissue immune cells are affected by
these intestine-secreted proteins during the development of obesity. Characterization of these secreted
biomarkers will contribute to the development of targeted therapeutics, advancing medical practices
and enhancing overall health for individuals affected by obesity-related maladies.

application_id: 11178694
activity_code: K01
title: Enteroendocrine Regulation of Intestinal Metabolism
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this project is to identify how the nutrient-sensing cell of the intestine, the enteroendocrine cell, may
signal changes in nutrient availability to other cells within the gut. This has significant implications for how
changes in dietary intake or pharmacological manipulation of enteroendocrine peptides may influence the
capacity for regeneration, nutrient absorption, and overall metabolic health. With the staggering rates of
metabolic and digestive disease in the United States, understanding how enteroendocrine cells regulate gut
function is a pressing public health concern.

application_id: 10903917
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the role of sleep in brain resilience during aging using a scalable and short-lived vertebrate model
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Sleep is a critical behavior that is essential to human health, and yet sleep dramatically deteriorates with age.
The fundamental question that I will answer in this proposed research program is how sleep impacts brain
resilience late in life. Findings from this work will change how we think about the long-term health impact of
sleep and provide a novel yet accessible treatment to rejuvenate the aged and diseased brain.

application_id: 11162070
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Ferrostatic Role of Adipose Tissue Macrophages
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Resident tissue macrophages have recently emerged as essential accessory cells that support proper
physiology and metabolism of the tissues they reside in. While the role of these cells in systemic iron homeostasis
is well known, whether and how they support iron homeostasis at the level of individual tissues is still unclear.
This project aims to dissect the mechanisms by which resident tissue macrophages maintain iron homeostasis
within the white adipose tissue, with the ultimate goal of highlighting their often overlooked contribution to organ
physiology, which in turn may shed light on the role of iron in initiation and progression of type 2 diabetes and
other disorders.

activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of the Pdx1 C-terminal interaction domains during pancreatic endocrine differentiation and proliferation.
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The reduction of functional insulin-secreting pancreatic  cells underlies the pathogenesis of all forms of diabetes.
The pancreas transcription factor and diabetes susceptibility gene, Pdx1, is critical for -cell differentiation and
proliferation. Knowledge gained from the proposed studies to evaluate the role of the Pdx1 C-terminus and its
interacting proteins SPOP and Oc1 can be used to design better strategies to generate and/or expand  cells in
vitro and in vivo which could aid in prevention, treatment, and reversal of the diabetes.

application_id: 10914660
activity_code: F31
title: Stress Granule Formation in the Antiretroviral-Mediated Dysregulation of Oligodendrocyte Maturation in HIV-HAND
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Despite widespread use of antiretroviral (ARV) therapy, approximately half of people living with HIV exhibit symptoms of HIV-Associated Neurocognitive disorder, a hallmark of which is the atrophy of white matter and the loss of myelin. We have previously demonstrated that select ARV drugs inhibit maturation and differentiation of oligodendrocytes, the cells responsible for myelination in the CNS. The proposed experiments aim to elucidate the role that stress granules play in the inhibition of maturation in ARV treated oligodendrocytes, so as to better understand the mechanisms of oligodendrocyte dysfunction as a prelude to designing therapies.

title: Cellular reprogramming to promote longevity, resilience, and repair in motor unit degeneration
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Individuals with age-related neurodegenerative disorders often experience declining neuromuscular function that may be linked to accelerated biological aging, but clearer knowledge of these degenerative processes is needed to guide the development of new, effective therapies. application_id: 10919186
activity_code: DP5
title: Innovative therapeutic approaches to treat chronic kidney disease
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Potassium deficiency increases rates of chronic kidney disease (CKD), an illness that has a worldwide prevalence of 10-15% and claims over 130,000 lives annually in the US. Total body control of potassium balance involves both the kidneys and skeletal muscle with potassium channels in both organs being key molecular regulators of this process. This study aims to understand mechanisms by which potassium deficiency affects kidney epithelial cells and macrophages in the progression of CKD and the role of potassium channels in this process; the overall goal of this project is to identify novel CKD treatment targets in both the kidneys and skeletal muscle.

application_id: 10914255
activity_code: K99
title: Developing advanced diffusion MRI for early detection of Alzheimer's disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and related dementia are a leading cause of death and disability in the aging population,
affecting approximately 6.2 million people aged 65 and older each year in the United States alone. The ability to
non-invasively detect the earliest changes in AD progression is a critical and unmet need that will help advance
the treatment time window for the most effective interventions. This project aims to develop a novel Magnetic
Resonance Imaging (MRI) approach to probe brain microstructural changes as an early marker of AD
progression, which will further provide new insights into AD pathogenesis and lead to timely intervention
strategies to prevent and/or slow down AD onset and/or progression.

application_id: 11164922
activity_code: F31
title: Define mechanisms by which host-microbe metabolite exchange regulates cellular and system iron homeostasis.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Iron deficiency and overload disorders are often difficult to treat and impact up to 2 billion individuals worldwide,
posing a significant global health challenge. This proposal aims to understand the role the gut microbiome has
on iron absorption and identify the mechanism by which microbes upregulate iron storage via ferritin.
Determining this mechanism will allow us to explore the gut microbiome as a new potential therapeutic avenue
for treating iron disorders.

application_id: 11242143
activity_code: F31
title: BMP Signaling Promotes Immune Cell Differentiation in Human Intestinal Organoids
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Dysregulation of the immune system in the intestine has been linked to many diseases, such as Inflammatory
Bowel Disease, Celiac Disease, and Necrotizing Enterocolitis. Most human model systems, including
organoids, are not primed to investigate these diseases as they lack the immune component necessary to
recapitulate the inflammation that drives the pathophysiologic changes in vivo. The goal of this proposal is to
interrogate the complex signaling environment that leads to early immune cell differentiation in the intestine,
using complex stem cell derived human intestinal organoids as a model that can ultimately be implemented to
interrogate inflammation based intestinal pathologies such as necrotizing enterocolitis or inflammatory bowel
diseases.

application_id: 11127440
activity_code: T32
title: Diabetes, Endocrinology and Metabolism Training Grant
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The purpose of this Training Grant is to prepare qualified postdoctoral trainees in Endocrinology, Diabetes and
Metabolism for scientific research careers that significantly impact on the health-related research needs of the
United States. This training program will utilize Stanford University’s world-class faculty and resources in the
fields of basic, translational and population health research in endocrinology with a primary focus on diabetes.
Trainees who graduate from this program will be well-prepared to independently lead (within an academic or
industrial setting), create and apply new scientific discoveries that enhance public health in areas of diabetes,
obesity, bone health and hormone-dependent cancer.

application_id: 11243053
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular Characterizations of the Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes in Hispanic/Latino Populations
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Type 2 diabetes (T2D) affects over 35 million adults in the United States, with Hispanic/Latino populations
experiencing disproportionate prevalence and complications. This study aims to advance understanding of T2D
in these populations by leveraging multi-omic data (transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics) from two
large and extensively phenotyped cohorts to identify key biomarkers associated with T2D risk and pathogenesis.
By uncovering causal molecular pathways and biomarkers, this research will provide a critical foundation for
discovering novel insights to advance precision medicine and therapeutic interventions.

activity_code: K99
title: Identifying the mechanistic role of and reversing aberrant neural activity in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Alzheimer’s Disease is a devastating disease with enormous unmet medical need and few treatments, and it is
likely necessary to understand, detect, and treat Alzheimer’s Disease earlier in disease development. Patients
often exhibit aberrant neural activity very early, even before Alzheimer’s Disease pathology or cognitive decline,
and aberrant neural activity may be a primary driver of and tractable therapeutic target for Alzheimer’s Disease.
This project has discovered a neural stimulation approach that fully restores learning and memory function in an
Alzheimer’s mouse model, and will use cutting-edge systems neuroscience techniques to conduct single-cell
resolution examination and perturbation of the brain to determine the mechanistic role of aberrant neural activity
...

title: GPCR-dependent microglial function in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative GPR56, an adhesion G-protein coupled receptor, was recently identified as upregulated in microglia in individuals with early-stage Alzheimer's disease (AD) compared to those with no pathology or late-stage AD, implying its potential function in the disease. In this proposal, I hypothesize that microglial GPR56 limits AD pathological progression in both AD mouse models and human patients. I will use cutting-edge techniques, including single-cell sequencing, mouse genetics, live imaging, and human brain tissues, to reveal the role of microglial GPR56 in AD pathology, shedding light on a novel therapeutic target for AD.

administration, providing an innovative and novel data collection approach for monitoring participant
performance in research studies and clinical trials in adults with DS that will directly benefit projects funded

application_id: 11143954
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the link between propionate metabolism and aging
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mounting evidence support the idea that aging is rooted in metabolic dysfunction, which occurs when chemical
reactions become dysregulated during aging and negatively alters the body's processing and distribution of
nutrients, inducing the aberrant accumulation of certain metabolites. Identifying metabolic pathways and
metabolites that drive cell senescence and metabolic dysfunction is critical to understanding and treating age-
related diseases. This project aims to unravel the association between propionate metabolism and aging,
providing insights into potential diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets for mitigating aging and age-
related diseases.

application_id: 11143766
activity_code: K99
title: Adaptation of PATH-Pain for Dementia Family Caregivers Living with Persistent Pain and Negative Emotions
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Many family caregivers to people with dementia have persistent musculoskeletal pain, which exacerbates their
emotional and psychological stress and makes care tasks more difficult to perform. This study aims to adapt an
existing evidence-based pain management intervention, Problem Adaptation Therapy for Pain (PATH-Pain), for
use by dementia family caregivers who have co-existing persistent pain and high negative emotions. The
adapted intervention will be the first evidence-based pain management program customized for dementia
family caregivers and will offer a sustainable and scalable approach to person-centered pain management for
the growing number of dementia family caregivers who are managing their own physical and mental health
comorbidities.

application_id: 11144984
activity_code: K99
title: Establishing reactive astrocyte memory as a risk factor for aging-related neurodegenerative disease
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Viral and bacterial infections result in transient neuroinflammation and are a common occurrence throughout life,
yet how or why temporary inflammation increases the risk of developing neurodegenerative disease (ND) remains
elusive. Similarly, despite the key role of astrocytes in neuron function and modulating the immune response, they
are often overlooked in the study of ND. The proposed research will address these outstanding questions and
provide fundamental insight into how transient astrocyte reactivity affects future insults and how this impacts on
ND.

application_id: 10865920
activity_code: K99
title: Adaptation of PATH-Pain for Dementia Family Caregivers Living with Persistent Pain and Negative Emotions
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Many family caregivers to people with dementia have persistent musculoskeletal pain, which exacerbates their emotional and psychological stress and makes care tasks more difficult to perform. This study aims to adapt an existing evidence-based pain management intervention, Problem Adaptation Therapy for Pain (PATH-Pain), for use by dementia family caregivers who have co-existing persistent pain and high negative emotions. The adapted intervention will be the first evidence-based pain management program customized for dementia family caregivers and will offer a sustainable and scalable approach to person-centered pain management for the growing number of dementia family caregivers who are managing their own physical and mental health comorbidities.

application_id: 11240193
activity_code: F31
title: Visual Stimuli for Selective Modulation of Neural Pathways in the Retina
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The primate retina’s output is split into several retinal ganglion cell (RGC) types whose differential contributions
to downstream visual processing and behavior remain poorly understood due to a lack of validated RGC type
selective stimuli. The current proposal will provide the first direct population tests of existing stimuli that rely on
flawed older models of RGC encoding, refine newer mechanistic and deep neural network models of RGC
encoding, and leverage newer models to produce ethologically relevant RGC type selective stimuli. Completion
of this work will provide validated noninvasive tools for probing the contributions of different RGC types to the
visual system and aid the development of diagnostic and prognostic tools for RGC type selective diseases.

application_id: 11369508
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating TFEB as a critical node to improve proteostatic maintenance and skeletal muscle function with age
org_name: OKLAHOMA MEDICAL RESEARCH FOUNDATION
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Millions of aged individuals suffer from loss of skeletal muscle mass and function because of
sarcopenia and disuse atrophy. Despite the impact sarcopenia and disuse atrophy have on subsequent quality
of life and healthspan, no pharmacological therapies are currently available because the underlying
mechanisms that trigger muscle dysfunction are not fully understood. This proposal will explore the novel
concept that TFEB functions as a critical node to improve maintenance and recovery of skeletal muscle
function in two conditions of muscle loss, sarcopenia and disuse atrophy.

application_id: 10887330
activity_code: K99
title: Fatty acid remodeling of the translatome during fasting and aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: From ancient to modern times fasting has been recognized as an effective way to improve health, slow down aging, and reduce the risk of many aging-related pathologies, such as insulin resistance; yet it is unclear how fasting establishes those benefits at molecular levels. My data provide evidence for the existence of a novel gene expression network that regulates metabolic reprogramming upon fasting; however, this pathway is blunted during aging, and disruption of this pathway may lead to aging related insulin resistance. Characterizing this novel pathway will lead to the identification of new therapeutic targets for aging related insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes.

title: Upstream drivers of healthy aging: Assessing the role of governmental spending priorities in early life on midlife cognition and later-life dementia risk
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Experiences in childhood and adolescence (e.g., differential exposure to childhood adversity) contribute to risk for Alzheimer’s Disease and Alzheimer’s Disease Related Dementias (AD/ADRD). Population distributions of these early life experiences, however, are themselves shaped by upstream factors that remain underexplored in the AD/ADRD literature. The proposed research will be the first to examine the implications of federal, state, and local governmental spending priorities – as intervenable exposures that condition early life environments –
on population distributions of midlife cognition and later life dementia risk, providing important information to support government officials in prioritizing investments that facilitate and sustain lifecourse health.

title: Determining the Postfertilization Regulatory Functions of a Sperm microRNA Cluster
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:

of the paternal genome; however, research in the past decade has revealed that sperm

transfer of paternal non-genetically inherited phenotypes to progeny. The Fx-miRs are a cluster
of miRNAs whose expression in sperm is highly conserved and who influence early
development. By using preimplantation embryos and mouse embryonic stem cells as an in vitro
model of early developmental cells, I will determine the capacity of the Fx-miRs to regulate
targets through translation, as well as the direct targets of these miRNAs and the downstream
effects of those binding events to reveal the first m...

application_id: 11162218
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Role of GSK3A in Cohesin Dynamics and Gene Regulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The cohesin complex plays an evolutionarily conserved role in spatially organizing the genome and regulating
gene expression during interphase. Mutations in cohesin subunits or regulators cause a group of developmental
disorders termed cohesinopathies; however, there are no targeted therapies for cohesinopathies due to the lack
of characterized cohesin regulators during interphase. Elucidating the mechanisms by which the recently
identified cohesin regulator GSK3A modulates cohesin activity is crucial for exploring its potential as a
therapeutic target for cohesinopathies.

application_id: 11163972
activity_code: F31
title: Deciphering the Dynamic Coordination of Chromatin Accessibility and Gene Expression to Uncover the Mechanisms of Cellular Differentiation
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
The core questions of developmental and regenerative biology are: (1) What sequence of changes to cell state
describe a cell's progression from a multipotent stem cell to each terminal fate, and (2) what factors and
mechanisms control these changes? This proposal outlines new algorithms that use single-cell paired
chromatin accessibility and gene expression data to model cell state dynamics, identify key regulatory factors,
and compare dynamics across modalities giving insight into these key questions. This work will enhance our
understanding of development, regeneration, and reprogramming, and improve our ability to control cell fate to
avoid disease and replace damaged cell types or tissues.

title: Studying Neuronal Proteostasis During Aging and AD with Novel Protein Tagging Tools
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Dysregulated proteostasis, particularly protein turnover, is a hallmark of brain aging and Alzheimer’s Disease
(AD), but many details remain to be understood about protein turnover due to previous technical limitations. In
this proposal, leveraging new protein-tagging methodologies, l aim to (1) identify aberrations in neuronal
protein turnover dynamics and their functional consequences during aging and AD and (2) understand a
neuron-intrinsic regulator of protein turnover (autophagy/lysosome) and a neuron-extrinsic regulator of
neuronal protein turnover (microglia) during aging and AD. The findings from my studies will substantially
increase our basic biological understanding of neuronal proteostasis/protein turnover during brain aging and
AD, and ultimately identify new...

activity_code: K99
title: Quantification of microstructures in the entorhinal-hippocampus pathway as a sensitive biomarker for neurodegeneration during the preclinical stage of Alzheimer's disease
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Once cognitive impairment occurs in an individual with Alzheimer’s disease, it is difficult to modify the devastating
disease process. The long-term goal of this project is to develop a non-invasive tool that can visualize and
quantify microstructural brain changes during the preclinical stage of Alzheimer’s disease. To this end, Dr. Yuto
Uchida will conduct a proof-of-concept study to investigate the feasibility of ultra-high-field quantitative magnetic
resonance imaging as a sensitive biomarker for neurodegeneration in preclinical Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 10920459
activity_code: T32
title: Health Services and Outcomes Research for Aging Populations
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Our population is aging, which raises many important questions regarding how to deliver optimal care for the growing number of older adults who are frail or have complex health needs such as multiple co-existing conditions or loss of independence. We propose an innovative, interdisciplinary training program to produce the next generation of researchers with robust training in person and family-centered health services and outcomes research, all applied to geriatrics and gerontology. Our program will provide training in the methods to understand how health services, delivery of health care, and financing systems might optimally be designed to serve the needs of older adults.

application_id: 11234326
activity_code: K99
title: T cell surveillance of senescent cells under homeostasis and aging
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Age-associated changes in T cells compromise their functionality and render older adults vulnerable to infections
and age-related diseases, yet it remains unclear how T cells and their age-related dysfunction contribute to
senescent cell accumulation. The overarching goal of this project is to 1) investigate how senescent cells
stimulate T cell-mediated surveillance under physiological conditions, and 2) explore how T cell aging facilitates
senescent cell immune evasion resulting in pathological senescent cell accumulation with human aging and
disease. Discoveries from these studies will contribute to the development of immunotherapies for the elimination
of pathological senescent cells, thereby supporting healthy aging.

application_id: 11083774
activity_code: K99
title: State of the ART: Functional genomics of RNA-binding proteins and their role in artemisinin drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The artemisinin family of drugs are the most effective frontline class of drugs used to treat infections with
Plasmodium falciparum, the deadliest malaria-causing parasite in humans, yet the emergence of artemisinin
resistance threatens disease eradication efforts. Our understanding of the molecular mechanisms implicated in
artemisinin resistance remains incomplete. This project investigates how RNA-binding proteins, important post-
transcriptional regulators of gene expression, support artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum.

application_id: 11238787
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying the genetic causes of stillbirth using C. elegans as an in vivo platform
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative:
Stillbirth, defined as fetal death in utero after 20 weeks of gestation, occurs at a rate of 5.73 per 1000 live
births in the United States, and is devastating for families touched by it. There are currently no reliable
prenatal genetic tests for identifying alleles that cause stillbirth because in silico analyses must rely on small
fetal exome datasets and thus do not have the power to provide more than the predicted deleteriousness of
any given variant. Here, I will employ the genetic power of Caenorhabditis elegans to develop an in vivo
variant-to-function (V2F) platform capable of validating alleles from fetal exomes for their deleteriousness,
thereby opening the door to a new realm of prenatal diagnostics.

activity_code: F31
title: MicroRNA-290 regulates maternal-fetal nutrient exchange during neural tube closure.
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Maternal hyperglycemia increases the risks of neural tube defects and neurodevelopmental disorders. Early in
pregnancy, the yolk sac and, later, the placenta supplies the growing embryo with nutrients. This proposal aims
to investigate the role of miR-290, a microRNA highly expressed in the visceral yolk sac, in protecting the
embryo against metabolic dysregulation during neural tube closure.

application_id: 11242666
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating mechanisms controlling tissue-level coordination of Planar Cell Polarity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Over the course of development, the Planar Cell Polarity (PCP) pathway coordinates cell behaviors across a
tissue or body axis to direct collective cell movements required for neural tube closure and axis elongation.
Currently, the mechanisms that align PCP activity with a body axis remain unknown. Because hair follicle
orientation is controlled by the PCP pathway, we can utilize natural genetic variants that decouple hair follicle
orientation from the body axis to understand how PCP activity is aligned with a tissue axis, revealing a
fundamental principle required for embryonic development.

title: Investigating the molecular basis of context-dependent STAT3 function in NK cells
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Natural Killer (NK) cells are crucial for antiviral defense and require stimulation via cytokines through STAT transcription factors to exert their function. Multiple cytokines activate STAT3 in NK cells which is involved in immune disorders and is regularly mutated in malignancies including NK cell derived lymphoma. The goal of this study is to dissect how STAT3 influences NK cell function in a new model of viral infection that shows a context-dependent role of STAT3 in NK cells.

application_id: 11116856
activity_code: K99
title: Regulatory T cell coordination of the mucosal NK cell response during viral infection
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
We continue to lack vaccines for many pathogens of public health importance, including herpes simplex virus-2
(HSV-2), human immunodeficiency virus-1 (HIV-1), and tuberculosis (TB). In many infections of public health
importance, the mucosal barrier is the portal of pathogen exposure, yet the regulation of mucosal immunity is
incompletely understood. Completion of this proposal will provide insight into regulation of the anti-viral mucosal
immune response and how pathogen clearance can be achieved while preserving barrier tissue integrity.

application_id: 11405327
activity_code: K01
title: Effectively Linking Molecular Informatics and Network Analytics to Reduce Malaria (ELIMINAR-Malaria)
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative
Malaria kills over 600,000 people every year, and causes hundreds of millions of additional infections that
impose significant financial burdens on the individuals infected, their families, their communities, and the health
system. The Amazon region aims to eliminate the disease by 2030, but there are important gaps in our
understanding of malaria epidemiology that will prevent successful elimination. The objective of this proposal is
to address one of those gaps: how human mobility contributes to malaria population structure, importation, and
diffusion, and do so by leveraging data fusion to effectively link efficient, cost-effective survey and genotyping
methods.

application_id: 10919174
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the role of Cdk5 and p35 in natural killer cell cytotoxicity
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Natural killer (NK) cells are an important part of the immune system that protects the body by recognizing and killing virally-infected cells and cancer cells, and they carry great potential for use as cancer treatments. Unfortunately, existing NK cell therapies have been unable to efficiently kill tumor cells in cancer patients, who themselves also tend to have poorly functioning NK cells for unclear reasons. This project aims to better understand the mechanisms behind NK cell anti-tumor functions, with the ultimate goal of developing new, enhanced NK cell cancer therapies.

application_id: 11244249
activity_code: F31
title: Neuro-Cognitive Trajectories of Language and Executive Functioning Co-Developmentin Early Childhood
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Language and executive functioning (EF) skills are critically important building blocks for children’s developing
cognitive, social, emotional, and academic abilities. In this novel study, I will examine behavioral and
neurobiological developmental trajectories of language and EF skills and how individual variation in children’s
home language environment shapes these trajectories longitudinally across early childhood. Findings will help
provide critical insight into language-EF co-development to better inform targeted and maximally effective early
interventions to support children’s success and well-being.
