application_id: 11123794
activity_code: DP5
title: Predicting and controlling polygenic health traits using probabilistic models and evolution-inspired gene editing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Predicting and controlling polygenic health traits using probabilistic models and evolution-inspired gene
editing
PROJECT NARRATIVE:
DNA is passed down with multiple errors per generation, some mutations being beneficial, but the majority being
neutral or causing health disorders. We can now correct these with CRISPR/Cas9, but the models that predict
the consequences of such gene interventions are still not robust. I will conduct large-scale experiments with the
plant A. thaliana to investigate the predictability of fitness impacts of DNA errors in many environmental
conditions, and safely trial new gene editing approaches to reduce the number of mutations arising per
generation and shift their beneficial-to-deleterious ratio.

application_id: 11132903
activity_code: K99
title: Functions of Rapidly-Evolving Proteins and Their Roles in Pathogenicity
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Leveraging the recent breakthrough in protein structure prediction and progress in the analysis of
protein sequences and structures using Artificial Intelligence, I will study the rapidly evolving and
pathogenicity-associated proteins encoded by the pan-genomes of Vibrio species that cause
vibriosis in humans. This project will establish a framework to combine computational tool
development and large-scale data analysis with experimental validation to decipher protein
functions, contributing to our understanding of the pathogenicity of bacteria and human diseases.

application_id: 11318327
activity_code: F31
title: The Shapes of Family Conflict: Differentiating Adaptive and Maladaptive Parent-Child Conflict in Daily Life
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Parent-child conflict is a common part of daily family life, yet a more precise understanding is needed of the
core features—such as intensity, frequency, duration, escalation, and resolution—that differentiate maladaptive
conflicts, which contribute to adverse outcomes, from adaptive ones, which support communication and
growth. This training grant will address this gap by leveraging real-time smartphone audio recordings and
daily surveys to capture the natural progression of parent-child conflicts, yielding both moment-to-moment
insights and broader family-level patterns. By clarifying how conflict dynamics shape child and family well-
being, this research will establish a foundational evidence base to identify specific conflict behaviors to target,
enabling more precis...

application_id: 11247985
activity_code: K99
title: Structural and Functional Alteration of host RBCs by Babesia
org_name: NEW YORK BLOOD CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Babesia is an emerging zoonotic tick-borne parasite, with a fast-expanding geographical range of human
infections globally, and a heavy prevalence in northeastern USA. Little is known about how the parasite remodels
its host red blood cell (RBC) during infection, although clinical complications of the disease are linked to these
changes. The current study aims to understand the nature and functionality of parasite proteins exported to the
RBCs and detail their interactions with the RBC cytoskeleton and examine how this impacts RBC morphology
and stability, all of which ultimately affect the pathological outcomes of the disease.

application_id: 11110361
activity_code: K99
title: Molecular understanding of maternal humoral responses to pregnancy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Complications from preterm birth are the leading causes of global mortality in children under the age of five, yet
currently there are no predictive diagnostics or preventative therapies for preterm pregnancies. Premature
inflammation initiates the biological cascade that results in preterm labor and this proposal investigates the
contribution of maternal antibodies to this process, identified using a new robust approach. In this proposal, I
will chronicle maternal antibodies through the progression of pregnancy for non-invasive diagnostics
development and dissect the molecular pathways that may serve as therapeutic entry points to diminish labor-
promoting inflammation.

application_id: 10783554
activity_code: K99
title: Functional genomics approaches to decode the roles of transcription factor isoforms
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Transcription factors are proteins which play key roles in a variety of biological processes and many human diseases, including cancer. Most transcription factors are expressed not as a single protein, but rather as a series of distinct protein “isoforms”, yet the vast majority of transcription factor isoforms—thousands of proteins—remain entirely uncharacterized. The proposed project will develop approaches to probe the roles of thousands of transcription factor isoforms at once in breast cancer cells, which will uncover novel disease- relevant biology and lay the foundation for studying isoforms more broadly, genome-wide and in other cellular contexts.

title: Mutation-Specific Calmodulin Kinase II Activation in Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: Project Narrative:
Ca2+/Calmodulin kinase IIδ (CaMKIIδ) is a key regulator of electromechanical and calcium homeostasis in
cardiac myocytes, and dysregulation in the form of overactivation is a mutation-specific driver of pathogenesis
in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. This proposal focuses on deciphering the myofilament-based, mutation-specific
mechanisms by which this dysregulation occurs and establishing small molecule CaMKIIδ inhibition as a
therapeutic option for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy patients. We propose a variety of in vivo experiments to
investigate CaMKIIδ activation and activity, calcium handling, and small molecule CaMKIIδ inhibition in
hypertrophic cardiomyopathy mouse models.

The skin is our body’s first line of defense from external stressors, such as pathogens or physical injuries. Under

application_id: 10948604
activity_code: K99
title: Cellular and molecular mechanisms driving regeneration and fibrosis of the tendon-bone attachment
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Injuries to the tendon-bone attachment, or enthesis, are frequent amongst the general population and existing treatments are limited with variable efficacy, leaving patients with high re-rupture rates, impacted quality of life, and increased likelihood of developing joint degenerative diseases. Unlike the mammalian enthesis which scars after injury, the zebrafish tendon-bone attachment can fully regenerate through mechanisms yet to be discovered. By employing cross-species comparative genetics and genomics approaches in zebrafish and mice after injury, this proposal will elucidate divergent signaling mechanisms driving successful enthesis regeneration versus fibrosis which may be leveraged to inspire new treatment strategies in the clinic.

phr: he overall goals of the Center’s training, capacity building, and education program are to: 1) support training and professional development of community-partnered reproductive and perinatal health researchers; and 2) build and provide high quality training for a community-informed clinical care and public health practice workforce focused on reproductive and perinatal health and reduction of maternal morbidity and mortality. The training program will serve as a backbone of the EMBRACE Center’s thematic focus to improve maternal health and well-being of individuals at risk for maternal morbidity and mortality.

application_id: 11245151
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding the Efficacy of T-TEER in Models of Primary Tricuspid Regurgitation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Primary tricuspid regurgitation is a disease in which damage to the valve leads to leakage of blood into the right
atrium. Because open heart surgery is risky, novel minimally invasive transcatheter technologies have been
developed to treat tricuspid valve leakage. However, little is known about the applicability of these technologies
to primary tricuspid regurgitation, a knowledge gap I aim to overcome with this proposed research.

activity_code: K38
title: Investigating the Genotype-Phenotype Relationships that Underlie Congenital Disorders with Cardiovascular Symptoms through Population-scale Analyses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Clinical genetic testing is becoming increasingly common, particularly among patients with cardiovascular disease. Much of our understanding of genetic disorders was obtained prior to the wide use of genetic testing in clinical practice, creating a biased view of the symptoms and outcomes that occur among patients that carry disease-associated mutations. This project seeks to use computational modeling and large electronic health record datasets to learn more detailed information about the consequences of rare mutations in genes linked to congenital disorders with severe cardiovascular symptoms.

application_id: 10783007
activity_code: K01
title: Elucidating the therapeutic utility of targeting metabolic dependencies in osteosarcoma
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Metastatic osteosarcoma (OS) in humans and dogs remains a therapeutic challenge, compounded by a low mutational landscape and absence of clear molecular drivers that limit the utility of immune checkpoint blockade and classic kinase pathway inhibition. Data suggest that OS may be particularly vulnerable to targeting of metabolic pathways; indeed, I have shown that loss of monocarboxylate transporter (MCT) 1 and 4 function in OS cells leads to intracellular lactate accumulation, altering tumor cell phenotype and impairing aerobic glycolysis. Building upon these data, I will employ a comparative cross-species approach to define the regulation and function of MCT1/4 in OS, assess the therapeutic potential of MCT inhibition using zebrafish and mouse models of OS, and explore...

application_id: 11114050
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the Role of Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts in Immunodetection of Mycosis Fungoides
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Some patients with mycosis fungoides (MF), a skin cancer, will progress to late-stage disease with involvement
beyond the skin and a higher mortality risk. This study will investigate the subtypes and the location of cancer-
associated fibroblasts (CAFs) responsible for immune evasion of MF tumor cells in the skin of diverse patient
populations. Results obtained here will provide insights into the role of the tumor microenvironment in the
progression of MF and identify potential targets for more direct personalized therapies to improve overall
survival.

application_id: 10886986
activity_code: K99
title: Nutrition-based interventions to ameliorate pain in sickle cell disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Globally, Sickle Cell Disease (SCD) is prevalent in resource-limited settings and plagued by malnutrition including for individuals living in the United States. Our goal is to identify the multigenerational effects of diet and cannabinoid exposure in the initiation and progression of pain in SCD using humanized transgenic sickle mice which show the pathobiology and features of pain similar to persons with SCD. High-throughput data and mechanisms examined herein will pave the way for clinical studies of integrative approaches to improve pain related outcomes in SCD.

application_id: 10873703
activity_code: K99
title: Targeting proteoglycan-mediated signaling in Ewing sarcoma
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
The cell of origin for Ewing sarcoma and the mechanisms of tumor initiation and progression are a matter of
constant debate. My project takes advantages of an innovative zebrafish genetic model of Ewing sarcoma to
gain new insights into understanding the developmental origin of that cancer, and to investigate the role of
proteoglycan-mediated signaling in cancer and cancer niche cells in tumor initiation and progression. In this work
I will test a new strategy targeting the enzymes involved in heparan sulfate proteoglycan metabolism for Ewing
sarcoma treatment.

application_id: 11163576
activity_code: F31
title: Role of cell-specific durotaxis in pulmonary arterial hypertension pathogenesis
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative
Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is a rare but fatal disorder of lung characterized by extensive pulmonary
arterial remodeling. This study aims to understand the role of the stiffness gradient of the extracellular matrix in
the migration of pulmonary arterial cells, particularly smooth muscle cells, in PAH. By using novel transgenic
mice (CRISPR KI FAK L994E) and novel microfluidic chip models, the research aims to investigate how changes
in matrix stiffness drive cell migration and the involvement of specific proteins, such as FAK or Paxillin or
microtubules, associated with creating molecular clutch that initiates cellular durotaxis in PAH.

application_id: 11067829
activity_code: K08
title: Intelligent wearable system for preventing medication errors in anesthesiology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
Medication errors or adverse events are a major public health problem causing thousands of deaths annually.
This proposal aims to build wearable cameras with real-time computer vision algorithms to identify medications
when initially selected by an anesthesiology provider during drug preparation or before injection to potentially
prevent accidentally giving the wrong drug. Drug dose will also be determined and recorded in an electronic
health record, potentially increasing record accuracy and decreasing manual tasks for anesthesiologists so
they can focus on direct patient care.

title: The context-dependent role of Caveolin-1 as a driver of cellular adaptation in Ewing Sarcoma
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
While the scaffolding protein Caveolin-1 has been implicated in disease progression in cancer, its
precise role remains controversial. This proposal aims to leverage innovations in imaging,

application_id: 11242515
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding how appropriate apoptotic cell degradation ensures lung homeostasis
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
Efferocytosis, the phagocytic engulfment of apoptotic cells, maintains tissue homeostasis, yet improper handling
of apoptotic cells post-engulfment is a previously underappreciated contributor to the pathogenesis of a panoply
of diseases. This proposal will reveal the cellular mechanisms that underlie how macrophages, the body’s
professional phagocyte, handle apoptotic material in both healthy and disease contexts. Understanding apoptotic
cell digestion is especially important as the insights obtained will provide mechanistic evidence for the etiology
of inflammatory diseases of the lung.

application_id: 11243909
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the Role of Transcription Factor Snail1 in Astyanax mexicanus Heart Regeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: (8) PROJECT NARRATIVE
Comparisons between cave and surface populations of Astyanax mexicanus have provided great avenues for
researching the rapid evolutionary divergence of many traits, including metabolic responses to differing food
and oxygen availability as well as the loss of heart regeneration abilities. This project explores the link between
their metabolic and regenerative differences through the regulation of the epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition
(EMT) transcription factor, Snail, which was found to be upregulated in the non-regenerative cavefish. In this
study, I will be assaying A. mexicanus’ metabolism, snail gene regulation, and EMT responses to ventricular
amputation to identify the underlying cause of retention or loss of heart regeneration abilities.

application_id: 11083579
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of Photoreceptor Contribution to Retinal Inflammation in Diabetic Retinopathy
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Diabetic retinopathy blinds approximately 12% of diabetic patients each year and its incidence is expected to
increase. While there are currently no therapeutic strategies directed at its early stages, there is accumulating
evidence that retinal photoreceptors are involved in the pathogenesis of diabetic retinopathy and are major
contributors to diabetes-induced local inflammation in the retina. This project aims to study the responses of
photoreceptors to hyperglycemia and interventions that address this pathway with the potential to be effective
in the early stages of diabetic retinopathy to prevent the development of vision-threatening pathology.

application_id: 11012334
activity_code: K12
title: Neurosurgeon Research Career Development Program (NRCDP)
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The goal of the NRCDP is to recruit, identify, and supporting promising neurosurgeons at the beginning of their careers, when they are most vulnerable, so that they can successfully transition into independent neurosurgeon scientists. The first 10 years of the program were tremendously successful. During the next five years, we aim to build on this success by fostering greater diversity, equity, and inclusion at all program levels, creating a “Pipeline Program” for recruiting the most talented and diverse group of K12 applicants, expanding the “Training Program” to accelerate timing to first independent NIH award, and establishing an NRCDP leadership structure that embraces continuous renewal and evolution.

application_id: 10932295
activity_code: K99
title: Role of BCL11B in lineage ambiguous leukemia
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute leukemias of ambiguous lineage pose significant clinical challenges due to their unusual “lineage-
ambiguous” phenotype that complicates choice of therapy and obscures our understanding of the cellular origins
and molecular drivers of this disease. This proposal will build upon my recent discovery that aberrant BCL11B
activation is a unique and specific genomic driver of lineage ambiguous leukemia. In this proposal, I will identify
how aberrant BCL11B activity disrupts gene regulatory programs of different hematopoietic stem and progenitor
cells to alter self-renewal capability and confer a lineage ambiguous phenotype.

title: Mechanisms of CDH1-inactivated advanced gastric adenocarcinoma tumorigenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Advanced gastric adenocarcinoma (GAC) presents significant treatment challenges due to peritoneal metastases, necessitating effective patient stratification for targeted therapies. application_id: 10782795
activity_code: K01
title: Neurogenetic contributions to the spread of tau pathology in sporadic early-onset Alzheimer's disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Early-onset Alzheimer’s disease (EOAD) is a critically understudied form of AD compared with its late-onset counterpart, with little evidence regarding the mechanisms underlying disease progression. By combining longitudinal multimodal in vivo neuroimaging acquired in patients, graph theory, and brain transcriptome analysis, the current project will characterize the contributions of brain network connectivity and specific genetic factors to the spread of tau pathology. Results will help elucidate drivers of the pathological cascade in EOAD and contribute to the development of patient-specific approaches to monitor disease progression.

title: Cancer Clinical Epidemiology Training Grant
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA

There is a national shortage of qualified clinician-scientists able to conduct the rigorous clinical research
needed to optimize cancer prevention and treatment. The Cancer Clinical Epidemiology Training Program

application_id: 11245630
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular basis of mitochondrial calcium extrusion - a key target in ischemic heart disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mitochondria have multiple protein-driven pathways to uptake and release calcium ions, which helps regulate
overall cellular calcium levels and functions as an important signal for tuning cell function. These pathways are
disrupted during heart disease, contributing to injury at the cellular level, but the mechanisms underlying these
pathways are poorly understood. This study investigates novel protein agents of the cell responsible for releasing
mitochondrial calcium in response to sodium, which will improve our mechanistic understanding of these
pathways and help guide the development of new drugs and therapeutics.

title: Mechanisms of CDH1-inactivated advanced gastric adenocarcinoma tumorigenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Advanced gastric adenocarcinoma (GAC) presents significant treatment challenges due to peritoneal
metastases, necessitating effective patient stratification for targeted therapies. Our study identified two advanced
GAC subtypes: Type I and Type II, each with unique molecular characteristics, including CDH1 inactivation in
Type I. Understanding the mechanisms, particularly CDH1 loss, is crucial for developing novel therapeutics
based on subtypes of GACs.

activity_code: F31
title: Defining the role of Fzd6 in lymphatic network development and function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Discovering novel genetic causes of lymphatic system disease will inform treatment decisions in patients. The
genetic cause for most cases of central conducting lymphatic anomaly remains a mystery. The proposed study
will investigate FZD6 as a candidate gene that causes lymphatic system disease by assessing lymphatic
network morphology, function, and cell behavior in a loss-of-function mammalian model system.

activity_code: F31
title: CFTR gene correction via targeted exon replacement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to develop a gene editing therapy that could offer a cure for cystic fibrosis (CF), a chronic
and life-threatening disease affecting >120,000 individuals worldwide. If successful, I will develop a gene editing
modality that will offer a potential cure for 20% of cystic fibrosis patients who do not have any therapeutic options,
ultimately reducing healthcare costs and the burden of disease on affected individuals and families.

application_id: 11244524
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering the Roles of PIP5KDuring Cell Migration
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 Cardiovascular diseases and pulmonary fibrosis collectively affect millions of individuals worldwide and
remain the leading causes of mortality. This project employs advanced genetic, biophysical, biochemical, and
imaging techniques to investigate how PIP5K regulates cell migration and polarity through its interactions with
Ras and Arf GTPases. By uncovering these molecular and biophysical mechanisms, this research aims to inform
the development of innovative therapies to reduce disease burden and improve health outcomes.

application_id: 10783391
activity_code: F32
title: A Comparative Analysis of OPC and Microglia Phagocytosis and its Relevance to Neurodegenerative Disease
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The overarching goal of this proposal is to gain a deeper understanding of OPC phagocytosis in both health and disease and determine how phagocytosis alters their differentiation capacity. These results will have implications for neurodegenerative disease as lack of OPC differentiation is observed in multiple neurodegenerative states. This data will lay critical groundwork for future investigation into the diverse functions of OPCs in disease and will provide novel insight into new mechanisms that may alter OPC differentiation.

application_id: 11213166
activity_code: K38
title: The Role of Rheology and Transfusion on Microvascular Thrombosis in Sepsis
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sepsis affects nearly 50 million people worldwide each year with mortality rates estimated between 20% and 30%. While the syndrome is defined by a dysregulated inflammatory response to a microbial infection, the exact mechanisms which lead to the organ failure associated morbidity and mortality remain incompletely characterized. The proposed work will test the hypothesis that rheologic disturbances in the microvasculature, driven by increased in red blood cell aggregation and decreased cell membrane deformability, contribute to endothelial injury, micro-thrombisis and subsequent organ failure and that these changes can be influenced by blood product transfusion.

application_id: 10865828
activity_code: K99
title: Defining novel mechanisms of microbiota in regulating anti-tumor responses
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cancer is the second leading mortality in the world, while many different treatments exist including revolutionary immunotherapies, adverse events are frequent and response rates are limited. Basic, translational, and clinical studies defined a key role for the gut microbiota in affecting tumor progression and immunotherapy efficacy, but the underlying mechanism remains at early stage of investigation. The focus of this proposal is to delineate two novel pathways by which microbiota interacts with mammalian hosts to impact tumor growth, progression, or immunotherapy response, thus provoking novel opportunities for intervention.

application_id: 10794822
activity_code: K99
title: Quantitatively predicting drug-resistant mutations to improve precision oncology
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE My long-term goal is to build quantitative models that predict and circumvent cancer therapy resistance, specifically focusing on protein kinases currently targeted by over 75 FDA-approved inhibitors. Unfortunately, multiple kinase mutations have been found to resist known inhibitors and drastically reduce patient survival rates, and it remains unclear why these mutations induce such dramatic drug-resistance. Through a combination of high-throughput experiments, physics-based simulation, and machine learning based data-science methods, I will identify the mechanistic impact of clinically observed kinase mutants and build models that predict if a drug- resistant mutant is newly sensitive to alternative clinical therapeutics.

title: HopkinsPREP: Research, Community, Professional Training and Personal Growth
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY

title: An Automated Multimodal, Multiscale Algorithm for Cervical Cancer Screening in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cervical cancer is a life-threatening disease that is associated with microscopic and macroscopic changes in the
cervix, such as the development of lesions, and disproportionately affects low- and middle- income countries. A
major barrier in implementing and sustaining screening programs for cervical cancer in low-resource countries
is the lack of trained clinicians. This proposal will develop and evaluate a novel multimodal, multiscale deep
learning algorithm workflow that integrates microscopic and macroscopic features from cervical images to
automatically arrive at a diagnostic result, which could improve access to cervical cancer screening and
significantly decrease mortality due to the disease.

activity_code: K99
title: Paving the way for bystander T cell immunotherapies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE T cells with specificity for tumor antigens are the primary target of most immunotherapies; however, T cells specific for tumor-unrelated antigens, termed “bystanders,” are often found in human solid tumors. Despite being a major component of the tumor infiltrate, it is unknown if bystander T cells can be broadly activated to elicit antitumor functions. This project seeks to define the mechanisms that permit or restrict the functions of tumor-infiltrating bystander T cells so that this population can be harnessed as a therapeutic agent for cancers.

application_id: 11263611
activity_code: K99
title: Human leukocyte antigen and immune response in non-small cell lung cancer: A multi-omics approach
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: Project Narrative
While immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have transformed cancer therapy, durable response
has been observed in only 20-30% of patients with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC),
highlighting the urgent and unmet need for novel accurate biomarkers. Human leukocyte antigen
(HLA) genes are key to antigen presentation, with their inherited and acquired polymorphisms
markedly influencing the diversity of peptide repertoire and individual immune response. The
overarching goal of this proposal is to elucidate the role of germline and somatic HLA variations
in cellular immunity and the efficacy of ICI monotherapy in patients with NSCLC, employing novel
statistical and machine learning methods to analyze multi-omic and clinical data.

application_id: 11035860
activity_code: K99
title: Human leukocyte antigen and immune response in non-small cell lung cancer: A multi-omics approach
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: Project Narrative While immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have transformed cancer therapy, durable response has been observed in only 20-30% of patients with non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), highlighting the urgent and unmet need for novel accurate biomarkers. Human leukocyte antigen (HLA) genes are key to antigen presentation, with their inherited and acquired polymorphisms markedly influencing the diversity of peptide repertoire and individual immune response. The overarching goal of this proposal is to elucidate the role of germline and somatic HLA variations in cellular immunity and the efficacy of ICI monotherapy in patients with NSCLC, employing novel statistical and machine learning methods to analyze multi-omic and clinical data.

title: Advancing understanding of perinatal binge eating attitudes and behaviors: A longitudinal investigation of the role of ovarian hormone and executive function changes across pregnancy and postpartum
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Binge eating attitudes and behaviors during pregnancy and the postpartum period are extremely common and
associated with adverse health outcomes for birthing individuals and their offspring. This research will further
knowledge of whether and how two biopsychological mechanisms, ovarian hormones and executive
functioning, contribute to binge eating attitudes and behaviors across pregnancy and the postpartum period.
This research therefore has the potential to identify malleable risk and protective factors crucial to developing
interventions that effectively reduce and prevent binge eating within the unique and high-risk contexts of
pregnancy and postpartum and, ultimately, promote intergenerational health.

application_id: 11033675
activity_code: K99
title: Metabolically Reprogramming Hepatic Macrophages to Conquer Immune Desert Tumors
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) play a crucial role in initiating and activating cytotoxic immune cells to target and eliminate cancer cells. However, “immune-desert” tumors, which lack sufficient APC infiltration, often exhibit reduced cytotoxic immune cell activity. This research project aims to address this challenge by metabolically reprogramming hepatic macrophages, turning them into highly effective APCs to suppress tumor progression via boosting cancer immunosurveillance. These investigations hold the potential to introduce innovative strategies for turning “cold” tumors into “hot” tumors, offering new hope in the fight against cancer.

org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed project will be among the first to examine the bidirectional and temporal associations between daily sleep dysregulation and smoking behavior and cessation outcomes using intensive longitudinal data via EMA and wearable sensors during a scheduled smoking cessation attempt among socioeconomically disadvantaged smokers. A better understanding of the relations between sleep dysregulation, smoking behavior, and smoking cessation will inform the development of novel interventions that address sleep dysregulation in smokers seeking cessation among socioeconomically disadvantaged smokers.

application_id: 10783326
activity_code: K99
title: Decoding Neuropeptide S Modulation of OFC-mediated Reward Seeking
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Here we will examine the neuronal mechanisms of Neuropeptide S (NPS), a neuropeptide shown to increase drug-seeking behavior as well as inhibit stress-induced behaviors. The orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) is a brain region linked to natural reward and drug-seeking behavior, which I found receives input from hindbrain NPS neurons. This proposal seeks to thoroughly determine how NPS transmission in the OFC and downstream targets is recruited during repeat exposure to high value rewards, ultimately identifying potentially novel treatment targets for treating opiate use disorder.

application_id: 11052465
activity_code: K99
title: Mechanisms of mGlu1 regulation of cortical inhibition and cognitive function: Implications in adolescent cocaine exposure
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Early onset cocaine use during critical neurodevelopmental periods like adolescence is associated with
pronounced cognitive deficits later in life which are paralleled by neurobiological changes in the prefrontal cortex
(PFC). Therefore, we propose a series of studies aimed at testing the hypothesis that physiological and cognitive
deficits associated with adolescent cocaine exposure are mediated by the mGlu1 subtype of metabotropic
glutamate (mGlu) receptor on specific neuron populations in the PFC. Results from these studies will enhance
our understanding of the neurobiology underlying neurodevelopmental consequences related to early onset
cocaine exposure and will aid in efforts to develop mGlu receptor modulators as novel medications to treat
cocaine use disorder.

activity_code: K99
title: The influence of astroglial mu opioid receptors in opioid potentiation of HIV neuropathogenesis
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The interwoven opioid and HIV epidemics have been linked to opioid exacerbation of inflammation and
pathology within the central nervous system (CNS), an increased likelihood of opioid use disorder, and
cognitive and behavioral deficits. µ-opioid receptors (MORs) are critical in opioid and neuroHIV co-exposure;
but the convergence point of synergistic opioid and HIV neuroinflammation and pathology, and their effects in
the particularly vulnerable striatum (critical for motivation and reward) are poorly understood. The goal of this
study is to identify the role of CNS cell-specific MOR activation in mediating opioid and HIV-induced
neuroinflammation and pathology, and whether targeting these mechanisms can rescue resultant neuronal
damage and associated behavioral deficits...

application_id: 11014962
activity_code: K99
title: Endocannabinoid modulation of behavioral engagement via an aPVT-NAc circuit
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: A key feature of substance use disorder is pathological behavioral engagement with rewarding stimuli despite
negative outcomes, yet the neural mechanisms driving these behaviors remain unresolved. Consumption of
many classes of abused drugs is established to induce maladaptive changes in the endocannabinoid signaling
system within the Nucleus Accumbens, a key nodal structure in the neurocircuitry of addiction. In this proposal,
we will use cutting-edge in vivo genetic and imaging approaches to dissect how endocannabinoids regulate
engagement in reward seeking behaviors through a Paraventricular Thalamus-Nucleus Accumbens circuit to
deepen our understanding of substance use disorders and the neural mechanisms of cannabis action.

title: Adapting and Testing a Smoking Cessation Intervention in Adults with Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will increase our understanding of critical cigarette smoking treatment barriers for people living with
intellectual and developmental disabilities. This project will also bridge a significant treatment-focused research

intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDDs) utilizing stakeholder feedback and perspectives.

title: Valuation Mechanisms that Shape the Interaction of Phasic and Tonic Dopamine
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Disordered motivation and learning in psychiatric and substance use disorders stem from a breakdown of
dopamine regulation across brain regions and timescales. This research aims to characterize the precise
behavioral conditions and circuit mechanisms that facilitate multi-timescale dopamine interactions in service of
motivational flexibility. This proposal seeks to characterize the subjective valuation processes and circuitry
governing multi-timescale dopamine release, aiming to identify distinct mechanisms selectively vulnerable in
addiction and psychiatric disorders.

application_id: 10865328
activity_code: K99
title: Dissecting the algorithmic and neural circuit basis of dopamine-driven learning in the striatum
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The neurotransmitter dopamine (DA) is thought to play a central role in reward-based learning, but the precise nature of this role and how it may be implemented in neural circuits remain the subject of controversy. Taking a reductionist approach, this project leverages novel optical, electrophysiological, and machine learning tools to precisely identify the algorithmic and neural circuit basis of dopamine-driven learning in the striatum of behaving mice. By isolating and dissecting dopamine’s role in learning with unprecedented precision, this project has the potential to transform our understanding of diseases that involve the dysregulation of dopamine, including Parkinson’s disease, addiction, and mood disorders.

title: Neural circuitry and population dynamics for perception of social vocalizations in the auditory pathway
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Social animals depend on vocal communication for survival in many contexts, and the brain must extract information from acoustic features of calls and communicate with circuits for appropriate behavioral responses. Depending on the call meaning, this processing must occur over time scales needed for guiding various behaviors. The research proposed here tests the overall hypothesis that top-down circuits in the auditory system facilitate vocalization-guided behaviors across contexts, using multiple methods for monitoring and manipulating activity in neurons and ensembles to dissect the circuits involved in driving behavior.

title: Molecular and Functional Mechanisms Underlying Cortical Activity in CACNA1A Epilepsy
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
CACNA1A mutations are an underdiagnosed cause of severe, treatment-resistant, early-onset
epilepsy and other neurological disorders including familial hemiplegic migraine type 1, episodic
ataxia type 2, and spinocerebellar ataxia type 6. This proposal will use the first human-derived, three-

activity_code: K99
title: Defining the role of MET components in vestibular hair cell maturation and gene therapy responsiveness
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Balance disorders affect an estimated 14.8% of the population annually and often involve some genetic
component affecting hair cells. There are currently few effective treatments for vestibular hypofunction
targeting hair cell function, in part due to limitations in the scope of research methodologies. In this proposal, I
describe experiments designed to characterize role of mechanotransduction genes in calcium exchange within
vestibular hair cells, and the optimal therapeutic window for MET channel gene replacement in vestibular hair
cells.

title: Roles of neutrophil-sensory neuron interactions in dental pulp inflammation and pain
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Pulpitis, or inflammation of the dental pulp tissue, is a debilitating painful disease and one of the main causes of
invasive dental procedures. In this project, we investigate the roles of neutrophils in sensory afferent injury and
pain and the role of sensory neurons in modulating innate immune response in pulpitis. The project is relevant
to NIDCR’s mission because of its potential to improve dental health by investigating neuro-immune mechanisms
driving dental pain and inflammation.

title: The Academy at MD Anderson UTHealth Houston Graduate School  - Maximizing Opportunities for Training, Development, and Community
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: Project Narrative The purpose of this project is to contribute to increasing diversity, inclusivity, and belonging for those underrepresented in the biomedical workforce that is known to increase productivity of teams in the biomedical sciences. Specifically, this proposal seeks funding for eight PhD trainees who will engage in activities to strengthen their academic skills and confidence, promote their career exploration across biomedical fields, and establish a community of belonging. The proposed activities will improve the overall environment of the graduate school and help our students excel as researchers to propel them to careers of their choice in the biomedical scientific community.

activity_code: K08
title: Understanding the long-term effects of hurricanes on cardiovascular health and outcomes
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH STATEMENT
The short-term effects of extreme weather such as hurricanes on cardiovascular disease (CVD) are well-docu-
mented but long-term CVD effects are unknown, likely leading to adverse outcomes, particularly for vulnerable
residents whose limited resources often mean staying in place. This project will identify hurricane victims at
high risk of CVD mortality and morbidity by measuring Hurricane Sandy’s impact on New York City in 2012,
and will lay the groundwork for a validation study using Medicare claims data from the devastating 2017 hurri-
cane season – the costliest in US history. Our goal is to inform individual- and population-level pre- and post-
disaster management strategies and assure that they address the need for interventions among those at great-
est risk ...

application_id: 10820953
activity_code: F31
title: Aristotelia Alkaloids as Probes for the Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: NARRATIVE The nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) are crucial membrane-bound receptors found throughout the central nervous system that have been implicated in a variety of disease states. High levels of similarity between nAChR subtypes, however, have limited their pharmacological utility, as few ligands that are both selective and potent have been identified. This proposed project investigates a series of alkaloids from Aristotelia chilensis, accessed through isolation and biomimetic synthesis, for their activity as subtype-selective nAChR antagonists, which will lay the foundation to develop new tools to improve the field’s understanding of the nAChRs.

title: A signaling crosstalk regulation in neural crest-derived cranial suture
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Craniofacial deformity often involves cranial sutures, and adequate understanding of the molecular mechanism
underlying cranial suture development is urgently demanded for exploring new diagnostic and therapeutic
methods for cranial diseases, such as craniosynostosis and sutural agenesis. Neural crest cells, a multipotent
stem cell population during embryonic stage, make great contributions to cranial suture development, especially
frontal (metopic) suture. I proposed a multipronged approach to understand how the neural crest-derived cranial
suture is coordinately regulated by the complicated signaling network, and how this contributes to the cranial
suture development and related diseases, in order to explore novel and critical factors for the diagnostic and
therapeutic ...

application_id: 11237906
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular mechanism underlying FUS regulation under transcriptional and DNA damage stress
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Fused in Sarcoma (FUS) is a nuclear RNA Binding Protein (RBP) involved in many cellular processes, including
DNA damage response (DDR) and transcription regulation. Mutations in FUS can induce aggregation,
mislocalization, and disruptions in DDR and transcriptional pathways, contributing to the pathogenesis of adult-
onset neurodegenerative disorders such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal lobar
dementia (FTLD). Through advanced microscopy and next-generation sequencing approaches, we aim to
investigate the molecular mechanism underlying FUS regulation under transcriptional and DNA damage stress
and its implications in ALS/FTLD, paving the way for novel therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 10906019
activity_code: F31
title: Non-cell autonomous consequences of cytoplasmic DNA
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chromosomally unstable cancer cells frequently harbor cytoplasmic DNA that activates a cell-intrinsic innate immune response. We currently do not understand whether these cytoplasmic DNAs are capable of eliciting non-cell autonomous effects to surrounding cells in the tumor microenvironment. The proposed research seeks to elucidate whether and how fragmented chromosomes entrapped in the cytoplasm can undergo lateral transfer between cells in a shared environment, which will advance our understanding of cell-to-cell interactions in the tumor microenvironment.

application_id: 10864723
activity_code: K99
title: Bone-like organoids to understand factors controlling local bone immune response and regeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Large craniofacial bone defects are challenging to heal without surgical intervention and often require bone grafting, which has variable clinical outcomes with limited metrics to predict healing outcomes. Patient-specific organoids and systemic immune cell profiling are promising tools to understand mechanisms regulating regenerative processes, yet to date these tools are not widely used to study craniofacial bone healing. The experiments outlined in this proposal will characterize a newly developed bone-like organoid, as well as the local and systemic immune response during healing of a craniofacial defect, to identify therapeutics to improve healing and develop predictive models of healing outcomes.

application_id: 11194397
activity_code: K99
title: Exploring PAR2 Trafficking in Oral Cancer: Implications for Pain Signaling and Drug Delivery
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Oral cancer pain, the most common and severe form of pain associated with cancer, is ineffectively treated with
opioids. The proposed studies aim to investigate how cancer influences the protease-activated receptor 2
(PAR2), a demonstrated mediator of oral cancer pain, to move inside neurons (trafficking) and promote pain
signals (signaling). The proposed research training and career development programs will contribute to my long-
term goal of directing an academic research laboratory devoted to developing nanomedicines that target
trafficking and signaling of PAR2 and similar receptors to provide more effective relief for oral cancer pain.

application_id: 11065904
activity_code: F31
title: Dissecting How SYNJ2 Facilitates mRNA Targeting and Translation at Neuronal Mitochondria
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Neurons depend on the local transport of mRNA to maintain healthy axons and dendrites. Our laboratory has
demonstrated that mRNA encoding PINK1, a gene associated with Parkinson’s disease, is locally trafficked with
neuronal mitochondria through a protein anchor called synaptojanin2. My project aims to determine precisely
how mRNAs can be targeted to mitochondria in this fashion, and how important this particular targeting
mechanism is for PINK1 function.

title: Acupuncture for Cognitive Health in Older Survivors of Prostate Cancer (ACHIEVE)
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Maintaining cognitive health represents a key priority for older survivors of prostate cancer. The goal of this
project is to advance scientific understanding of cognitive dysfunction in prostate cancer survivors and to
evaluate an acupuncture treatment for this debilitating condition. This research addresses an important public
health issue for the aging population and has the potential to lead to novel therapies to promote cognitive
health and well-being in millions of cancer survivors.

activity_code: F31
title: Dendritic integration of spatial and object features in a Drosophila sensorimotor circuit.
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Neuronal computations underly information processing which are critical in driving our behavior and experiences,
however, are disrupted under neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental diseases, ultimately affecting
behavior and cognitive function. Here we propose an innovative approach to investigate general mechanisms
that govern the interactions between a neuron’s intrinsic properties and synaptic organization, which influence
neuronal computations. A deeper understanding of neuronal computations will directly provide new perspectives
to approaching neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental disorders by providing potential new avenues for
treatments.

application_id: 11243928
activity_code: F31
title: Revealing cell type-specific connectivity motifs in orofacial premotor circuits
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Orofacial movements are hierarchically controlled by high-level motor centers in the cerebrum, midbrain, and
cerebellum and low-level premotor circuits in the pons and medulla. The cellular and circuit organization of
these premotor circuits is unknown largely due to longstanding deficiencies in hindbrain cell type atlases. This
project will leverage new BRAIN initiative cell type datasets along with cutting-edge techniques for
transsynaptic viral tracing and multiplexed fluorescence in situ hybridization to identify cell type-specific
connectivity motifs in orofacial premotor circuits.

application_id: 11162453
activity_code: K99
title: The role of lipid droplet proteins in eicosanoid production and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Narrative
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) affects ~30% of the global adult population and is associated with
morbidity and mortality from hepatic and extra-hepatic complications, causing a major health burden for our
society. Hepatic lipid droplets (LDs) have emerged as important players in NAFLD, yet their impact on its
pathogenesis and progression is not fully understood. By studying common genetic coding variants in two LD
proteins (PNPLA3-I148M and PLIN2-Pro251), this proposal aims to elucidate the role of LDs in NAFLD, leading
to a better understanding of this disease and providing new therapeutic targets.

activity_code: F31
title: Mitochondrial Regulation of Interferon Response in Melanoma
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Reduced interferon (IFN) signaling is major way tumor cells evade the immune system. Mitochondria are central players in cell metabolism, but their role in regulating anti-tumor immune responses is less well understood. This project examines the link between mitochondria and IFN signaling, which may provide new ways to target tumor metabolism to bolster anti-tumor immune responses and the effectiveness of immunotherapy.

application_id: 11161828
activity_code: F31
title: How does mutant ATXN1 expression in microglia impact Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 1 pathogenesis?
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Spinocerebellar ataxia type 1 (SCA1) is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disease with no cure.
Previous work has elucidated microglia contribution to SCA1 motor phenotypes and microglia molecular
phenotypes in SCA1 mice. The goal of this proposal is to investigate how mutant ATXN1 expression in microglia
impacts motor function, microglia molecular phenotype, and cerebellar cell pathology, which is vital for
understanding the underlying disease mechanisms of SCA1.

application_id: 11240792
activity_code: F31
title: The role of neurotrophins in the maintenance and function of MF-CA3 synapses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The mechanism of learning and memory is widely studied, however, the detailed function of neurotrophins BDNF
and NT3 in the hippocampus remains limited. This proposed research aims to elucidate the role of BDNF and
NT3 in MF-CA3 synapse development, maintenance and function. Research proposed will enhance our
understanding of the mechanism underlying learning and memory at MF-CA3 synapses.

phr: Project Narrative Asymptomatic colonization (i.e., carriage) with carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), a type of highly drug-resistant bacteria, poses urgent challenges in U.S. hospitals: undetected colonized patients can transmit CRE to other patients, and colonized patients themselves are at a significantly higher risk of developing CRE infections. Because widescale screening for CRE colonization remains impractical for most U.S. hospitals, hospitals miss critical opportunities to identify patients at high risk of CRE colonization and infection early, when it is still possible to prevent deleterious patient outcomes. title: Pancreatic beta-cell glucose toxicity and mRNA translation
org_name: JOSLIN DIABETES CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Failure of pancreatic β-cells to secrete sufficient insulin hormone to regulate blood glucose is a hallmark of diabetes. application_id: 10947852
activity_code: K99
title: The Role of the PBN Melanocortin System in the Regulation of Ingestive Behaviors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The parabrachial nucleus serves as a crucial sensory hub, relaying data from internal and external senses to shape motivated behaviors; in this intricate network, the activation of melanocortin 3 and 4 receptors (MC3R and MC4R) expressing neurons can either stimulate or suppress ingestive behaviors. Of particular importance, the PBN MC3R neurons represent a novel population in the PBN as they contradict the overall role of the PBN in inhibiting feeding. Understanding how these two neuronal populations in the PBN integrate opposing ingestive behaviors is the major goal of this project.

application_id: 10812471
activity_code: K99
title: Novel optical detection methods for ultrafast positron emission tomography (PET)
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Positron emission tomography (PET) is a powerful imaging modality to visualize molecular activities in the
human body. In this project, we aim to dramatically improve the imaging resolution of PET by developing a
novel positron detection mechanism that will bring ultrafast coincidence time resolution (CTR) of 10ps. If
successful, the 10ps CTR PET will enable dynamic PET imaging to probe human physiology, and we will
investigate this possibility for neurochemistry-specific functional brain imaging.

activity_code: F31
title: Mechanism of Par1c-mediated AMPA receptor trafficking and synaptic plasticity
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
The human brain is remarkably unique in its high capacity to learn and adapt to new environments, which has
been attributed to the plasticity of synaptic connections. MARK1, which encodes partitioning defective 1c
(Par1c), is a gene that has human-specific accelerated evolution and is associated with autism spectrum
disorder (ASD) and bipolar disorder, suggesting a role for this protein in learning and memory. This project
aims to describe a molecular mechanism of Par1c-mediated synaptic plasticity and learning through AMPA
receptor trafficking.

application_id: 11315655
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the cellular basis of neurological dysfunction in models of ALG8 Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Crosstalk between neurons and specialized support cells called glia is essential for proper neural circuit
development and function. Neuron-glia communication is dependent on precise protein-protein interactions that
are modified by small sugar groups, a process called glycosylation, to increase signaling strength and specificity.
The proposed project will define how loss of one critical glycosylation enzyme, ALG8, changes cell proliferation,
survival, and morphology of neurons and glia during brain development, which will provide valuable insight into
future therapeutic targets for human congenital disorders of glycosylation.

application_id: 11164703
activity_code: K99
title: Open-Source Software Tools for Rapid Radiofrequency Coil Modeling and Simulation in MRI
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
 The goal of this project is to develop an open-source electromagnetic simulation suite for radiofrequency
coil design and modeling in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Novel computational electromagnetics methods
will be developed and combined with optimization routines and tensor decompositions to allow efﬁcient simula-
tions of realistic MRI setups for brain and body imaging at any operating frequency. The proposed suite will be
used for the rational design, optimization, assessment, and construction of a novel coil array for prostate
imaging at 7T MRI.

application_id: 11244022
activity_code: F31
title: Discovering Drug-Like Probes for the α9α10 Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: NARRATIVE
The nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) are ligand-gated ion channels whose various subtypes have
been targeted in many central nervous system diseases and disorders. The α9α10 nAChR has been implicated
as a drug target for neuropathic pain, but very few drug-like and selective ligands have been developed. This
research project will fill this gap by developing an α9α10 nAChR-selective antagonist with promising drug-like
properties to further study this receptor’s role in neuropathic pain.

application_id: 11078799
activity_code: K99
title: Toward unshielded MRI for improved safety and access
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
Whole-body MRI scanners face limited accessibility due to their high expense and specific siting requirements,
which can substantially increase costs. MRI suites rely on a costly Faraday shielded room to eliminate image
artifacts caused by external radio waves as well as reduce radio wave emission which can disrupt nearby hospital
equipment. We aim to enhance MRI accessibility and reduce expenses by replacing the Faraday shield with a
combination of strategically placed passive EM absorbers within the MRI bore and an active cancellation system
based on the parallel transmission (pTx) technology.

application_id: 10783418
activity_code: F32
title: Resurgent Currents in Human Dorsal Root Ganglia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS DALLAS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Voltage gated sodium channels (VGSCs) are critical gatekeepers of nociceptor excitability that mediate acute and chronic pain. We discovered that VGSC modulatory proteins that evoke resurgent sodium current are expressed in human, but not murine, nociceptors, strongly suggesting that human nociceptors display resurgent sodium currents. This study will exclusively use human dorsal root ganglia and, focusing on resurgent current in nociceptors, characterize sensitization- and pain-dependent alterations in VGSC expression, modulation, and function.

application_id: 11363996
activity_code: K99
title: AN ADAPTIVE FRAMEWORK TO SYNTHESIZE AND RECONFIGURE BACTERIAL VIRUSES (PHAGES) TO COUNTER ANTIBIOTIC RESISTANCE
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Phage therapy, which uses bacterial viruses (phages) to eliminate pathogenic infections, offers a promising
solution to the growing public health crisis of antibiotic resistance. However, current phage therapy development
is highly time and resource-intensive, limiting this treatment's scalability and, therefore, accessibility to the
general public. This proposal aims to create an adaptive framework using phage genome decompression and
cell-free synthesis to rapidly generate effective phages for treatment, significantly advancing our capacity to
address the escalating antibiotic resistance crisis.

application_id: 11248411
activity_code: K99
title: Development of Highly Multiplexed Raman Imaging Platform for Alzheimer’s Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immune cells have recently been recognized as vital players in Alzheimer’s disease, as they change with disease
progression and can shape the disease pathology through complex mechanisms. We require a new imaging tool
to closely study various immune cell populations simultaneously within the spatial context of human brain tissue.
The complete molecular mapping of the brain-immune relationship, enabled by our Raman imaging approach,
will pave the way for improving our ability to understand, prevent, and treat Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 11241849
activity_code: F31
title: Tract-based benchmarking of limbic abnormalities in temporal lobe epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
This proposed work addresses the critical need for improved diagnostic and treatment strategies in epilepsy by
investigating the role of white matter tract abnormalities in temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE). By employing
advanced neuroimaging techniques to characterize structural and functional disruptions in mesial limbic white
matter tracts, this research aims to provide a more comprehensive understanding of epileptogenic networks
than conventional methods. The insights gained from this study have the potential to inform more precise,
personalized interventions for epilepsy patients and lay the foundation for future research on tract
abnormalities in other neurological disorders.

application_id: 11103247
activity_code: K99
title: Paternal exposures, sperm epigenetic marks, and autism spectrum disorder in offspring
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: The study will examine the role of paternal factors in risk of autism spectrum disorder (ASD). I will examine
whether exposure to air pollution during the sperm development period preceding a conception is associated
with risk of autism in the offspring, and the additive or synergistic effects of paternal comorbidities and related
pharmacologic treatments during spermatogenesis. I will also examine whether exposure to air pollution is
associated with changes in the sperm methylome overall and specifically in ASD related loci, which might
inform mechanistic pathways.

application_id: 11124159
activity_code: K08
title: Increasing Access to Transplant through Improved Clinical Decision Making
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: The proposed project will generate new knowledge of the magnitude and mediators of factors that influence access to transplant at each phase of the transplant selection process (aim 1), how center-level processes and individual clinician decision making may influence access to transplant. The results will elucidate the influence of individual clinician practices embedded in center-level processes may influence access to transplant. Our toolkit will represent the first intervention developed to standardize clinical practice and mitigate obstacles to transplant access at each step in the transplant selection process.

application_id: 10886398
activity_code: K99
title: Paternal exposures, sperm epigenetic marks, and autism spectrum disorder in offspring
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: The study will examine the role of paternal factors in risk of autism spectrum disorder (ASD). I will examine whether exposure to air pollution during the sperm development period preceding a conception is associated with risk of autism in the offspring, and the additive or synergistic effects of paternal comorbidities and related pharmacologic treatments during spermatogenesis. I will also examine whether exposure to air pollution is associated with changes in the sperm methylome overall and specifically in ASD related loci, which might inform mechanistic pathways.

title: Investigating the Role of Slc4a4 in Multiple Sclerosis
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Multiple Sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune disease characterized by central nervous system demyelination and
neurological deficits. Emerging evidence suggests that astrocytes may contribute to MS pathogenesis by

astrocyte-enriched gene, in regulating BBB function and its impact on MS disease progression.

application_id: 11031935
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating roles for oxidative guanine damage in transcription regulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
Oxidative stress emanating from endogenous sources and genotoxic environmental exposures is a causative
agent of DNA damage that is linked to inflammation, age-related diseases and cancer in humans. This proposal
will utilize a novel tool to induce singlet oxygen mediated base damage in human cells specifically at telomeres,
at endogenous gene promoters or throughout the genome, to study the specific impact of 8-oxoguanine lesion
processing on the regulation of transcriptional responses to genotoxic oxidative damage. The project is therefore
relevant to understand the crosstalk between repair of oxidative damage and gene expression, and their role in
human disease.

application_id: 10917275
activity_code: K99
title: Prohibiting Cell Death in Human Keratocytes: New Insights for Non-surgical Keratoconus Treatment
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
Stromal cell apoptosis plays essential roles in keratoconus pathology, which can be inhibited through covalent
and non-covalent ligands. This project established an advanced drug screening pipeline facilitated by high-
throughput technologies including mass spectrometry, in-cell ELISA, and glycan microarrays. The proposed
analytical platform has great potential to discover novel non-surgical keratoconus therapies targeting the
extrinsic apoptosis pathway and can be also utilized in other corneal dystrophies.

application_id: 11312893
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of lipid-laden astrocyte formation after spinal cord injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Spinal cord injury (SCI) results in significant cell death causing the accumulation of excess cellular and lipid
debris which, if not cleared timely from the injury site, can impede tissue regeneration and recovery. The
proposed research on lipid laden astrocytes after SCI will characterize a novel cell subtype that is a component
of the chronically inhibitory and persistent gliosis surrounding the lesion. Understanding the function of foamy
astrocytes and factors that contribute to the perpetuation of gliosis are crucial in allowing for modulation of the
injury site and facilitation of a more permissive environment – resulting in axon growth and functional recovery
for patients.

application_id: 11313102
activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the structural and molecular mechanisms of a TRPA1 channelopathic mutation
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There is currently no safe and effective therapeutic for individuals suffering from chronic or hypersensitive pain,
often times inadvertently leading to opioid overuse and addiction as a consequence of trying to manage pain.
Transient receptor potential ankyrin 1 (TRPA1) is at the forefront of pain perception and this project seeks to
understand how a disease-causing mutant leads to TRPA1 structural and functional dysregulation. By studying
how aspects of this channel are perturbed in a hypersensitive pain disorder, my work will define the key structural
and functional components that can be targeted for therapeutic development to create a long-term solution for
individuals experiencing impairments in pain perception and avoid opioid misuse.

title: Local Economic Conditions and Patterns of Family Instability and Complexity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will assess how local economic conditions shape patterns of family instability and complexity, with attention to how these processes developed over historical time and vary by race and social class. The findings will be critically important for understanding population health patterns because the family environment shapes parent and child well-being across the life span. title: Identification and Characterization of Functional lncRNAs in human cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project narrative: Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) play a role in many fundamental biological processes and are implicated in human disease, yet few have been characterized to date due to insufficient tools. application_id: 11317928
activity_code: F31
title: In search of the template: the neural substrate of birdsong memory
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
When learning motor skills like speaking or playing an instrument, we imitate experienced mentors, comparing
our actions to theirs, but how this comparison occurs remains unknown. Similarly, songbirds learn to sing by
encoding a template memory of their father's song, which they then use to compare and guide learning of their
own song. This proposal integrates experimental and computational approaches to identify the neural
mechanisms underlying this comparison, with the potential to inform how this process is disrupted in neurological
disorders such as Parkinson’s disease.

application_id: 10992605
activity_code: K99
title: Regulation of exon junction complex composition and assembly
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Nonsense mediated mRNA decay (NMD) promotes the normal gene expression of unmutated messenger RNAs
(mRNAs) and destroys mutated mRNAs that produce potential disease-causing proteins. The exon junction
complex (EJC) is one key determinant of whether an mRNA undergoes NMD and contains the NMD factor UPF2
or the central cell-signaling protein AKT, the latter of which is promoted by insulin. This work will build upon this
recent finding by defining the molecular mechanisms involved in this process, which is important because NMD
and AKT signaling are both dysregulated in human diseases like Fragile X Syndrome, some cancers, and type
1 and type 2 diabetes.

application_id: 10947390
activity_code: R36
title: Molecular Mechanisms of VMAT2 Function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Narrative The vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) is essential for loading neurotransmitters such as dopamine, serotonin and norepinephrine into synaptic vesicles before their release into the synapse. Amphetamine has been shown to alter VMAT2 function, causing release of neurotransmitters from synaptic vesicles. In this proposal, we aim to study the structure and function of VMAT2 and the differences that occur as a result of amphetamine in order to generate new therapies to treat neuropsychiatric disorders and amphetamine abuse.

application_id: 11031946
activity_code: K99
title: Understanding how transposons generate new immune gene protein-coding splice variants
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: Project Narrative
More than 80% of human protein coding genes are alternatively spliced, effectively increasing the proteome in
comparison to the total number of genes. While transposable elements have been largely recognized to provide
alternative promoters and enhancers, their contribution to the protein coding sequences is still debated. The goal
of this proposal is to understand how transposable elements shape the evolution and diversification of vertebrate
proteomes, as well as which events and mechanisms promote their origin and affect their expression.

application_id: 11242113
activity_code: F32
title: Genetics and therapeutics pinpoint link between glucose metabolism and Alzheimer’s disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Diabetes and dementia have an unexpectedly high rate of comorbidity with a disparity of healthcare burden
based on race/ethnicity as well as sex differences. Previous studies have not deciphered the mechanisms by
which genetic diversity interacts with environmental factors to explain these discrepancies. Our proposed
research will take a two-pronged approach, first determining how changes in the Prdm10 gene affect dys-
glycemia and cognition, and second evaluating how lifetime exposure to a diabetes drug, miglitol, rescues
cognition in a mouse model susceptible to cognitive decline.

title: TULP3 integrates essential ciliary functions
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
The primary cilium is a tiny, extracellular-facing organelle, with a distinct composition from that of the rest of the
cell or plasma membrane, that coordinates diverse extracellular signals (i.e odorants, metabolites, light,
neuropeptides, hormones) and converts them into coordinated cellular responses. Though primary cilia have
been implicated in a number of pathologies including ciliopathies, diabetes, neural disorders, disrupted immunity,
and cancer, the mechanisms by which cilia are built and process signals remain poorly understood. The goal of
this proposal is to explore the role of the ciliary traffic protein TULP3 in coordinating both making a cilium and
maintaining receptors inside the cilium and how understanding these functions applies in the biological conte...

application_id: 11114062
activity_code: K23
title: Altered High-Density Lipoprotein function in Patients with Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Damage to the vascular endothelium is not only responsible for cardiovascular disease – the leading cause of
mortality in patients with autoimmune diseases – but also implicated in the pathogenesis of idiopathic
inflammatory myopathies (IIM). This study will investigate the possible link between vascular damage and
dysfunctional pro-inflammatory HDL, which is known to promote lipid oxidation and perpetuate endothelial
damage, in a longitudinal cohort of patients with IIM. Understanding how HDL function contributes to not only
macrovascular damage but also microvascular damage in IIM could offer promise for emerging therapeutic
targets to improve the lives of patients with IIM as well as other autoimmune diseases.

application_id: 11071074
activity_code: F32
title: Strategies and Consequences of Polymicrobial Nasal Colonization
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite the clear clinical significance of polymicrobial infections, the mechanistic factors and consequences of
polymicrobial interactions during colonization of the upper respiratory tract remain poorly understood. In this
proposal, I aim to 1) leverage functional genomics methods to determine how bacterial virulence networks are
altered in the context of complex infectious colonization models, and 2) determine the effect of these colonization
networks on cellular host responses following colonization through profiling of host mucosal responses. This
research serves to identify the precise mechanisms underlying the poor clinical outcomes associated with
bacterial co-infection.

application_id: 11123185
activity_code: R25
title: Cancer Aging and Metabolism research experience Program (CAMP)
org_name: BECKMAN RESEARCH INSTITUTE/CITY OF HOPE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Almost all human cancers have alterations in metabolic pathways associated with aging and cancer signaling,
but undergraduates rarely receive cross-training in cancer, aging, and metabolism research. The proposed
Cancer Aging and Metabolism research experience Program (CAMP) will address the need for a well-trained
workforce by providing a unique 10-week hands-on learning experience for 20 undergraduates each summer.
This first iteration of CAMP will also pilot the integration of school-year academic and research activities with
California State University, Dominguez Hills in an effort to bolster the pipeline for undergraduates to thrive as
future cancer researchers.

activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating mechanisms underlying NSCLC progression to leptomeningeal disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. In rare but deadly instances, lung cancer can spread to the cerebrospinal fluid, and this form of metastatic disease is difficult to diagnose and treat. My research focuses on advancing our understanding of how exactly cells enter and survive in the cerebrospinal fluid and the mechanisms by which developing resistance to targeted treatments can increase the likelihood of cells making this journey. The results of this research will hopefully lead to identification of novel treatment targets for this currently incurable disease.

title: Chemoenzymatic Total Synthesis of Pentaene Antifungals and Analog Development
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Invasive fungal infections affect millions of patients worldwide and are more deadly in immunocompromised
patients, including those with COVID, AIDS or undergoing chemotherapy. This research fellowship will

structure–activity relationships will enable the development of new treatments for invasive fungal infections that
may rival treatments in the clinic today.

application_id: 11164128
activity_code: F32
title: Investingating the mechanisms controlling Neisseria gonorrhoeae Type IV pilus dynamics
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Type IV pilus (T4p) is the major colonization and virulence factor of Neisseria gonorrhoeae
(Gc), the causative agent of gonorrhea in humans. We recently identified a novel component of
the T4p in Gc, TfpC, but we do not yet understand how specific components of the T4p, like TfpC
and others, contribute to its function. In this proposal, we aim to define the function of TfpC and
its relationship to other proteins within the T4p; thus, we will define how these molecular
components work together to regulate T4p dynamics, which will assist in developing future drug
targets for gonorrhea.
