title: The role of the protocadherin gene cluster in neurodevelopment and the implications for neurodevelopmental disorders
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE During development, functional circuits between the approximately 80 billion human neurons connecting trillions of synapses must be established. activity_code: R36
title: CA1 cell-type susceptibility in Alzheimer's
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Narrative In the early stages of Alzheimer’s disease (AD), entorhinal cortex connections with hippocampal CA1 emerges as a primary neural circuit that undergoes significant neurodegenerative change, but the cell type specific connections and their susceptibility remains unclear. Here, we will use advanced viral labeling strategies in an AD mouse model to identify vulnerable CA1 projection cell types and characterize their morphological neurodegenerative changes to AD. After identifying CA1 structural vulnerabilities, we will use MERFISH spatial transcriptomics to investigate the changes to AD-relevant gene expression within these vulnerable neurons and characterize the molecular changes that are associated with AD neurodegeneration.

application_id: 11240886
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating spatio-temporal innate immune cell modulation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis stress response and replication heterogeneity
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heterogeneity in multiple facets of Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), including in bacterial physiological status,
is a major driver complicating disease treatment and infection outcome. Immune cells with their specialized
arsenal of antimicrobial factors and different metabolic status play an important role in the local environment
experienced by Mtb, leading to different bacterial responses and active versus non-active populations of Mtb
within lung lesions. This project aims to provide vital spatiotemporal insight into Mtb stress response and
replication in vivo, and reveal how macrophage subtypes differentially control Mtb growth in the context of
necrotic lesions.

application_id: 11164253
activity_code: F32
title: Role of Myeloid Cell Lrp1 in Rift Valley Fever Virus Infection and Dissemination
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Rift Valley Fever virus (RVFV), a pathogenic arbovirus with significant pandemic potential, relies on low density
lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1 (Lrp1) for efficient cell entry and infection. I hypothesize that RVFV first
infects myeloid cells at the site of infection in an Lrp1-dependent manner and then spreads to distant organs.
This proposal will interrogate the role of myeloid Lrp1 in RVFV pathogenesis, dissemination, and the initiation of
the host immune response.

application_id: 11163731
activity_code: F32
title: Defining mechanistic drivers of impaired B cell response to influenza A virus vaccine under conditions of obesity
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Individuals with clinical obesity (BMI < 30) are prone to poor responses to influenza vaccination
and at elevated risk for severe respiratory pathology following influenza infection. This proposal
aims to discover the mechanistic shortcomings of B cell responses to influenza vaccination in
conditions of obesity. Defining these impairments can inform future vaccine design to bolster
protection from influenza in a population of humans susceptible to severe respiratory disease.

application_id: 11094240
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating UPF3 paralog function in Nonsense-Mediated mRNA Decay and Genetic Compensation
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Nonsense Mediated mRNA Decay (NMD) degrades both aberrant transcripts and “normal” transcripts with other specific features, impacting around 10% of the transcriptome. This project will investigate the biological roles and molecular functions of UPF3, a disease-linked core NMD factor, in both human cells and zebrafish development. Taken together, this study will help us understand how UPF3-activated NMD and transcriptional regulation is modulated, and elucidate connections between mRNA decay and transcriptional regulation.

title: Genomic and transcriptomic analysis to dissect the role of the intrinsic resistance factor RarA in carbapenem resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae
org_name: BROAD INSTITUTE, INC.
phr: Project Narrative
This project investigates how a genetic regulator, RarA, contributes to antibiotic resistance in Klebsiella
pneumoniae, a bacterial pathogen that is often difficult to treat. By understanding the role of RarA in resistance,

Neutralizing antibodies that prevent and control human disease are of considerable

application_id: 11012405
activity_code: K23
title: Understanding components of mind-body exercise for physical activity engagement in metabolic syndrome
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Physical inactivity is prevalent, costly, and deadly, particularly among individuals with cardiometabolic
conditions. While mind-body exercise interventions (e.g., yoga, tai chi), which include several therapeutic
components (e.g., physical exercise, mindful attention), are promising for improving physical activity levels, it is
difficult to optimize these interventions for physical activity promotion without understanding the effects of these
core components on health behavior change processes. Using metabolic syndrome as a model, this project
will assess the feasibility and acceptability of a factorial design that examines the effects of two mind-body
exercise components, exercise and mindful attention, on key health behavior change processes.

title: Towards a structural understanding of transcriptional regulation by ZMYM2-KDM1A-CoREST
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Genome organization is essential for gene regulation, which controls cell fate commitment and
differentiation. I utilize integrative structural biology, combining proteomics, biophysics, structural biology,

activity_code: K01
title: Development and feasibility testing of a Boys & Girls Clubs' sports intervention to promote physical activity in rural girls: Girls PLAY
org_name: SAN DIEGO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Less than a third of United States youth meet physical activity guidelines, with lower activity levels found among girls, racial minorities, and those living in rural areas. The proposed research aims to develop, tailor, and examine the impact of a tailored Boys & Girls Clubs (BGC) sport sampling and physical literacy intervention on rural girls’ physical activity levels. This work is significant in that addressing barriers to physical activity and sport via BGC can reduce gender, racial, and geographic disparities in youth activity levels.

application_id: 11140375
activity_code: K08
title: Barriers and Facilitators for Delivery of Guideline-Concordant Care for Adolescents and Young Adults with Ewing Sarcoma and Osteosarcoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Significant disparities in cancer mortality persist among adolescents and young adults (AYAs) with Ewing
sarcoma (EWS) and osteosarcoma (OS), which may be partially due to unequal access to evidence based,
guideline concordant care. My previous research demonstrated that 50% of AYAs with OS in California
received guideline concordant care and that not receiving this care negatively impacted survival. This study
seeks to increase the delivery of guideline concordant care to AYAs with OS and EWS to improve overall
survival.

application_id: 10864881
activity_code: K99
title: Elucidating the sterol-sensing mechanisms that regulate lipid metabolism.
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  HMG CoA reductase (HMGCR) is the molecular target of cholesterol lowering statin drugs, and a key regulatory enzyme of the cholesterol biosynthetic pathway which is subjected to feedback regulation by sterol and nonsterol isoprenoid end products that act to induce its endoplasmic reticulum (ER)–associated degradation (ERAD). I have shown that the 1,1-bisphosphonate ester SRP3042 mimics sterols in inducing HMGCR ERAD without disrupting transcriptional feedback mechanisms, and a photoactivatable form specifically crosslinks HMGCR. This proposal aims to determine the sterol- sensing mechanisms through which HMGCR ERAD is initiated to determine whether SRP3042 induced HMGCR ERAD is a viable strategy for overcoming statin-resistance, will identify the molecular target of LY29...

application_id: 11241509
activity_code: F30
title: Identification of genes that limit CAR T persistence in solid tumors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
CAR T cell therapy has failed to translate to solid tumors, partly due to limited intratumoral persistence of CAR
T cells. The proposed work builds upon the first in vivo genome-scale CRISPR screen of CAR T persistence in
solid tumors and will investigate ATXN7L3 and USP22 in regulating this process. This work will provide further
knowledge on engineered T cell biology and its findings have the potential for direct clinical translation to enhance
CAR T cell therapy.

application_id: 11158435
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the functional role of KLRG1+ conventional helper T cells in the tumor microenvironment
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
CD4+ T cells shape the tumor microenvironment to promote antitumor or pro-tumor immunity. Our data suggests
a novel, mechanistic link between KLRG1+ FoxP3- CD4+ T cells (Tconv) and both tumor progression and
immunotherapy response, yet little is known regarding the functional characteristics of this tumor-infiltrating cell
subset. The goal of this proposal is to fill this gap in knowledge by elucidating the functional, transcriptional, and
epigenetic characteristics of KLRG1+ CD4+ Tconv in cancer and determine whether novel strategies to deplete
KLRG1+ CD4+ T cells can augment antitumor immunity and enhance response to immunotherapy.

application_id: 11071341
activity_code: F32
title: Manipulating Sex Hormone Signaling for Therapeutic Benefit in Liver Cancer
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is on track to surpass breast, prostate, and colorectal cancer to become the
third-leading cause of cancer death in the United States by 2040 but no highly effective therapies are available.
HCC-related deaths in men outnumber those in women, and sex hormone signaling through the androgen re-
ceptor (AR) is thought to be responsible. Here, we investigate the functional role of sex hormone signaling and
explore the therapeutic potential of AR inhibition in a novel genetically engineered mouse model of HCC.

application_id: 11168989
activity_code: K99
title: Revealing signal transduction mechanisms through protein design
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Signaling proteins like bacterial histidine kinases act as switches, sensing environmental stimuli and initiating a
cellular response through a series of conformational changes and enzymatic steps. For these predominant
receptors, we still lack significant understanding of how signals are propagated through the protein scaffold to
induce switch-like behavior. By design signaling components from scratch and engineering them into signaling
proteins, we can interrogate signaling mechanisms from first principles, bringing us closer to a molecular level
understanding how nature has evolved dynamic yet specific signaling pathways.

application_id: 11190902
activity_code: K99
title: Building Dendrite Architecture via Microtubule Nucleation
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
Just like the skeleton in our body, cells have a cytoskeleton, which provides a framework for their shape and
thus their function. The key cytoskeletal element, microtubules, are generated at precise locations and times in
order to build cellular structures. In this proposal, I will determine how MT nucleation is used to drive the formation
of two microtubule-based structures, the mitotic spindle and neuronal dendrites, which will provide the underlying
principles of microtubule self-organizing and how it gives rise to specialized cell architecture and function.

application_id: 11165968
activity_code: F32
title: Cannabidiol and buprenorphine: A poly-pharmacy approach to treat opioid use disorder
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Millions of Americans are diagnosed annually with an opioid use disorder and buprenorphine is the most widely
prescribed medication assisted treatment. Although larger buprenorphine doses can improve treatment
outcome, they can also produce adverse physiological effects, physical dependence, and discontinuation can
result in withdrawal and increase the risk of relapse. The present study will evaluate the potential of cannabidiol
as a safe, non-psychoactive, and non-addictive adjuvant treatment that enhances the effectiveness of smaller
doses of buprenorphine, which could ultimately improve treatment outcomes for thousands of opioid use disorder
patients.

application_id: 11165011
activity_code: F32
title: Specificity and stability of cocaine ensembles
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE.
Cocaine use disorder is characterized by a variety of symptoms, including an increase in the attribution of
value of drug-associated stimuli and a simultaneous decrease in the value of non-drug stimuli, thereby driving
drug-seeking behavior at the expense of non-drug alternatives. The goal of this proposal is to determine
whether cocaine & sucrose activate distinct neurons in the nucleus accumbens – a key brain region involved in
reward – and how long-term cocaine use alters activity in this region.

application_id: 11190871
activity_code: K99
title: Understanding eukaryotic proteasome assembly regulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it focuses on understanding the mechanisms that
regulate eukaryotic proteasome assembly, which is critical in many diseases. Once such knowledge is
established, there is the potential for a significant advance in new therapies for patients targeting the proteasome.
Thus, the current interdisciplinary proposal is relevant to the part of the NIH’s mission that pertains to reducing
illness.

title: Vestibular Ganglion Neurons:Heterogeneity and Molecular Regulation of Afferent Terminal Morphology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
The diversity of vestibular ganglion neurons and the molecules that drive the differences in the morphology
of their terminals onto type I and type II vestibular hair cells are yet to be elucidated. Here, I propose to utilize

utricle.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acinar-to-ductal metaplasia and pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia are precancerous lesions and risk factors for the development of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. title: Molecular Mechanisms of Neuronal Hyperactivity in Tuberous Sclerosis Complex
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is a neurogenetic disorder with high rates of epilepsy and autism spectrum disorder due to dysregulation of the mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway. We have preliminary data suggesting that the transcription factor, EGR1, is down-regulated downstream of mTOR dysregulation in certain types of pyramidal neurons with loss of TSC2. We propose to characterize the mechanisms and consequences of this interaction in human neurons derived from induced pluripotent stem cells from patients with TSC, which will provide novel insight into the pathways underlying neurological symptoms in TSC. !

title: Feasibility of Network Interventions to Facilitate Community Coalition Adoption of Evidence-Based Strategies for Child Obesity Prevention
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY BOSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Child obesity and overweight are leading preventable causes of many adulthood chronic diseases, including many types of cancers, ranking second only to tobacco use. The scientific premise of this proposal is to select, refine, and test network interventions aimed at increasing the adoption of evidence-based strategies among childhood obesity prevention coalitions serving communities with socioeconomic disadvantage. This study will help researchers and public health practitioners explore improving coalition uptake of evidence-based strategies, help coalition leaders maximize their organizing and mobilizing practices in childhood obesity prevention, and add to the public health research base on optimal knowledge and research translation.

title: Single cell multi-omics approaches in identifying driving cells and genes in pediatric MODS patients requiring ECMO support
org_name: HENRY FORD HEALTH + MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Multiple organ dysfunction syndromes (MODS) frequently occur in pediatric patients and severe MODS patients
needed Veno-Arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation support (ECMO) (label as ECMO patients) as a
palliating maneuver to recover their cardiopulmonary function. The major knowledge gap exists regarding the
cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying MODS deterioration for their advanced life support. The proposed
work will provide a comprehensive overview of cellular and molecular mechanism associated with deterioration
of MODS patients who may require ECMO support, which will be applicable for early diagnostic purposes.

title: Novel use of mobile phone big data to evaluate the effect of climatic and non-climatic shocks on human mobility and women's health outcomes in sub-Saharan Africa
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Modified Public Health Relevance Section

Exposure to environmental variability has dramatic impacts on human health. This research uses novel mobility data derived from mobile phones, georeferenced environmental and contextual data (e.g., weather extremes, natural disasters, and armed conflict), and population-representative health data to assess the impact of these environmental exposures and changes in mobility patterns on women’s risk of intimate partner violence in Kenya. application_id: 10906154
activity_code: K08
title: Probing the Molecular Mechanisms of Diastolic Dysfunction Using Patient-Specific Stem Cells
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE Impaired relaxation of the heart, termed diastolic dysfunction, is a severe condition leading to significant morbidity and mortality with limited options for treatment. Restrictive Cardiomyopathy is a cardiac disease with particularly severe diastolic dysfunction and poor outcomes, for which there are no specific treatments because the underlying mechanisms are poorly understood. In this proposal, I use novel stem-cell models of Restrictive Cardiomyopathy and large-scale functional genomics analyses to define pathogenic mechanisms that will inform the design of therapeutics for this deadly disease.

application_id: 10903910
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the role of nuclear mechanics in the regulation of chromatin structure and embryonic cell fate
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
The first cell differentiation in mammals produces two cell types, the inner cell mass (ICM) and the
trophectoderm (TE), which give rise to the embryo proper and the placenta, respectively.
Investigating the mechanisms that regulate the specification of these cell types is therefore critical
for understanding early pregnancy and the factors that contribute to infertility. In this proposal I
investigate how mechanical properties of the nucleus contribute to the specification of the ICM and
TE, shedding light on this important early developmental period, and having broader implications for
generally understanding cellular transitions during development.

title: Adrenomedullin 2 Regulates ILC2 Responses to Control Intestinal Inflammation

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Early child development interventions in low-resource global settings usually include a focus on responsive
caregiving or early learning and nutrition, and most evaluate the effects on short-term outcomes in early
childhood. The proposed work will fill current research gaps by identifying mechanisms through which early
interventions impact health and development outcomes, assessing whether these effects persist into
adolescence, and estimating the potential impact of multicomponent interventions that target multiple prevalent
risk factors in early life simultaneously. This work will contribute to the optimal design of interventions to
improve health and development through childhood and adolescence in low-resource global settings.

application_id: 11165820
activity_code: F32
title: Neural mechanisms underlying the prioritization of protein-rich foods during protein need states.
org_name: LSU PENNINGTON BIOMEDICAL RESEARCH CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Our project aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms that drive the prioritization of protein-rich
foods under protein restriction, a critical adaptive response that directly influences metabolic
health. We will investigate how the brain distinguishes and values protein foods by evaluating
the importance of vagal transmission and FGF21 signaling in protein preference learning and by
mapping neural circuits in the brain that connects dopamine neurons and beta-klotho, FGF21
co-receptor, expressing neurons. This research will close the gap in our understanding of
macronutrient preferences, which has predominantly focused on fats and carbohydrates,
thereby enhancing our insight into the complex decision-making process behind food choices.

application_id: 10866292
activity_code: K99
title: Multiscale Experimental and Computational Methods to Characterize Hormonal and Mechanical Contributions to Pregnancy-Induced Remodeling of Skeletal Muscle
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative Vaginal childbirth is a leading risk factor for morbid conditions that affect a quarter of U.S. women, referred to collectively as pelvic floor disorders. Vaginal birth injury to the pelvic floor muscles is the posited link between vaginal delivery and pelvic floor disorders, thus, this proposal will determine how pregnancy-induced remodeling prepares these skeletal muscles for the mechanically demanding task of vaginal childbirth. Multiscale computational and experimental methods will elucidate the hormonal and mechanical drivers of pelvic floor muscle alterations in pregnacy that impact whole muscle active and passive mechanical function.

application_id: 10920482
activity_code: K99
title: Neuroanatomically informed biomarker discovery and neurofeedback intervention for OCD
org_name: MCLEAN HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Neural connections mapped in the macaque brain can improve our understanding of the brain circuits involved
in avoidance symptoms, such as those observed in obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). This project will
combine macaque neuroanatomy with neuroimaging data in humans to describe circuitry-level biomarkers for
these symptoms. Later, during a new neuromodulation protocol, OCD patients will receive training to self-
modulate the local activity within these biomarkers while receiving real-time feedback.

application_id: 11166389
activity_code: K08
title: Mapping and modeling inhibitory interneurons in Alzheimer's disease and related dementias: novel strategies to reverse neural network dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and AD-related dementias (AD/ADRD) are a major public health
concern, costing over $321 Billion and affecting more than 6.5 million individuals in the United
States alone, and these numbers will continue to grow with our aging population. The exact
causes of AD/ADRD are unknown, although an important component involves dysfunction of
regulatory brain cells, called inhibitory interneurons, and results in over-excitation and abnormal
activity in brain circuits that serve memory and cognition. In our planned research, we aim to
define the causes for inhibitory interneuron dysfunction and find ways to reverse this problem,
advancing the mission of the NIH to decrease illness, reduce disability, and prolong life for
AD/ADRD patients.

activity_code: F32
title: Comparative safety of insulin glargine-yfgn (a biosimilar insulin) in a real-world population with novel pharmacoepidemiology methods: prevalent new-user study and clone-censored weight approach
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Insulin glargine-fygn (Semglee), a biosimilar insulin, offers a solution to overcome the burden of high-cost
insulin. However, the randomized trials used to support the approval of Semglee were small in sample size,
short in duration, and lacked diversity. This study will provide critical real-world evidence to support the use of
Semglee as a safe, effective, and affordable alternative to the originator-biologic insulin glargine (Lantus) with
novel pharmacoepidemiology methods.

title: Characterization and Optimization of a Novel Bioreactor for Biomanufacturing and Cryopreservation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Liver transplantation remains the only treatment for severe liver dysfunction—the 10th leading cause of death
in the United States annually—but a critical shortage of organ donors severely limits how many patients can
receive a transplant. The ability to bioengineer functional liver tissue to treat severe liver dysfunction can
significantly decrease the problem of donor shortage and address a critical unmet need in modern-day
medicine. By improving the ability of our customized technology to bioengineer and cryopreserve liver tissue,
our proposed work could drastically increase the availability of bioengineered tissues to researchers and
healthcare facilities around the world.

title: The world’s smallest vertebrate brain: A new model for nervous system function and regeneration research
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: Project Narrative
Studies focusing on regeneration within the nervous system hold great potential for the development of
therapeutic approaches that aim to generate cures for central nervous system injury and degeneration. In this
project, I aim to establish the emerging model Danionella cerebrum – one of the world's smallest vertebrates
and a member of a clade of vertebrates (teleost fish) that can regenerate after brain injury – as a novel vertebrate
model system for nervous system function, and regeneration research. Combining single-cell RNA sequencing,
cell-type-specific ablations, and live calcium imaging will allow study of regeneration in the central and peripheral
nervous system, and reveal principles concerning the functional and structural basis of regeneration in specific
neura...

application_id: 11173686
activity_code: K08
title: Role of Endothelin-1 signaling in the development and progression of atrial fibrillation
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained arrhythmia, and is the cause of significant morbidity and
mortality. This proposal aims to elucidate the role of endothelial dysfunction - a prominent feature of multiple
risk factors for atrial fibrillation - in the development of an arrhythmogenic substrate leading to atrial fibrillation,
which will allow the emergence of mechanism-guided therapies. Given the rapidly increasing prevalence of
atrial fibrillation and the limited efficacy of current therapies for this disease, it is in the interest of public health
to better understand the molecular mechanisms leading to atrial fibrillation.

title: Renal Circadian Clock Protein Regulation of Immune Activation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Inflammation is a common factor among individuals with kidney disease, a major contributor of death in the US. Many aspects of kidney function are linked to the circadian clock, however, its contribution to inflammation is not well known. This project intends to define the role of the circadian clock in activation of renal inflammation, thus identifying novel targets that may be used for treatment to improve the quality of life in individuals with kidney disease.

application_id: 11174273
activity_code: K08
title: Racial Stigma and Substance Use Vulnerability Among African American Young Adults: Examining Cognitive and Affective Mechanisms
org_name: UNIV OF MARYLAND, COLLEGE PARK
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The current application aims to address health disparities experienced by African Americans—
established as a central public health priority—through an examination of racial stigma
experiences which have been found to contribute to substance use vulnerability. Substantial
information about stress, executive functioning, and emotional processing has emerged in the
field of cognitive neuroscience and there are exciting new opportunities to assess these types of
processes underlying responses to racial stigma. Deeper understanding of these core
processes will offer important new information and form a clear basis from which to develop
targeted prevention/intervention strategies.

application_id: 11174411
activity_code: K08
title: The Impact of Anesthetics on Lymphatic Vessel Contractility and Extracellular Fluid Uptake
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
The lymphatic system is essential for regulating fluid and maintaining tissue water balance, and its dysfunction
can result in significant edema. Commonly used anesthetics may impact the lymphatic system and contribute to
dangerous edema in critical illness, but this remains largely unexplored. The goal of this research project is to
understand how anesthetic medications interact with the lymphatic system, with the potential to guide safer
medication choices and improve patient outcomes in surgeries and intensive care settings.

title: Mechanism of Eph-ephrin Signaling in Intestinal Regeneration
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) affects approximately 3 million Americans and costs the health care system
more than 20 billion dollars annually. Despite recent advances in biological therapy, mortality rate of IBD remains
high at 1.8% annually. The aim of this study is to better understand how cells communicate with each other
during intestinal healing with the hope to use this knowledge to help devise better treatments and improve the
outcomes of IBD patients and reduce the cost of IBD on our healthcare system.

title: Assessing for and Intervening upon Early Childhood Violence Exposure in Pediatric Primary Care Settings
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Childhood exposure to violence (CEV) in early childhood is a significant and preventable public health problem
in the United States, and pediatric healthcare settings are centrally positioned to identify and intervene upon
early CEV. The proposed research aims to (1) understand existing CEV assessment tools and follow-up
processes in pediatric primary care settings; (2) identify facilitators of and barriers to routine CEV assessment in
these settings; (3) characterize patterns of early CEV assessment across one large medical system; and (4)
determine whether these assessments are associated with subsequent healthcare referral, follow-up care
receipt, and child health outcomes. Findings will provide translatable information for primary care settings
implementing the recent...

activity_code: F32
title: Molecular and Circuit Analyses of Color Discrimination without Opsins
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Environmental light influences daily behaviors, such as navigation and feeding, through both visual and non-
visual pathways in animals. In humans, light-processing systems engage complex neural networks, and
dysfunctions in these systems can lead to conditions like blindness or disrupted sleep. Understanding
generalizable molecular and neural mechanisms involved in light sensing will improve our knowledge of both
human health issues and fundamental principles of sensorimotor processing.

title: Dynamics and mechanisms of long-range enhancer activity
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Enhancers are cis-regulatory elements that control precise spatiotemporal gene expression in a cell-type-specific
manner. Despite being discovered over 40 years ago, the fundamental mechanisms of enhancer activity are still
not well understood. Here, we propose to study the mechanisms by which cell-type-specific enhancers activate
genes over long distances using MYC as a model locus.

title: Early Events in Extravillous Trophoblast Cell Differentiation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER

Impaired extravillous trophoblast (EVT) cell development leads to early loss of pregnancy, a

application_id: 11196219
activity_code: K99
title: Studying Protein Affinity to DNA by in vitro Transcription and Sequencing (PADIT-seq)
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Genes, which contain instructions for making all proteins in the body, are regulated by sequence-specific DNA
interactions by transcription factors (TF). Current methods to detect TF-DNA interactions are biased for only the
highest affinity interactions when medium-low affinity interactions are very important and more common. I have
developed PADIT-seq, an improved technology to detect TF-DNA interactions across the entire spectrum of
affinities. The overall goal of this proposal is to utilize and further develop PADIT-seq to uncover fundamental
TF-DNA binding principles, which will significantly advance our mechanistic understanding of the principles
governing gene regulation and genetic risk factors for complex diseases.

title: Mechanisms of platelet stimulation in hemolysis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE High-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) has a recognized role in platelet activation and thrombosis formation, but its mechanism of platelet activation in sickle cell disease remains unclear. I propose that circulating hemoglobin oxidizes HMGB1 to trigger an intracellular platelet signaling cascade that potentiates platelet mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (mtROS) and platelet activation. Investigating this novel mechanism of platelet activation will improve our understanding of the mechanisms linking hemolysis and inflammation to platelet activation and thrombosis, and establish a foundation for developing and applying HMGB1-inhibitors to prevent thrombotic complications in sickle cell disease.

title: IL1 as a Driver of Mucosal Immune Dysregulation in Inflammatory Bowel Disease
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative This project seeks to characterize interleukin-1 (IL1)-related inflammatory signatures in the blood and intestinal tissue of patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), interleukin-10 receptor (IL10R) deficiency, and in healthy controls, using a combination of proteomic and transcriptomic single cell approaches. application_id: 10783904
activity_code: K99
title: Regulation of exon junction complex composition and assembly
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Nonsense mediated mRNA decay (NMD) promotes the normal gene expression of unmutated messenger RNAs (mRNAs) and destroys mutated mRNAs that produce potential disease-causing proteins. The exon junction complex (EJC) is one key determinant of whether an mRNA undergoes NMD and contains the NMD factor UPF2 or the central cell-signaling protein AKT, the latter of which is promoted by insulin. This work will build upon this recent finding by defining the molecular mechanisms involved in this process, which is important because NMD and AKT signaling are both dysregulated in human diseases like Fragile X Syndrome, some cancers, and type 1 and type 2 diabetes.

application_id: 11244134
activity_code: F32
title: Use of de novo Protein Design Methods to Rationally Redesign P450 Enzymes for New Cofactors and Substrates
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Artificial metalloenzymes are increasingly powerful catalysts for the enantioselective synthesis of biologically
active molecules relevant to human health, but intermolecular, regioselective reactions catalyzed by such
enzymes remain challenging to achieve. I proposed to use de novo protein design methods to redesign
cytochrome P450 enzymes to accommodate unnatural cofactors and large substrates. My new approach for the
development of artificial metalloenzymes will combine the flexibility of de novo protein design with the dynamic,
evolved active site of P450 enzymes to achieve intermolecular nitrene and carbene group-transfer reactions.

title: Advancing Epigenetic Sequencing Through Solid-Phase Enzymatic Approaches
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Chemical modifications to DNA impact biological function, and when they deviate from the norm, it can lead to disease. This study aims to develop biotechnological methods to precisely map these epigenetic DNA modifications under challenging scenarios, when either sample amount is limited or diverse modifications within a sample are clustered. These methods will advance our understanding of developmental biology and lay the groundwork for future applications in the study of disease progression.

application_id: 10784256
activity_code: K08
title: Etiology of naturally-occurring anti-ABO antibodies
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative: ABO(H) blood group antigens were the first human polymorphisms described and corresponding anti-ABO(H) antibodies continue to be the most common immunological barrier to transfusion and transplantation. However, despite their discovery over a century ago, the factors responsible for anti-ABO(H) antibody development remain relatively unknown. Using a series translational studies coupled with a new preclinical model of anti-blood group antibody formation, I will leverage my existing training in transfusion immunology with training in glycobiology to define key factors that influence the overall levels and specificity of anti-ABO(H) antibodies.

title: Molecular basis to regulate the cardiac-specific form of mitochondrial phospholipid cardiolipin
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Mitochondrial specific phospholipid cardiolipin plays many crucial roles in bioenergetics. In the heart, linoleic acid

activity_code: K99
title: Mechanisms linking the frail sarcomere to noncompaction cardiomyopathy
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Genetic changes in the single gene, MYH7, which encodes the major motor protein in the heart, cause a variety
of human heart diseases, called cardiomyopathies. Up to 1/250 individuals carries a genetic change in this gene.
Through this work, we will detail why specific genetic defects cause a specific type of developmental heart
disease which manifest as cardiomyopathies, and this information will help identify and reduce disease risk.

title: Uncovering the Impact of Innate Immunity on Transfusion Medicine Immunobiology
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative: ABO(H) blood group antigens were the first human polymorphisms described and corresponding anti-ABO(H) antibodies continue to be the most common immunological barrier to transfusion and transplantation. application_id: 11241759
activity_code: F32
title: Light-Driven Deracemization of Atropisomers Enabled by Chiral Photocatalysts
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The development of new disease-treating compounds that possess high potency and low toxicity requires
synthetic strategies to rapidly create rigid molecular scaffolds that fix the position of substituents in space to
selectively target complex proteins. This proposal comprises an approach to access to conformationally rigid
atropisomers found in medicinally relevant molecules by leveraging the native functional groups found in these
motifs to facilitate deracemization of readily generated racemates. Employing two classes of newly designed
chiral photocatalysts, deracemization of numerous atropisomers can be achieved by enantioselective electron
or energy transfer to selectively convert one enantiomer into the other.

application_id: 11369488
activity_code: DP1
title: Integrative approaches to dissection of endocrine communication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative
 Endocrine interactions have been demonstrated as causal and contributory to most common, most
notably obesity, type 2 diabetes and resulting cardiovascular complications. The goal of this proposal is to
closely bridge computational and experimental methods to systematically dissect mechanisms by which
tissues communicate and how these interactions are perturbed in disease settings. Collectively, we will begin
with newly developed and unbiased computational approaches which survey population data, validate using
high-throughput in vitro assays and evaluate therapeutic potential of new endocrine interactions using mouse
models of metabolic disease.

title: Molecular mechanisms underlying functional evolution of bioluminescent proteins from haloalkane dehalogenases
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Bioluminescent proteins are widely used in biological research as molecular reporters for in vitro gene

title: Integrated Program in Endocrinology Translational Postdoctoral Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Narrative: Research scientists focused on the fields of pregnancy and reproduction are critically important in

application_id: 10985750
activity_code: K99
title: WNK1-dependent macropinocytosis mediates emergency myelopoiesis
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Emergency hematopoiesis induces the rapid expansion of inflammatory neutrophils during infection, a process which must remain tightly regulated as the overproduction of hyperactivated granulocytes underlies the disease progression of systemic inflammation into septic shock. Therefore, myeloid progenitor fate during emergency granulopoiesis must be carefully controlled at a molecular, cellular, and systemic level. This proposal aims to examine the machinery that regulates the dramatic shift in myelopoiesis towards emergency neutropoiesis, with the goal of targeting this pathway to reduce the adverse effects of an over-activated immune system during septic shock.

application_id: 10784461
activity_code: K99
title: Aminopeptidase N Driven Signaling in the Development of Pulmonary Hypertension
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pulmonary Hypertension (PH) is a lethal disease with no effective treatment due to a limited understanding of the underlying mechanisms that cause extreme vascular proliferation and remodeling in the pulmonary circulation. Our data indicate that mitochondrial dysfunction leads to increased expression of Aminopeptidase N in vascular cells - pericytes, causing them to proliferate and contribute to severe vascular remodeling in PH. This discovery has crucial clinical significance, potentially leading to new treatments targeting excessive vascular proliferation.

application_id: 10784393
activity_code: K99
title: The role of AHR in modulatingimmunotherapy response and adverse events in bladder cancer
org_name: VAN ANDEL RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE A subgroup of bladder cancer patients who are treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) develop and succumb to hyperproliferative immune-related adverse events (HP-irAEs), for reasons that are unknown. I discovered a genetic and metabolic signature for HP-irAE that seems to pre-condition the tumor environment for HP-irAE before ICI treatment. By understanding how the genetic and metabolic pathway is differentially activated in tumor and immune cells within the tumor microenvironment, we will be able to better stratify bladder cancer patients before ICI treatment, and identify new opportunities for future drug discovery and precision medicine efforts to minimize HP-irAE in ICI-treated patients.

activity_code: F32
title: Leveraging machine learning and EMA data to identify factors that predict children's energy intake
org_name: LSU PENNINGTON BIOMEDICAL RESEARCH CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A better understanding of children’s eating behavior will lead to the development of innovative intervention
strategies for preventing childhood obesity. The current F32 application is designed to employ machine
learning techniques to identify factors that predict children’s eating behaviors based on ecological momentary
assessment (EMA) data. The findings of the proposed research have the potential to inform personalized
interventions that integrate EMA data and provide real-time feedback to promote healthy eating and prevent
childhood obesity.

application_id: 11171470
activity_code: K99
title: Functions and Mechanisms of Lung Microfold cells during Invasive Pneumococcal Disease
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Influenza-associated secondary pneumococcal infections are dreaded complications that cause serious
illnesses and significantly contribute to Influenza related mortality. Highly endocytic Microfold cells arise in
the lower respiratory epithelium during the resolving phase of Influenza linked inflammation when
susceptibility to secondary pneumococcal pneumonia is highest. This proposal will unravel the pathologic
role of lung Microfold cells as entry portals for pneumococcal invasion and will have the potential to develop
novel therapeutic avenues for targeting virus-associated secondary infections.

application_id: 11068082
activity_code: F32
title: Understanding the relationship between sleep, motor learning, and motor development in early childhood
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MASSACHUSETTS AMHERST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The acute and chronic impact of sleep on motor learning and motor development in early childhood is
foundational for long-term health and behavioral outcomes and may be a target for intervention, but no studies
have yet examined their interaction through a cohesive framework. The proposed research is tailored to serve
as the initial stepping stone, bridging this gap through dual aims of studying the relationship experimentally and
longitudinally. The accompanying training plan will prepare me to forge my independent line of inquiry, expanding
the study of sleep, motor learning, and motor development to younger populations, and build on its implications

title: The role of proteoglycans in Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: Implications for pathophysiology, diagnosis and treatment
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is a thrombotic disorder that develops when patients anti-coagulated with heparin develop anti-PF4 antibodies that stimulate platelet activation and thrombosis; however, the platelet surface molecules that recruit PF4 to the platelet surface to elicit the epitopes recognized by anti-PF4 antibodies remain unknown. This paucity of knowledge has resulted in the low accuracy of frontline diagnostic assays and ineffectual treatment options for HIT patients. application_id: 10906234
activity_code: F99
title: Mapping p53 dynamics to cell-fate outcomes in reprogramming and oncogenesis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative The objective of this proposal is to understand the role of the tumor suppressor protein p53 in aiding cell fate transitions including cancer. As p53 mutants dominate the mutational landscape of cancer, it is important to understand what functions of normal p53 are co-opted and enhanced by mutants to promote cell fate transitions in order to establish effective therapies that target cells harboring these oncogenes. By studying the role of p53 and p53 mutants during transitions, this work will shed light on early-stage cancers, aiding early- detection of the disease and improving patient outcomes.

application_id: 11242121
activity_code: F32
title: Amniotic fluid entrains embryo-wide branching morphogenesis
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Low levels of amniotic fluid, a condition known as oligohydramnios, results in underdeveloped
lungs and kidneys in humans and mice. The lung, kidney, and salivary gland have tree-like
epithelium, which is formed through a developmental process called branching morphogenesis.
Here, I will combine mouse genetics with state-of-the-art microscopy and novel microfluidic
devices to directly test whether amniotic fluid couples organ development across the embryo.

title: Uncovering the role of Importin9 in Hedgehog signaling
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The Hedgehog pathway is a critical signaling pathway involved in vertebrate development and adult tissue
homeostasis with defects resulting in numerous developmental pathologies. Despite its strong genetic

application_id: 11171386
activity_code: K99
title: Metasurface Optical Guidewire for percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Our research project aims to improve the treatment of coronary artery calcification (CAC)
by developing metasurface optical guidewires, which have the potential to revolutionize
percutaneous coronary interventions (PCIs). Through precise control of laser dosimetry and the
use of laser intravascular lithotripsy (L-IVL), these novel devices could significantly enhance the
treatment of calcified stenotic arteries. Successful development and testing of novel metasuface
optical guidewires will allow conducting PCI in highly stenotic and calcified arteries to provide
therapy and increase vessel compliance.

application_id: 11134668
activity_code: K99
title: Complement in the cellular microenvironment during cardiac renewal
org_name: TEXAS HEART INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Heart attacks result in permanent loss of cardiac muscle cells, leading to increased morbidity and
mortality with limited treatment options. To regenerate cardiac muscle cells, special populations
of fibroblasts and immune cells are required, yet their precise functions and contributions to
cardiac renewal are not well understood. In support of the NIH mission to promote better treatment
and prevention strategies, my research project will elucidate the interplay between these non-
muscle cells and the cardiac muscle cells, which may lead to novel therapeutics promoting cardiac
renewal.

activity_code: F31
title: Cognition in Essential Tremor: A Neuroimaging and Biomarker Study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Essential tremor is a common neurological disorder that has been linked to an increased risk of developing dementia. A large gap remains in the understanding of neuroanatomical correlates of cognitive changes in essential tremor, particularly whether they represent cerebellar dysfunction alone or potentially more widespread neurological processes, such as those seen in age-related neurodegenerative disorders like Alzheimer’s disease. The proposed study will examine biomarkers (i.e., neuroimaging, plasma) in individuals with essential tremor in an effort to better understand the link between essential tremor and cognitive dysfunction.

application_id: 11171494
activity_code: K99
title: Multi-omic investigation of the adiposity-lipid intersection and its role in cardiovascular disease risk
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
Anti-obesity medications designed to reduce excess body fat or “adiposity” contribute to weight loss and
substantially reduce the risk of heart disease. The genetic impact of adiposity on lipid levels—both critical risk
factors for heart disease—remains uncertain but holds considerable clinical and therapeutic potential. This
proposal will leverage comprehensive clinical and multi-omics data from large-scale cohorts to uncover key
biological components at the intersection of adiposity and lipid pathways, potentially advancing heart disease
risk assessment and identifying new therapeutic targets for individuals with obesity.

application_id: 11160552
activity_code: K99
title: Post-transcriptional mechanisms of direct cardiac reprogramming
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Heart failure affects about 5.7 million adults in the United States and continues to be the leading cause of death
globally, partly due to the limited therapeutic options. Direct cardiac reprogramming, which converts non-
myocytes in the heart to induced cardiomyocytes, offers a promising alternative strategy for repairing the heart.
This proposal will use state-of-art approaches to delineate the post-transcriptional mechanisms of this process
for its future clinical implementation.

title: Human genetic supplementation without donor DNA or a DNA break
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: NARRATIVE Human genome engineering has widely anticipated promise as a healthcare strategy, but technologies in development are unlikely to safely or efficiently introduce a gene into the human genome. Starkly missing from transgene introduction strategies is an approach based on what is already quite successful across eukaryotic evolution: gene insertion by synthesis of an RNA-templated complementary DNA (cDNA). title: Whole chromosome fine-mapping integrating diverse ancestries for psychiatric disorders
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Statistical fine-mapping identifies a handful of putative causal variants from hundreds of GWAS loci for
psychiatric disorders, but due to the unavailability of methods, current findings are primarily based on analysis
of the European cohort, leading to bias in causal genetic variant discovery and limiting the resolution of findings.
This application proposes to develop novel statistical fine-mapping methods for admixed populations and the
gene-based Neanderthal introgression burden and performs the most inclusive fine-mapping analysis on
psychiatric disorders. By integrating genomic data across ancestries, this work will create a comprehensive list
of fine-mapped variants/genes for psychiatric disorders, which serves as a valuable resource for experimentalists
and clinic...

application_id: 10924052
activity_code: K99
title: Anxiety in Youth with Autism Spectrum Disorder
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Untreated anxiety disorders among youth with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) carries tremendous personal and
societal costs including serious behavioral problems, failure of occupational or residential placement, and
decreased independence. Progress towards the development of interventions that reduce anxiety severity in this
population has been limited by difficulties in accurately measuring anxiety severity due to the differences in social
communication that are core to ASD. This study will develop objective methods to detect physiological measures
of anxiety which will be incorporated into a clinical trial assessing buspirone for the treatment of anxiety in youth
with ASD.

application_id: 10985545
activity_code: K99
title: Mapping Neural Dynamics in Hormone-sensitive Networks
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Gonadal hormones profoundly influence social behavior and many psychiatric disorders associated with social dysfunction, such as depression and anxiety disorders; some social behaviors, such as self-initiated (“proactive”) behaviors can promote resiliency to these disorders. Despite this, we lack a clear understanding of how gonadal hormones shape network scale neural coding of the social environment and proactive behavior at a moment-to- moment level. Here, I develop new technology to probe the effect of hormonal change on “proactive” social behavior and its encoding by a multi-brain region network of neural circuits, building an experimental framework for future studies on the relationship between hormones, self-initiated behavior, and resilience.

application_id: 11013311
activity_code: F30
title: Harnessing Amide Linkers for Antitubercular Drug Development
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Novel drugs for the treatment of tuberculosis that are capable of shortening treatment duration, treating drug- resistant infection, and preventing the rise of further resistance are urgently needed. Our proposed research will harness Mycobacterium tuberculosis amidases to activate amide prodrugs, thereby identifying prodrugs that are activated by several amidases and yielding novel, pyrazinoic acid-releasing amides that can restore pyrazinamide-like activity in pyrazinamide-resistant strains. In doing so, this work will demonstrate a novel approach to drug discovery that enables the design of prodrugs that can treat drug-resistant disease and have extremely high barriers to resistance.

title: Characterizing early signs of neurobehavioral dysregulation in infants at increased risk for behavioral disorders
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The proposed K99 study will leverage integrative data analysis to identify potential risk mechanisms (attention, motor, and sleep behavior) associated with behavior dysregulation in infants exposed to nicotine in utero and in unexposed infants. The R00 study will expand on this foundational knowledge to longitudinally characterize attention, motor, and sleep behavior earlier in development—across the first postnatal year—associated with parent-reported behavior dysregulation at 1-year of age, using sensitive, automated, and scalable measures. title: Training Program in Investigative Rheumatology
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Rheumatologic disorders are an increasing cause of morbidity and mortality due both to a rising incidence of autoimmunity and to our aging population. application_id: 11189645
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Type I Interferons in Factor VIII Inhibitor Formation
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Antibodies against factor VIII (inhibitors) occur in 20-30% of patients with severe hemophilia A following life-
saving factor VIII replacement therapy, rendering treatment less effective, with no current therapies available to
prevent their formation. Type I interferons are cytokines that play a role in the initiating immune steps of inhibitor
development and are an attractive potential therapeutic target for decreasing inhibitor formation and/or predicting
those patients at risk of developing inhibitors. The ability to prevent or predict inhibitor development will reduce
the public health burden associated with the increased morbidity/mortality, decreased quality of life, and
significant expense associated with the treatment of hemophilia A patients with inhibitors.
.

activity_code: F32
title: Conformational mechanisms underlying allosteric regulation of the human serotonin transporter
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Dysregulation of the human serotonin transporter (hSERT) contributes to disorders such as anxiety, depression,
and irritable bowel syndrome. Despite intensive study, critical aspects of hSERT regulation—particularly how
endogenous and exogenous allosteric ligands modulate transporter function—remain unclear, limiting our
understanding of their roles in disease. This proposal aims to define the structural and functional basis of
allosteric regulation in hSERT, providing foundational knowledge to support the development of novel therapies
for serotonin-related disorders and to advance our broader understanding of neurotransmitter transporter
biology.

title: Identifying transcriptomic axes governing neural dynamics, behavior, and states
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Environmental inputs are dynamically processed by a network of functionally distinct cell types organized into
specific brain circuits to generate purposeful motor actions. While it is evident that physiological requirements
significantly influence the selection of behavioral responses, the precise mechanisms through which internal
states shape these innate behaviors remain poorly characterized due to lack of methodological and
conceptual frameworks to integrate molecular, circuit and behavioral data into a coherent model. This
proposal aims to understand state-specific innate and social behavior using a combination of spatial
transcriptomic, functional imaging, and behavioral methods.

application_id: 10821907
activity_code: F31
title: RNA splicing regulation of lipid metabolism and longevity in C. elegans
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Manipulation of TORC1 signaling extends lifespan and increases disease resistance but has pleiotropic effects making it critical to understand novel downstream mediators, like pre-mRNA splicing, to better target interventions. The goal of this project is to investigate the mechanisms by which pre-mRNA splicing regulates TORC1 downstream processes including lipid metabolism and the causality for longevity. This work will expand our understanding of RNA homeostasis as a novel paradigm in aging and physiology and ultimately serve as a basis for the design of interventions to promote healthy aging and disease resistance.

application_id: 11110869
activity_code: F32
title: Exploring the role of hippocampal hypervascularization in Rett syndrome pathogenesis
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Rett syndrome, a severe neurological disorder driven by mutations in MECP2, features severe intractable epileptic seizures. My recent experiments have revealed increased vascular density and branching in the hippocampus of Rett mice, a phenotype which has been shown to contribute to pathology in mouse models of temporal lobe epilepsy. The proposed study aims to explore the molecular origins and pathological consequences of aberrant hippocampal vascularization in Rett mice, focusing on the potential role of VEGF signaling and its impact on epileptogenesis.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
An evolutionary expansion of the cortex, in particular corticospinal neurons, has long been hypothesized to
underlie the exceptional dexterity of primates, but it is unclear how this expansion affects neural circuits and
whether there is a causal link between corticospinal neuron number and dexterous behavior. Deer mice
(Peromyscus maniculatus) are a uniquely advantageous system in which to address this problem as an
experimentally tractable system with innate natural variation in motor skill that correlates with variation in
corticospinal neuron number. This proposal thus aims to use deer mice to test the hypothesis that the
expansion of specific corticospinal neuron populations results in changes in neural activity and dexterous

application_id: 11212371
activity_code: F32
title: 3D Printing Hydrogel Vasculature and Bioactive Cues within Brain Organoids
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research project aims to address key limitations of brain organoids by developing a bioengineered
solution to improve their growth, maturation, and reproducibility. By optimizing a 3D printed synthetic hydrogel
scaffold with hollow vascular networks and bioactive mechanical and peptide signaling cues, this project seeks
to create brain organoids that more accurately reflect human brain complexity, allowing for the study of
neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative diseases. The long-term impact of this research could lead to
better in vitro models for drug screening and therapeutic development, ultimately improving treatments for
brain-related disorders.

application_id: 11049192
activity_code: K99
title: Network dysfunction and impaired consciousness in epilepsy
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Impaired consciousness in epilepsy has a significant impact on patient safety, productivity, physical and
psychological health. We will investigate ictal and interictal network dysfunction and their association, and
develop a clinical tool to predict impaired consciousness during seizures from EEG without the need for
behavioral testing. This study will provide an improved understanding of brain network abnormalities in
epilepsy and will provide approaches that can significantly improve patient quality of life by facilitating accurate
diagnosis, decisions about medication adjustment, and presurgical evaluation as well as allowing clinicians to
precisely evaluate the ability of patients to complete daily-life activities such as driving.

application_id: 11230235
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of a therapeutic DNA vaccine targeting Mycobacterium tuberculosis persisters
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
We developed a novel therapeutic tuberculosis (TB) vaccine that potentiates the efficacy of the traditional,
prolonged TB treatment. With the mechanistic studies described in this proposal, we will dissect the multifaceted
immune response to TB, offering shorter treatment and alternative options to standard TB treatment when
adverse drug reactions, resistance, recurrence, or treatment failure complicate clinical management, especially
in high-risk populations, including patients living with HIV. Understanding how our TB therapeutic vaccine works
can serve as proof-of-concept for targeting other key TB-related enzymes and treating other chronic infections.

application_id: 11281419
activity_code: F32
title: Interactions Between Motor Cortex and Dorsolateral Striatum in a Model of Naturalistic Motor Behavior
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative
Daily behaviors rely on a network of brain regions to work together to sequence and execute a series of
movements. This project will uncover how the motor cortex and dorsolateral striatum interact to control natural
behaviors by integrating computational modeling with high-density neural recordings. By revealing fundamental
principles of motor control, this research will bridge the gap between theory and experiment, providing critical
insights that could drive new treatments for movement disorders such as Parkinson’s and Huntington’s disease.

application_id: 10984598
activity_code: K99
title: Transcriptional regulation of oligodendroglial differentiation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) are the most abundantly mitotic cell in the brain and give rise to oligodendrocytes responsible for myelination through the process of OPC differentiation, which is critical to neurodevelopment and maintenance of myelination, yet the processes mediating this constancy are unclear. Oligodendroglia dynamics require the precise timing of transcription factors expression, that is essential for efficiently remyelinating lesions. The proposed research will determine genetic mechanisms through which the main circadian factor BMAL1 and other key transcription factors control OPC differentiation during myelination, providing critical insights into both normal and aberrant myelination associated with the demyelinating neurological disorder multiple sclerosis.

application_id: 10864599
activity_code: K99
title: Mechanisms of activity-dependent myelination through oligodendrocyte exocytosis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Activity-dependent myelination is an emerging facet of neuroplasticity in which neuronal activity stimulates new myelin formation by oligodendrocytes. Although numerous studies have demonstrated the importance of activity-dependent myelination for learning, cognition, and memory, how neuronal activity drives myelin membrane expansion in oligodendrocytes is unclear. My proposed research will reveal fundamental cellular mechanisms of activity-dependent myelination that may be harnessed as therapeutic strategies to promote myelin regeneration and neuronal health in neurological disease and disorders.

application_id: 11232355
activity_code: K08
title: Uncovering Epigenetic and Transcriptional Drivers of Neuroendocrine Plasticity at Single-Cell Level in Patients with Small Cell Lung Cancer Transformation
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Histological transformation from lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) to small cell lung cancer (SCLC)
and subsequent diversification into more aggressive SCLC subtypes, are examples of
neuroendocrine lineage plasticity, a process that promotes metastasis and treatment resistance
in lung cancer. This proposal uses 1) single-cell sequencing in patient tumors of combined
LUAD/SCLC histology and 2) functional experiments in preclinical models of SCLC transformation
to identify transcriptomic and epigenetic changes driving lineage plasticity. These molecular
drivers may identify a novel class of drug targets that ablate lineage plasticity, with therapeutic
implications in not just lung cancer but also any cancer type where small cell transformation may
arise.

application_id: 11067763
activity_code: K99
title: Hippocampal Mechanisms of Episodic Memory Recall and their Contribution to Naturalistic Navigation Decisions
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Episodic memory is central to the human sense of self and wellbeing, and its disruption in Alzheimer’s disease
or other forms of dementia profoundly reduces quality of life. This work contributes to an understanding of the
fundamental mechanisms, shared across vertebrate species, by which the hippocampus supports episodic
memory function. Results will have broad scientific relevance for the development of future technology to improve
memory function in health, and alleviate functional declines with age and disease.

title: Research Training Program in Psychiatric Epidemiology
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project narrative
Psychiatric epidemiology, a core science of public mental health, is critical to development of prevention,
intervention, and translation efforts to improve population mental health. For 50 years, the Psychiatric
Epidemiology Training (PET) program at Columbia University has advanced scholarship, science, and training
in psychiatric epidemiology. Through balanced growth of historical legacies and new intellectual resources, we
will continue to grow this highly successful program, one of only a few of its kind in the US, to prepare the next
generation of scholars and change-makers to address critical challenges among those with mental illness across
the globe.

activity_code: F99
title: Mechanisms of adipose tissue functional decline with aging.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Visceral adipose tissue drives metabolic dysfunction and chronic inflammation, both of which increase the risk for age-related diseases such as type 2 diabetes, sepsis and Alzheimer’s disease. This proposal investigates how molecular and cellular changes in adipocyte GDF3-SMAD2/3 axis and macrophage mitochondrial dysfunction contribute to these processes during aging. The insights gained from this proposed research will advance our understanding of the mechanisms mediating adipose functional decline and
potentially identify therapeutic targets to mitigate metabolic dysfunction and age-associated inflammation.

application_id: 11232139
activity_code: F99
title: Discovery of the mechanisms governing Natural Killer T-cell activation and therapeutic potential in cancer
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
 Immunotherapies have revolutionized the treatment of many cancers; however, many cancers do not
respond to these treatments or become resistant over time. Natural Killer T (NKT) cells are a poorly understood
T cell subset whose activity may help to solve resistance in some current immunotherapies. This research
proposal will investigate the molecular mechanisms governing NKT cell recruitment to cancers, activation by
tumor specific antigens, and strategies to improve current NKT cell therapy response rates.

application_id: 11121864
activity_code: K08
title: Improving Diagnosis in Gastrointestinal Cancer: Integrating Prediction Models into Routine Clinical Care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) is a marker of various clinical conditions, including serious diagnoses such as
gastrointestinal (GI) tract cancers, but it is not always identified by clinicians. Clinical prediction models could
help clinicians to improve the diagnosis of GI tract cancer sooner using electronic health record (EHR) data,
but clinical prediction models are not routinely used. This proposal will refine a prediction model for GI tract
cancers, identify barriers to using the model, and design a strategy to help encourage clinicians to use the
model.

application_id: 11193929
activity_code: K99
title: Enabling mRNA Therapies for Pediatric High-Grade Gliomas
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: Diffuse midline glioma (DMG) is a lethal form of brain tumors that occurs in the brain stem region of children and young adults. DMG is an exceptionally challenging cancer to treat because of its unique cellular features and location, resulting in a median survival time of 9-12 months and a 5-year survival rate that is nearly 0%. This proposal aims to develop drug delivery technologies that can facilitate mRNA therapies for DMG.

application_id: 11242300
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of Cerebral Blood flow by Intrinsic Neuromodulation in Wakefulness and Sleep
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This predoctoral fellowship project applies a quantitative, bioengineering approach to a key neuroscience
question about the regulation of cerebral blood flow by neural activity. Proposed research will contribute to an
informed interpretation of noninvasive brain imaging and deliver mechanistic understanding needed for studies
of neurological disease, where a deterioration of neurovascular physiology contributes to motor and cognitive
decline. This fellowship will offer the applicant an invaluable training opportunity facilitating his career
development as a future independent investigator.

application_id: 11141555
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the Role of Diet and ACSS2 in Metabolic and Epigenetic Regulation of Colorectal Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Studies of how distinct dietary inputs integrate to affect colorectal cancer metabolism and epigenetic regulation are limited. This project will contribute to our understanding of how metabolic pathways regulate the differentiation and function of CRC, as well as how manipulation of host genetics can augment or limit the efficacy of dietary interventions. Determining the interaction between dietary inputs in the context of host genetics has the potential to identify novel mechanisms to inform dietary interventions to prevent and combat CRC.

application_id: 11233288
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating environmental and gene-environment contributors to Parkinson's disease risk by coupling quantitative environmental exposure data to iPSC modeling
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This work seeks to understand how environmental factors, specifically pesticides, influence the development of
Parkinson's Disease (PD) at the level of effects on dopaminergic neurons, the critical cell population lost in PD.
Goals of the work include testing how specific pesticides cause damage to neurons derived from PD patient
stem cells, and how that damage is influenced by astrocytes and microglia—two other influential central
nervous system cell types. Finally, we will begin to set the stage in this proposal for performing “epidemiology
in a dish”, growing the stem cells of many PD patients at once, to ultimately test which genetic factors render
patients susceptible to pesticides in the first place.

title: Microbial Genomic Determinants of Antibiotic Treatment Failure in Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY

Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia is the most common cause of death due to bloodstream infection in the

aureus contributes to antibiotic failure in S. aureus bacteremia patients.

application_id: 11235137
activity_code: K08
title: Leveraging the Gut Microbiome to Regulate Hepatic Gluconeogenesis
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is the most-prevalent chronic liver disease worldwide and can progress
to cirrhosis and liver cancer, but has no approved pharmaceutical treatments. The proposed studies are relevant
to the mission of the NIDDK because they will identify novel mechanisms by which the gut microbiome regulates
hepatic gluconeogenesis in states of health and obesity-related metabolic disorders. The proposed research is
relevant to public health because it will determine specific gut microbes and microbially-derived metabolites that
regulate hepatic gluconeogenesis, with the goal of identifying new pharmacotherapeutic approaches to the
management of NAFLD.

application_id: 11235146
activity_code: K08
title: Machine learning-based methods for the analysis of microbial glycomes and proteomes in inflammatory bowel disease.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) affects over 1.3 million Americans, but despite intensive
research, the exact cause of IBD remains unclear. Interactions between the surface proteins
and sugars of microbes and human cells have important effects on inflammation and its
regulation, but their investigation is difficult due to the complex genetic and chemical
environment of the gut. This project aims to design and apply advanced computational methods
based on deep learning to enhance the discovery of significant changes in microbial proteins
and sugars with a goal of gaining new insights into the development, progression, and treatment
of IBD.

application_id: 11235151
activity_code: K08
title: Diversity generation in the gastrointestinal microbiome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Factors that promote microbial colonization in complex and dynamic communities such as the human
gastrointestinal microbiome are poorly understood, but knowledge of their functions could lead to therapeutic
interventions that reverse dysbiotic states. We predict that rapid gene diversifiers, such as Diversity Generating
Retroelements (DGRs), provide bacteria with selective fitness advantages through rapid evolution of ligand
binding proteins that facilitate colonization within the gut microbiome. By testing this prediction, we expect to
generate a toolkit for engineering adaptative systems in beneficial microbes that allow efficient engraftment in
the gut microbiome.
