activity_code: K99
title: Astrocyte-neuron interactions in Rett Syndrome and Fragile X Syndrome
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The neurodevelopmental disorders Rett Syndrome and Fragile X Syndrome are characterized by altered synaptic function, neuronal morphology, and behavioral deficits. Historically, neurodevelopmental disorder research has focused on neurons but another brain cell type, astrocytes, are increasingly implicated in these disorders with recent research showing aberrant astrocyte protein secretion of the class 3 semaphorin Sema3c in models of both disorders. This project investigates if reducing astrocyte Sema3c, or its receptor, can improve neuron function and behavior in Rett Syndrome and Fragile X Syndrome, informing on potential novel therapeutic targets.

application_id: 11235186
activity_code: K08
title: DEVELOPMENTAL REGULATION OF FIBROBLAST GROWTH FACTOR-19 IN NEONATES
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Preterm infants have immature gastrointestinal function, small bile acid pools, and adrenal insufficiency that
limit their development and response to enteral nutrition. Fibroblast growth factor 19 (FGF19) is a potent fetal
growth factor that is upregulated postnatally and functions to regulate bile acid homeostasis. In this proposal,
we seek to determine the impact of perinatal glucocorticoids and FGF19 on gastrointestinal, hepatic and
metabolic outcomes in preterm pigs.

title: Astrocyte-neuron interactions in Rett Syndrome and Fragile X Syndrome
org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The neurodevelopmental disorders Rett Syndrome and Fragile X Syndrome are characterized by altered
synaptic function, neuronal morphology, and behavioral deficits. Historically, neurodevelopmental disorder
research has focused on neurons but another brain cell type, astrocytes, are increasingly implicated in these
disorders with recent research showing aberrant astrocyte protein secretion of the class 3 semaphorin Sema3c

neuron function and behavior in Rett Syndrome and Fragile X Syndrome, informing on potential novel
therapeutic targets.

activity_code: F30
title: The role of NFkB in calcineurin inhibitor-induced renal fibrosis
org_name: WRIGHT STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative As our understanding of calcineurin signaling grows, we are able to elucidate the molecular mechanisms contributing to CNI-induced renal fibrosis. Our project goal is to test the hypothesis that renal CnAα inhibition upregulates NFκB signaling which promotes irreversible renal damage. These findings have clinical relevance and advance the field of renal physiology and pharmacology and pursues the idea that newer CNIs can be developed that effectively blunt the immune response while reducing or preventing kidney damage.

application_id: 11238458
activity_code: K08
title: Decoding the epigenetic landscape that delineates T cell homeostatic proliferation from uncontrolled growth”
org_name: ST. JUDE CHILDREN'S RESEARCH HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The mentored research proposed here will investigate the role of clonal hematopoiesis (CH)-associated genes
in driving T cell proliferation. Specifically, we will look at disruption of three main regulators of CH (DNMT3A,
TET2, ASXL1) to determine the specific epigenetic programs that delineate T cell homeostasis from a
hyperproliferative state. These results will inform on the underlying programs that sustain T cell proliferative
capacity and guide future efforts for the design of CAR T cells.

application_id: 11238514
activity_code: K08
title: Modulating the Microbiome to Enhance Right Ventricular Function in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
This proposal is designed to further train Sasha Prisco, MD, PhD in molecular and cellular cardiac physiology
and multiple omics techniques to help in the transition to becoming an independently funded physician-scientist
who studies the mechanisms of right ventricular dysfunction in pulmonary hypertension. Her project
investigates if Lactobacillus supplementation modulates the gut microbiome, systemic inflammation, the
microtubule cytoskeleton, and right ventricular function in pulmonary arterial hypertension. During her training,
she will refine skills that will enable her to perform clinically-relevant, translational research that will hopefully
allow for future development of novel therapies for this rare and devastating disease.

title: Enabling Novel Strategies for the Clinical Care of Urinary Tract Infection via Microbial Engineering

title: Fused Imaging and Large Language Foundational Model for 90-day Stroke Outcome Prediction
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Current endovascular therapy (EVT) patient selection heavily relies on neuroimaging for penumbra/core mismatch, yet this approach inadequately captures the full spectrum of stroke pathophysiology and overlooks key patient concerns, such as long-term functional outcomes, neglecting both structured data and crucial vital information from unstructured clinical notes. This project introduces the Fused Imaging and Large Language Foundational Model (FILLM), an innovative multi-modal AI system integrating holistic patient data, including imaging, unstructured clinical notes, and structured tabular data, to transform assessment of stroke patients and the prediction of treatment efficacy. application_id: 11238959
activity_code: K08
title: Circuit Modulation in Spinocerebellar Ataxias: Mechanisms and Impact
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs) are progressive, incurable neurodegenerative disorders that result in marked
motor decline and premature death due to cerebellar and brainstem dysfunction. This proposal seeks to
mechanistically define pathways that can improve cerebellar Purkinje neuron firing in SCAs and link
dysfunctional firing to dendritic plasticity and cellular degeneration. The proposed studies will expand our
understanding of SCA biology and potentially uncover novel targets for improved therapy.

application_id: 11238998
activity_code: K08
title: Sex as a Biologic Variable in Groin Hernia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Female patients have been excluded from prior clinical trials evaluating groin hernia management, which may
result in worse outcomes among females following surgery (e.g., higher rates of recurrent hernia, worse pain)
compared to males. This Career Development Award will provide the candidate with the necessary training as
well as establish the infrastructure and critical foundational data to conduct the first ever patient-centered
clinical trial for females with groin hernia. This work will inform the development of evidence-based guidelines
and training to improve care for tens of thousands of females with groin hernia.

activity_code: K08
title: Modeling and prediction of allergic trajectories in children
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Though allergies are among the most common chronic diseases of childhood, we have a poor understanding of why some children
develop specific allergic conditions while others do not. This proposal will utilize multi-institutional electronic health record data
and advanced statistics, machine learning, and natural language processing approaches to identify factors influencing allergic
outcomes and to develop risk prediction models to assist with clinical management. In the age of personalized medicine, this
research will help guide efforts to predict and prevent allergic diseases in children.

title: Identification of a Circuit Basis for Neuropathic Pain in the Parabrachial Nucleus
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Neuropathic pain disorders represent an enormous social and financial burden for society, yet treatments have remained largely unchanged for the last 30 years. application_id: 11242586
activity_code: F31
title: Influence of DNA methylation changes on gene expression from the inactive X chromosome in B cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Several autoimmune diseases exhibit a strong female bias, but the mechanisms underlying this bias are poorly
understood. Altered X-linked gene expression in female-biased autoimmune disease patient lymphocytes
suggests gene expression from the inactive X in female cells may be dysregulated. This project aims to
investigate how changes in DNA methylation, which is globally dysregulated in patient cells, impact gene
expression from the inactive X chromosome in B cells, and whether DNA methylation regulates gene
expression independently of Xist, the long non-coding RNA responsible for X chromosome inactivation.

application_id: 11241095
activity_code: K08
title: Understanding developmental neurogenomic mechanisms in externalizing disorders
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Externalizing disorders are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in the United States, and the
development of novel prevention and therapeutic intervention efforts for these illnesses will require a deeper
understanding of their etiological bases. Motivated by recent discoveries implicating early life as a critical risk
window, the proposed research will characterize the developmental neurogenomic pathways that confer
liability for externalizing psychopathology by combining cutting-edge methods from genomics, neuroscience,
and informatics. Findings will provide an integrated, systems-level understanding of the complex molecular
underpinnings of externalizing disorders, thus laying the foundation for improved risk stratification, drug
discovery, and other translation...

application_id: 11241099
activity_code: K08
title: Optimization and mechanisms of electrical neurostimulation in mouse models of epilepsy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Drug-resistant epilepsy is a devastating neurological disorder that afflicts approximately 1 million people in the
United States who have limited effective treatment options. Brain stimulation is a novel therapy for epilepsy that
is adjustable and generally well-tolerated; however, the optimal protocols and underlying mechanisms of
neurostimulation in epilepsy remain unknown. The proposed study will explore the temporal and spatial
optimization of neurostimulation in epilepsy, and investigate its novel mechanistic effects on disease evolution
with the ultimate goal to improve neurostimulation therapy and the lives of people with epilepsy.

application_id: 11241975
activity_code: K08
title: Impaired Pneumococcal Antibody Function and Exacerbations of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative
Exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) are a major source of morbidity, mortality,
and healthcare costs. Bacterial infections are a common cause of exacerbations, however it is not known
whether individuals with frequent exacerbations have specific defects in antibody function against bacteria.
Investigating antibody-mediated immune function in COPD could lead to the recognition of novel risk factors for
exacerbations, newly recognized sub-populations, and targeted therapeutic interventions for individuals with
immune dysfunction, with potential for broader application to other pathogen types and disease processes.

application_id: 11231026
activity_code: K01
title: Novel approaches for assessing the relationship between stress, cognition, and alcohol biomarkers in real-time
org_name: WASHINGTON STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
While stress and executive function have independently been associated with harmful alcohol use, these
variables have predominately been investigated in controlled laboratory settings and treatment seeking
individuals with alcohol use disorder, thereby potentially limiting the generalizability of findings. Using novel
technologies and intensive longitudinal assessments, the proposed study will investigate the relationship
between objective measures of stress, executive function, and alcohol use in laboratory and real-world settings
in non-treatment seeking individuals with alcohol use disorder. Findings from this study have the potential to
improve the field’s understanding of mechanisms underlying the addiction cycle and inform interventions for
AUD.

Patients with severe alcohol-associated hepatitis (AH) exhibit severe inflammation, and dysregulated immune

application_id: 11245793
activity_code: K08
title: Aging lymphocytes: senescence programs in lymphocytes relevant to human inflammatory bowel disease
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ARIZONA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because the discovery of the underlying mechanisms
leading to the development of senescent FOXP3+ cells in inflammation will open novel experimental
therapeutics, including cellular therapy in human Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) and other immune-
mediated diseases (i.e., rheumatologic, neurologic, dermatologic conditions, and cancer). Insight into the
development of senescent FOXP3+ cells will benefit TREG cell therapy trials, as well as trials of epigenetic
regulators in cancer research. Thus, the proposed research is relevant to the part of the NIH’s mission that
pertains to developing fundamental knowledge to reduce human illness.

application_id: 10796188
activity_code: K99
title: Breaching the Tumor-Immune Mechanical Barrier for Optimal Antitumor Immunity
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Despite the successful development of multiple immunotherapeutic modalities, tumor immune exclusion mechanisms prevent many patients from reaping their benefits. Recent models suggest that tumors actively enforce a mechanical barrier at the tumor-stroma periphery to exclude antitumor immune infiltration and to prevent subsequent immune-mediated killing. This proposal seeks to systematically dissect the components of the tumor-immune mechanical barrier through approaches including in vivo preclinical mouse models, genetic screening, and multi-omics spatial analysis of patient specimens, which will reveal unanticipated immune suppressive mechanisms utilized by cancer and thereby shed new light on novel anticancer treatment strategies.

application_id: 11247120
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating Critical Dependencies Underlying Therapeutic Evasion in Philadelphia Chromosome-like Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Relapsed acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) is a leading cause of cancer death in children/adolescents, and
Philadelphia chromosome-like ALL is a common, high-risk subtype characterized by miswired signaling, high
relapse rates, and extremely poor survival. Our research uses systems biology approaches to identify how
changes in multiple pathways at the single-cell level may lead to leukemia resistance and relapse. Through
these studies, we hope to improve our understanding of the critical biologic mechanisms driving this high-risk
leukemia and to develop more effective combination precision medicine therapies to cure patients.

application_id: 11247542
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of PCDH19-clustering epilepsy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
PCDH19-clustering epilepsy (PCE) is a severe developmental and epileptic encephalopathy predominantly
affecting girls and caused by pathogenic variants in an X-linked gene. Using a mouse model that we recently
developed, we propose to study how this unique inheritance pattern leads to abnormal interneuron
development and susceptibility to seizures. This work will provide insight into disease mechanisms and may
identify a therapeutic target to treat patients with PCE.

application_id: 11247956
activity_code: K08
title: Identifying mechanisms of immune dysregulation via severe pediatric SARS-CoV-2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Immune dysregulation underlies many forms of pediatric critical illness, including severe manifestations of
SARS-CoV-2 infection. Efforts to effectively identify and treat children with infection-related immune
dysregulation have been hindered by gaps in knowledge of the mechanisms connecting pathogen exposure to
severe disease. This proposal leverages state-of-the-art immune profiling technologies and a unique cohort of
children with life-threatening manifestations of SARS-CoV-2 to elucidate some of these mechanisms, towards
improved outcomes for children particularly vulnerable to common infections.

application_id: 11247962
activity_code: K08
title: Utilizing induced pluripotent stem cells to study the role of alveolar type 2 cell dysfunction in pulmonary fibrosis
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative
Pulmonary fibrosis (PF) syndromes, including adult idiopathic PF (IPF) and childhood interstitial lung disease
(chILD), cause significant morbidity and mortality worldwide. Treatment options are limited and the development
of new therapeutics is hampered by the lack of available human in vitro models that are needed to understand
pathogenesis or develop novel therapies. This proposal seeks to establish a new recombinant human organoid
model system, using patient-specific cells to study the role of the alveolar epithelium at the inception of PF.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease and a major contributor to morbidity and
mortality. Despite antihypertensive therapy and glycemic control, an estimated 40% of patients ultimately
develop chronic kidney disease. Single cell sequencing of early diabetic kidney samples can reveal the cell
types and pathways that contribute to disease progression and help to identify novel treatment targets.

application_id: 11248386
activity_code: K08
title: Transcriptional Responses to Wildfire Pollution in Airway Epithelial Cells Identify Genetic Risk Factors and Mechanisms of Asthma Exacerbations
org_name: NATIONAL JEWISH HEALTH
phr: Project Narrative
Asthma exacerbations are responsible for the major health impacts associated with the disease, and increased
air pollution from wildfire smoke is a major contributor to exacerbations. Patients with asthma experience
variable sensitivity to air pollution, and public health recommendations fail to appropriately target high risk
patients. Using primary human tissue culture, next-generation sequencing-based techniques and bioinformatic
tools, we will characterize genetic risk factors that connect wildfire air pollution with asthma exacerbations and
identify mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets for pollution-associated asthma exacerbations.

application_id: 11140950
activity_code: F32
title: Prospective contributions of the vaginal microbiome and immune milieu to spontaneous Chlamydia trachomatis clearance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Chlamydia trachomatis (CT) is the most common bacterial sexually transmitted infection in the US; consequences of untreated CT include pelvic inflammatory disease, ectopic pregnancy, and increased healthcare costs. This proposal will assess how the cervicovaginal metagenome and immune response contribute to rapid spontaneous CT clearance (without antibiotics) because CT clearance likely provides the greatest protection against reproductive sequelae when it happens early in infection. Findings from this work will identify potential therapeutic targets to promote rapid CT clearance, which can ultimately be translated into antibiotic-sparing CT treatments, and inform CT vaccine design.

application_id: 11248843
activity_code: K08
title: Lung resident Treg suppression of Th2 resident memory T cells in allergicasthma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
T helper type 2 tissue-resident memory cells (Th2-Trm) are key drivers of allergic asthma due to their
persistence in the airways and amnestic responses upon local allergen re-exposure. FoxP3+ regulatory T cells
(Tregs) are potent suppressors of allergic inflammation. This proposal explores the possibility that allergen-
specific tissue resident (r) Tregs in the lung are particularly effective at suppressing allergic inflammation due
to their positioning in a unique niche alongside Th2-Trm through shared chemokine receptor systems.

application_id: 11249571
activity_code: K08
title: Improving diagnosis of post-traumatic sepsis using plasma microbial DNA sequencing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sepsis is a life-threatening emergency that requires rapid diagnosis of infections and treatment with antibiotics.
In trauma patients, diagnosis of sepsis is particularly challenging because initial injury itself can cause
physiological derangements that mimic those attributed to infections. In this project, we propose to develop
biomarkers to improve diagnostics, clinical management, and outcomes for sepsis in trauma patients using
sequencing analysis of circulating microbial DNA in plasma.

title: Major pharmacy chain and retailer investments in primary care and implications for access to and quality of care in older adults
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Primary care is experiencing significant corporate investment, with a key subset of investments being made by
major pharmacy chains and retailers that own pharmacies seeking to provide primary care for older adults. This
project will examine such retailer-owned primary care clinics and their implications for access to and quality of
care for older adults (age 65+). Results will provide new evidence for clinicians,
characteristics
patients,

of retailer-owned primary care clinics, impacts of retailer-owned primary care clinics for older adult
and factors impacting care delivery at retailer-owned primary care clinics.

title: Mechanisms of proinflammatory glial activation in retinal degeneration
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Activation of the innate immune system has been recognized as a significant component of numerous forms of
neurodegenerative disease. This project aims to characterize the factors influencing microglia, key mediators
of inflammation in the retina, to switch from homeostatic to proinflammatory functions as part of disease
progression in age-related macular degeneration. Using novel methods for gene knockout of key pathways in
human stem-cell derived microglia-like cells, single-cell RNA sequencing analysis, and highly multiplexed
molecular mapping in tissue with digital histologic analysis, this work will identify tissue microenvironment
factors and immune checkpoints representing novel therapeutic targets for retinal degeneration.

application_id: 11249621
activity_code: K08
title: Health Opportunities & Promoters to Enhance Screening for Lung Cancer (Hopes for Lung Cancer)
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer-specific mortality in the US due to a complex combination of factors, and the literature has shown that patients from Hispanic/Latino communities experience worse lung cancer outcomes due to advanced stage at presentation. Lung cancer screening (LCS) can save lives and improve lung cancer outcomes through early detection among high-risk eligible patients that currently smoke or quit. Despite the proven lung cancer benefits of early detection with LCS, LCS uptake remains low nationally and many high-risk patients that are eligible for LCS will continue to die from lung cancer without tailored outreach interventions, particularly among Hispanic/Latino communities that have been shown to experience worse lung cancer outcomes. application_id: 11249648
activity_code: K08
title: Eicosanoid Pathways Underlying Disease Risk in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite advances in the diagnosis and treatment of pulmonary arterial hypertension, morbidity and mortality
remain unacceptably high. To improve our currently limited understanding of how complex bioactive metabolite
pathways interact to promote or prevent pulmonary hypertension, we will study upstream mediators of
endothelial dysfunction in relation to disease risk and progression, in comprehensive study design. These studies
can identify novel pathogenic and prognostic biomarkers and future therapeutic interventions.

application_id: 11249996
activity_code: K08
title: Physiology of Lifespan Extension and Metabolic Hormesis with Riboflavin Depletion
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Calorie restriction has been shown to be beneficial to healthspan and lifespan in multiple animal models. We
have shown that depletion of riboflavin, also known as Vitamin B2, extends lifespan in the nematode worm in a
model similar to calorie restriction. This project serves to investigate the mechanisms of how this occurs and
whether riboflavin depletion can promote similar benefits in mammals, with the hope of identifying new
treatments for aging associated diseases.

application_id: 11250147
activity_code: K08
title: Automated ascertainment of bleeding and target lesion revascularization after percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) using electronic health record (EHR) data
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Current methods of ascertaining complications after percutaneous coronary interventions (PCI) or ‘stent
procedures’ are resource intensive and inadequate. This proposal aims to create and validate hybrid
algorithms which use structured and unstructured data from the electronic health record leveraging natural
language processing techniques, to reliably detect two important post-PCI events – in-hospital bleeding and 1-
year target lesion revascularization. These algorithms will be incorporated into an automated routine which
generates scheduled reports on these complications.

application_id: 11251211
activity_code: K08
title: Interstitial macrophages in cigarette smoke-induced small airway remodeling
org_name: NATIONAL JEWISH HEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cigarette smoking-related lung diseases like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) remain a leading
cause of death worldwide. We believe Lyve-1 positive peribronchial interstitial macrophages play a key role in
promoting the pathologic thickening of the small airway walls in response to cigarette smoke and may be a
potential therapeutic target to intervene in COPD pathogenesis. We will test the hypothesis that matrix
metalloprotein 9 production in response to Lyve-1 on the IM surface binding to hyaluronic acid promotes airway
wall remodeling and fibrosis after prolonged cigarette smoke exposure.

application_id: 11251276
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of E3 Ligase MARCH10 in Influenza Virus Infection
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
There are ~ 1 billion human influenza virus cases worldwide each year that cause up to 650,000 deaths.
Influenza virus infection remains difficult to treat because the antiviral drugs available are usually limited to
preventing influenza virus infection early in the infection and increasing drug resistance limits their
effectiveness. We have identified an E3 ligase MARCH10 that targets an influenza virus virulence factor for
degradation and propose in vitro and in vivo studies with the ultimate goal of developing new therapies to
combat influenza virus infection.

activity_code: F31
title: Behavioral and neural mechanisms of chemosensation in skin-penetrating parasitic nematodes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Skin-penetrating parasitic nematodes cause significant global health burdens that disproportionately impact socioeconomically disadvantaged communities. This proposal aims to identify and characterize the behavioral and neural mechanisms mediating odor-evoked host seeking and host invasion in the human-parasitic, skin- penetrating nematode Strongyloides stercoralis. Findings from this work will generate fundamental insight into parasitic nematode chemosensory biology that will inform future preventative and control methods.

application_id: 10785057
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the Alternative Splicing Landscape of Human Retinal Development and Degeneration
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Retinitis pigmentosa (RP) encompasses a large and heterogenous group of inherited eye diseases characterized by irreversible vision loss and blindness due to the death of the light-sensing photoreceptor cells of the retina. Despite affecting over 1.5 million individuals worldwide, treatment options for RP remain limited. This proposal uses three-dimensional stem cell-derived retinal organoids to model human retinal development and investigate why retinal cells are primarily, and often exclusively, susceptible to certain genetic mutations, particularly in genes that are universally expressed in cells throughout the body.

application_id: 11253281
activity_code: K08
title: Single-cell dissection of CD4 T cell changes in patients with immune-related adverse events following PD-1 inhibition
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibition (ICI) in patients with cancer is associated with secondary immune-related
adverse events (irAEs) that limit its use, especially for those patients with the higher-grade toxicities. PD-1
signaling is important for maintaining immune homeostasis but the exact mechanism through which PD-1
inhibition leads to autoimmunity is not understood. This proposal will test the hypothesis that de novo loss of PD-
1 signaling enhances the antigen sensitivity of CD4 T cells and leads to the activation of autoreactive CD4 T
cells that mediate irAE development.

application_id: 11253289
activity_code: K08
title: Perivascular adipose tissue in the pathogenesis of lupus vasculopathy
org_name: AUGUSTA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE (PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE STATEMENT)
Patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE, commonly called lupus), a devastating autoimmune disease
that preferentially targets African Americans and women of childbearing age, are at high risk for cardiovascular
disease due to accelerated atherosclerosis. The proposed project will define mechanisms whereby fat that
surrounds the blood vessels contributes to lupus-associated vascular disease. Furthermore, this proposal may
lead to new therapeutic strategies directed against cardiovascular disease in patients with lupus.

activity_code: K23
title: Toward equity in vision health: Using implementation science to adapt a targeted transportation intervention for patients with diabetic retinopathy (PRONTO-EYE)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diabetic retinopathy is the most common cause of preventable vision loss in the U.S. and disproportionately affects minorities and people with limited socioeconomic resources. The proposed study will co-design, implement, and evaluate a ride-share transportation intervention, adapted from the existing PROgram for Non- emergency TranspOrtation (PRONTO), in patients with diabetic retinopathy residing in neighborhoods with high social vulnerability with a goal to increase adherence to scheduled ophthalmology visits as a critical step in reducing vision loss. This study will prioritize the association of social vulnerability with missed ophthalmology appointments to help guide a targeted patient-centered intervention.

application_id: 10807673
activity_code: K01
title: Cumulative effects of persistent organic pollutants and non-chemical stressors on child development
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Pregnant people are exposed to persistent organic pollutants (POPs) and non-chemical stressors which adversely impact maternal and child health outcomes, including cognitive development in offspring. African Americans, who disproportionally experience elevated rates of adverse maternal and child health outcomes, are also exposed to the highest levels of environmental hazards and social stressors. This research will leverage a prospective birth cohort of African Americans and will apply novel statistical methods to examine infant neurodevelopment in relation to a mixture of prenatal POPs and social stress exposures, better reflecting real-life exposure patterns.

phr: The purpose of the Fellowship In Genomics Outcomes Research (FIGOR) is for Genetic Counselor Scholars (GCS) to experience mentored research in a highly collaborative and supportive environment with 50% effort protected time for 24 months. application_id: 11257730
activity_code: K08
title: Evaluating mRNA-lipid nanoparticle platforms and adjuvants to overcome maternal antibody interference
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
Despite significant advances in vaccine technology, we still lack vaccines that effectively induce protective
antibody responses in newborns and young infants. Vaccine efforts in newborns using conventional platforms
are often hindered by interference from maternal antibodies. The proposed research will investigate the potential
for mRNA-lipid nanoparticles and lipid nanoparticle adjuvants to overcome maternal antibody interference with
the vision that identifying new approaches to vaccine and adjuvant development may impact rational vaccine
design for newborns and young infants.

application_id: 11122270
activity_code: K99
title: Modifiable risk factors and midlife cognition in diverse populations: a pooled cohort study
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Modifiable risk factors for Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias are more common in racial and ethnic
minorities in the United States, who also bear a disproportionate burden of Alzheimer’s Disease. The goal of
this proposal is to understand how these modifiable risk factors, which often co-occur, contribute to cognitive
disparities during midlife in diverse populations and explore the underlying mechanisms. The findings will
provide insights into whether and how modifiable risk factors prevention can benefit efforts to lessen cognitive
disparities as early as in midlife, particularly among a growing number of racial and ethnic minorities in the
United States.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our program aims to provide neurosurgery residents with the opportunity to engage in significant structured research during their residency by coupling residents with established productive mentors. application_id: 11258272
activity_code: K08
title: The role of endothelial derived Leucine-Rich Alpha-2-Glycoprotein 1 (LRG1) in the pathogenesis of COPD
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is the fourth leading cause of death worldwide and
current therapy only treats symptoms but cannot stop progression of this debilitating disease. We have
found evidence that vascular dysfunction leads the development of the type of lung destruction that is
found in COPD. In this proposal, we aim to uncover new therapies targeting the vascular impairments
causing the disease state of COPD in the hopes of improving the lives of millions of patients.

application_id: 11137769
activity_code: K99
title: The Role of Functional Modularity in the Neural Code Underlying Contextual Behavior
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Disorders in inferring and acting according to context as well as in learning how to behave in new contexts are implicated in numerous neurological disorders, including post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, and addictive behavior. The neural representations that support reliable contextual behavior are not well-understood, which complicates the development of specific behavioral and pharmacological interventions. This proposal will reveal the neural representations that underlie the learning and performance of contextual behavior.

activity_code: K01
title: Impact of Little Cigar and Cigarillos on Co-Use of Multiple Tobacco Products and Cannabis In Young Adults
org_name: CASE WESTERN RESERVE UNIVERSITY
phr: The purpose of this study is to identify how use of little cigars and cigarillos fits within a broader pattern of multiple tobacco product use and cannabis co-use and how variable patterns of consumption may contribute to the development and exacerbation of symptoms associated with tobacco use disorder. application_id: 11137289
activity_code: K01
title: Postpartum Recovery from Opioid Use Disorder through Daily Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (PROUD2ACT)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA HLTH SCIENCES CTR
phr: NARRATIVE
The postpartum period is characterized physiological and psychosocial changes that can destabilize
recovery from opioid use disorder (OUD), leading to medication treatment for OUD (MOUD) discontinuation,
return to substance use, and overdose. This project will develop a mobile health (mHealth) intervention
based on the principles of Acceptance and Commitment Therapy to support individuals with OUD to maintain
stability and continuity in MOUD care and recovery during the postpartum period. Results will establish a
novel, safe, and highly scalable resource to prevent postpartum return to substance use and overdose and
lay the groundwork for a future micro-randomized trial to optimize the delivery of prompts recommending that
users engage with the intervention.

application_id: 11259577
activity_code: K08
title: A Translational Bioinformatics Approach for Phenotyping and Genetic Risk of Severe Cutaneous Adverse Drug Reactions
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Severe cutaneous adverse drug reactions are life-threatening, associated with long-term morbidity, potentially
preventable, and human leukocyte antigen (HLA) restricted immune-mediated adverse drug reactions. Using a
translational bioinformatics approach, we will assemble one the largest cohorts of vancomycin-induced drug
reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) as a model to identify what comorbidities, genetic,
and other multi-omic risk factors interact with HLA to confer the resistance or susceptibility to DRESS. This
research will enable treating clinicians to better prevent, diagnose, and treat severe cutaneous adverse drug
reactions, bringing personalized care to patients.

title: Role of Transposable Elements in Septic Immune Aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Sepsis is a lethal dysregulation of the immune system that results in approximately 11 million deaths annually.
Despite advances in therapy, the mechanisms underlying the immune dysfunction that enables sepsis
progression are poorly understood. This proposed research will investigate the role transposable elements play
in affecting the immune response in sepsis.

application_id: 11261638
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidating Olfactory Epithelial Anti-Viral Responses in Persistent Post-Viral Olfactory Dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative:
Persistent smell loss after viral infections is common, especially in light of the COVID-19 pandemic, with
significant impacts on quality of life. This research will study the role of the innate immune system and how
local inflammation might cause hinder olfactory cell regeneration and cause persistent smell loss. A better
understanding of these mechanisms has the potential to guide development of novel therapies to treat post-
viral smell loss.

application_id: 11262225
activity_code: K08
title: TCR Targeting Antibodies for the Treatment of T cell Cancers
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Few treatment options are available for T cell cancers that affect ~100,000 patients worldwide each year. The
current proposal will generate a type of immunotherapy using new antibodies that specifically kill the T cell
cancers and retain enough healthy T cells to maintain a functioning immune system. This immunotherapy may
improve patient outcome and limit side effects associated with traditional chemotherapies.

application_id: 10786040
activity_code: K23
title: A Meal Delivery and Exercise Intervention to Increase Resilience in Homebound Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Older adults often fear loss of independence and the inability to stay in their homes as they age. There is a need to identify the mechanisms behind this loss of function and to evaluate interventions which could help older adults maintain their autonomy. Thus, this proposed pilot clinical trial will: 1) intervene to minimize the impact of frailty using a home-based exercise program, administered through a meal delivery service, in homebound older adults, and 2) explore possible specific frailty biomarkers to target for early interventions.

application_id: 11262264
activity_code: K08
title: NFkB dynamics in the stimulus specificity of trained immunity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cells of the innate immune system can be reprogrammed by exposure to stimuli such as cytokines or pathogens
in a phenomenon known as “trained immunity.” This can make immune cells either more inflammatory or less
inflammatory in response to subsequent infections, but what determines whether a given stimulus will result in
potentiated or diminished inflammation is unclear. This proposal explores the hypothesis that dynamic features
of the transcription factor NFκB – not if it is activated, but how it is activated – contribute to the stimulus-specificity
of trained immunity through the regulation of latent enhancers that control gene expression.

application_id: 11263620
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms of cardiomyocyte dysfunction in pediatric septic shock
org_name: CINCINNATI CHILDRENS HOSP MED CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
One in four children with septic shock die despite receiving intensive care and antibiotics, and cardiac
dysfunction seems to contribute to this high mortality rate. No treatments exist for cardiac dysfunction in
sepsis, and our goal is to better understand the causes of cardiac injury in children with septic shock to
facilitate development of new medications to help these children recover and survive. We will study these
processes using human cardiac cells and human serum, rather than animal models, to improve the ability to
translate discoveries back to the bedside to improve patient care.

application_id: 10785043
activity_code: K99
title: Establishing reactive astrocyte memory as a risk factor for aging-related neurodegenerative disease
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Viral and bacterial infections result in transient neuroinflammation and are a common occurrence throughout life, yet how or why temporary inflammation increases the risk of developing neurodegenerative disease (ND) remains elusive. Similarly, despite the key role of astrocytes in neuron function and modulating the immune response, they are often overlooked in the study of ND. The proposed research will address these outstanding questions and provide fundamental insight into how transient astrocyte reactivity affects future insults and how this impacts on ND.

application_id: 11264932
activity_code: K08
title: Rapid Anatomic and Quantitative MR Imaging of the Skull Base and Face
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Our goal is to devise clinically optimal MRI synthetic image contrasts for the skull base and face that can be
tailored for field strength, gradient capabilities, spatial resolution, and desired imaging time. We propose to
generate novel qualitative MRI contrasts that optimize clinically-relevant contrast-to-noise and simultaneously
acquire relaxometry biomarkers that are co-registered to the anatomic images at the voxel level with markedly
reduced scan times compared with current standard-of-care skull base and face MR imaging. We also propose
to develop a novel pulse sequence that incorporates scanner gradient strength as a parameter to further
decrease scan times and to evaluate these pulse sequences across 1.5T, 3T, and 7T field strengths.

application_id: 11267221
activity_code: K08
title: The role of cortico-striatal circuitry in model-based decision-making
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: NARRATIVE
Compulsive behaviors such as obsessive-compulsive disorder, addiction, and eating disorders are
characterized by deficits in model-based control, which is the ability to plan future choices using a cognitive
map of the environment. Additionally, compulsive behaviors are associated with disrupted activity in the same
cortico-striatal circuits that are hypothesized to be important for model-based control, yet the specific roles of
cortico-striatal circuits in model-based control are unknown due to a paucity of in vivo studies of neural activity.
This K08 proposal aims to determine how model-based control is represented in distinct cell types in the
prelimbic cortex and dorsomedial striatum - two areas implicated in both model-based control and compulsive
behaviors – by monitoring a...

application_id: 11270831
activity_code: K08
title: INVESTIGATING THE MOLECULAR MECHANISMS OF DIFFERENTIATION FAILURE IN NF2 MUTANT NEUROEPITHELIAL STEM CELLS
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Project Narrative
The only treatment for late-stage symptomatic ependymoma is high-risk surgery. We have identified novel
dysregulated radial-glia like cells that fail to differentiate normally in the setting of NF2 loss. This research will
determine dysregulated signaling pathways in these pre-neoplastic cells to identify new drug targets.

application_id: 11229418
activity_code: K01
title: Dynamic Treatment Regimes for Personalized Opioid Use Disorder Care
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research aims to improve opioid use disorder (OUD) treatment quality by using machine
learning dynamic treatment regime models (DTRs) that measure the time-varying relationship between
treatment and patient-reported OUD severity to identify optimal practice patterns. Several DTR models will be
estimated and compared in terms of their ability to identify treatment decision rules that minimize patient risk of
fatal and non-fatal opioid overdose, and a pilot hybrid factorial sequential multiple assignment trial (SMART)
will be conducted to assess the feasibility and acceptability of a full-scale trial that will evaluate the
effectiveness of the generated treatment decision rules. The research aligns with NIDA's strategic goals by
promising significant advancemen...

application_id: 11282251
activity_code: K08
title: Immunometabolic Regulation of CD8 T Cell Dynamics in Human Critical Illness and Sepsis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Critical illness, including sepsis, results in severe morbidity and mortality across all demographics worldwide
and is associated with progressive immune dysfunction and metabolic disarray. This research aims to
determine the metabolic mechanisms underlying CD8 T cell impairment, focusing on glutamine metabolism
and a distinct subset of CD8 T effectors defined by CXCR4 and IL7R expression. Understanding these
pathways will inform new therapeutic strategies to improve immune resilience and patient outcomes in critical
illness and sepsis.

application_id: 11097363
activity_code: K23
title: Balanced and Empowered EaTing (BEET) in Diabetes: A feasibility trial
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Disordered eating behaviors (DEBs) in type 2 diabetes (T2DM) is an under-recognized clinical issue that has
shown to worsen diabetes self-management and increase the risk for health concerns, such as higher
triglycerides and poor short-term metabolic control. By partnering with behavioral health providers in real
clinical settings and utilizing implementation science methods to evaluate the implementation of a novel
intervention to treat DEBs in T2DM, this project has the potential to fill a tremendous unmet need for people
with T2DM and accelerate the translation of evidence-based interventions in chronic disease management.

application_id: 11283923
activity_code: K08
title: Getting Excited about Cortical Malformations: Discovering the Mechanisms Leading to Seizure-prone Neurons in Malformations of Cortical Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative
Childhood epilepsy is a common and often devastating disease, and chronic seizures lead to a
significant increase in premature mortality and developmental delay. A substantial proportion of
children with Malformations of Cortical Development (MCDs) experience medically and
surgically refractory seizures. This project will determine whether elongated axons, caused by
known genetic changes, directly contribute to the untreatable seizures in children with MCDs
and are thus a potential therapeutic target.

application_id: 11285271
activity_code: K08
title: Lung Myofibroblast De-Differentiation and Fibrosis Resolution Depend on cAMP-mediated Inhibition of HuR.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is a common and deadly scarring lung disease for which there is no cure.
The development and progression of IPF proceeds when myofibroblasts – scar forming and contractile cells
that differentiate from fibroblasts – fail to de-differentiate following lung injury. This proposal seeks to
investigate the mechanisms by which myofibroblasts can be de-differentiated and cleared – thereby enabling
fibrosis resolution – as well as to support the career development of a promising and committed physician
scientist.

application_id: 11113411
activity_code: K01
title: Electronic Cigarettes as a Harm Reduction Strategy Among People with Opioid Use Disorder on Buprenorphine
org_name: CLEMSON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This K01 proposal aims to investigate the impact of e-cigarette (EC) flavors and usage patterns on achieving
harm reduction milestones. Utilizing the RE-AIM Framework, we will identify barriers and facilitators to
implementing and maintaining the EC protocol in both rural and urban settings. The findings will offer preliminary
evidence of the harm reduction potential of ECs and provide strategies for implementing and sustaining EC-
based harm reduction protocols. If successful and sustainable, these protocols may ultimately reduce tobacco-
related morbidity and mortality among individuals with opioid use disorder on buprenorphine.

activity_code: F31
title: Learning-induced changes in distinct auditory cortical cell-types
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Auditory categorization is essential for survival in the animal kingdom and speech comprehension in humans. This project aims to quantify differences between excitatory neural subpopulations, as well as how learning shapes their role in auditory-guided behaviors. Studying these specific cell-types in greater detail could bring about new theories of information processing, neural coding, and learning, providing a foundation for understanding numerous cognitive and behavioral disorders.

application_id: 11115345
activity_code: K01
title: Investigation into nTS mu opioid receptor mechanisms contributing to fentanyl-induced cardiorespiratory depression
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The recent surge in opioid overdose deaths is primarily due to the rise and distribution of the synthetic opioid
fentanyl, known for its high potency and rapid onset of respiratory depression. This proposal aims to evaluate
mechanisms underlying fentanyl-induced dysfunction in the nucleus of the solitary tract (nTS), a dorsal brainstem
nucleus that mediates cardiorespiratory function. The results of this work seek to establish the role of mu opioid
receptors in the nTS in determining the severity and duration of respiratory depression following intravenous
fentanyl exposure.

application_id: 11287852
activity_code: K08
title: Neoglycosylation Epitopes in Metaplasia and Cancer
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Neoglycosylation epitopes have been widely used to aid in the diagnosis as well as the management of
metaplasia and cancers; however, the function of these epitopes is unknown. Here, we begin to study how these
epitopes orchestrate the metaplastic and oncogenic transformations in foregut metaplasias and cancer using in
vitro cell and organoid culture as well as murine models. Specifically, we focus on the transition from sialylation
to sulfated epitopes, which has been associated with increased likelihood of progressing to gastric cancer by
numerous groups over the past several decades.

application_id: 11137020
activity_code: K99
title: Pharmacological Potential of Combined Translation and Autophagy Inhibition in High Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECTIVE NARRATIVE
High grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) is the most lethal gynecologic cancer with current treatment
strategies including the naturally derived compound, Paclitaxel. Unfortunately, drug resistance to current
therapies has led to relapse and eventually death in ovarian cancer patients. Combinatorial treatment of cytotoxic
natural products didesmethyrocaglamide and phyllanthusmin34 as well as mechanism of action will be studied
in vitro and in vivo as novel strategies for treating this deadly cancer.

application_id: 11301085
activity_code: K01
title: Understanding Rising Overdose Mortality in Individuals with Low Socioeconomic Status
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Ending the opioid epidemic is critical to improving America’s health. This K01 Mentored Research Scientist
Development Award provides Dr. J. Travis Donahoe with the opportunity to pursue training and research to: (1)
identify individual- and neighborhood-level factors shaping the opioid epidemic, (2) evaluate policies that
reduce opioid mortality among high-risk individuals, and (3) co-develop targeted interventions in high-risk
communities in Pennsylvania and West Virginia. Ultimately, this work will lay the groundwork for Dr. Donahoe
to secure multiple R01 grants that implement and rigorously evaluate innovative, targeted, and community-
informed interventions to address the opioid crisis.

application_id: 10807829
activity_code: K08
title: Dissecting the cooperation of FOXR2 and ETS transcription factors in FOXR2-expressing diffuse midline gliomas
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diffuse midline gliomas (DMGs) are devastating childhood brain tumors for which there are no effective therapies. I previously identified aberrant FOXR2 expression as a key driver event in DMG formation and growth, and that FOXR2 specifically activates ETS transcription factor circuits and is functionally redundant with growth factor receptor pathways. The work proposed here seeks to define the mechanisms by which FOXR2 cooperates with ETS transcription factors to drive MAPK signaling in FOXR2-expressing DMGs.

application_id: 11289421
activity_code: K08
title: Role of Atp6ap2 in renal proximal tubule lipotoxicity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: Project Narrative
Obesity is an independent risk factor for the development of chronic kidney disease, but current strategies for the
prevention and treatment of obesity related kidney injury are incompletely effective. Lipotoxicity is a recently identified
process that contributes to kidney injury in the setting of obesity, but the mechanisms underlying lipotoxicity in the
kidney are not fully understood. This studies in this proposal will investigate the role of ATP6AP2 in promoting renal
lipotoxicity with the goal of providing a basis for novel therapeutic strategies in the management of obesity related
kidney disease.

title: Precision Prediction of Right Ventricular Size, Function, and Outcomes in Patients with Repaired Tetralogy of Fallot
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
Adolescent and adult patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot, who are at increased risk of heart failure and
sudden cardiac death, require frequent cardiac MRI evaluation of right ventricular volumes (which are poorly
assessed by traditional ECG and echocardiography). To improve risk stratification and reduce the need for
cardiac MRI, which is resource-intensive and unavailable to some, we will create an algorithm to predict right
ventricular size, function, and clinical outcome from ECG waveforms, echocardiographic video, and clinical
data using deep learning technology. This mentored research proposal, along with comprehensive training in
machine learning methods for clinical risk prediction and career development, will position Dr. Son Duong as a
leader in the trans...

application_id: 11291316
activity_code: K08
title: The Establishment of Lung Resident Self-Antigen-Specific CD4+ Tregs Following Acute Tissue injury
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Self-antigen-specific regulatory T cells (Tregs) that expand in response to lung damage can be detected in the
immune system for months after injury. This proposal seeks to determine whether these “experienced” self-
antigen-specific Tregs develop into long-lived Tregs that reside in the lungs to protect the tissue from subsequent
injury. Insights grained from this study will deepen our understanding of how the immune system promotes tissue
health and provide exciting opportunities for potential for new therapies in the treatment of autoimmune diseases
and tissue inflammation.

title: Impact of Gut Epithelial Serotonin on Afferent Neuron Function and Visceral Pain Signaling
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

application_id: 11292413
activity_code: K08
title: Identification of Host-Specific Determinants of APOL1-associated COVAN
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Nearly all cases of COVID-19-associated nephropathy (COVAN) occur in persons with high-risk apolipoprotein
L1 (APOL1) genotype, resulting in significant morbidity and mortality and a disproportionate burden of kidney
disease on persons of West African descent. This study aims to identify host factors that regulate APOL1 and
immune-glomerular interactions in COVAN, providing novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets. Improved
understanding of the host factors that effect COVAN penetrance and development of targeted therapeutics are
critical to mitigating the racial disparities caused by COVAN in COVID-19 related health outcomes and other
forms of APOL1 nephropathy.

application_id: 11293422
activity_code: K08
title: Calcium-mediated paracrine signaling in beta cell cilia
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Diabetes results from an imbalance between insulin secretion and metabolic demand. Our research aims to
identify novel, primary cilia-based calcium signaling mechanisms that underlie islet cell crosstalk and are
important for regulating insulin secretion, in order to generate more effective therapies that preserve beta cell
function in human diabetes.

application_id: 11295473
activity_code: K08
title: Deep Learning of Renal Scans and Kidney Ultrasounds in Children with Antenatal Hydronephrosis
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
Obstruction of the flow of urine from the kidney to the urinary bladder can cause increased back pressure on
the kidney that over time can injure the kidney. Currently available imaging techniques are not able to reliably
identify children who are actively experiencing an obstruction. Machine learning can potentially better
determine which patients are experiencing obstruction by identifying features from imaging studies that are not
apparent to human reviewers.

application_id: 11300198
activity_code: K08
title: Etiology of naturally-occurring anti-ABO antibodies
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative: ABO(H) blood group antigens were the first human polymorphisms described and corresponding
anti-ABO(H) antibodies continue to be the most common immunological barrier to transfusion and
transplantation. However, despite their discovery over a century ago, the factors responsible for anti-ABO(H)
antibody development remain relatively unknown. Using a series translational studies coupled with a new
preclinical model of anti-blood group antibody formation, I will leverage my existing training in transfusion
immunology with training in glycobiology to define key factors that influence the overall levels and specificity of
anti-ABO(H) antibodies.

application_id: 11053039
activity_code: K01
title: Understanding the Cognitive Neural Signatures of Childhood Maltreatment in Adolescents as a Means to Advance the Science of Targeted Academic Intervention to this Vulnerable Population
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Childhood maltreatment is a significant worldwide public health problem, experienced by
approximately ⅓ of all children and costing the US approximately $2 trillion during the lifetimes
of a one-year cohort of children. Children with maltreatment histories are known to suffer long-
term negative academic outcomes that result in a loss of income and career attainment for
those affected. The brain basis of the adverse cognitive consequences of child maltreatment is
not well understood and this project will fill this gap so that targeted academic interventions can
be developed and implemented.

application_id: 10922726
activity_code: F31
title: Brain-based biomarkers of restricted and repetitive behaviors in toddlers at risk for autism
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative  Children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) are not typically diagnosed until 4 years of age and therefore miss a critical developmental time period important for optimizing outcomes. A core symptom of ASD is restricted and repetitive behaviors (RRBs), which impair daily functioning and impact all aspects of social and cognitive development. This proposal will explore early life brain-based biomarkers for ASD and RRBs and has the potential to improve the lives of diagnosed children and their families while providing an opportunity for the applicant to learn crucial skills necessary for an independent career as a developmental cognitive neuroscientist.

title: Modifying adipocyte and pre-adipocyte cell fate in fibroadipose tissue of secondary lymphedema

application_id: 11319793
activity_code: K08
title: A patch circuit dissection of opioid addiction
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Opioid use disorders present a devastating and major public health concern and are marked by behaviors such
as a compulsive desire to use opioids despite negative consequences, severe withdrawal symptoms upon drug
abstinence and incredibly high relapse rates. More knowledge is needed about the specific brain circuits and cell
types that express the mu opioid receptor through which opioids act. This proposal will investigate the properties
of such cells and circuits, with the ultimate goal of building upon these findings to uncover new therapies to
mitigate opioid abuse and relapse.

application_id: 10785474
activity_code: K01
title: Dissecting neural-immune mechanisms of psychosis and related neurodevelopmental disorders
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative Neural-immune pathways play a critical role in neurodevelopmental processes and growing evidence suggests that neural-immune gene expression is dysregulated in schizophrenia and other neurodevelopmental, psychiatric disorders. This project will (a) integrate gene expression and genotype data to generate a genome- wide resource for individual-level prediction of neural-immune gene expression, and (b) characterize the impact of genetically predicted neural-immune gene expression on longitudinal trajectories of structural brain development and change in dimensional measures of mental health relevant to psychosis in children and adolescents. Results will significantly expand our understanding of the genetic mechanisms regulating gene expression in the human brain and the deve...

phr: At least one in five children and adolescents in the United States suffer from a serious mental or neurodevelopmental disorder; these disorders not only affect children and their families but lead to far-reaching, negative educational, financial, vocational, and societal outcomes. title: Adverse childhood experiences and alcohol misuse in Latino adolescents: testing biopsychosocial and cultural mechanisms.
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Latino adolescents consume and misuse alcohol earlier than their peers, placing them at greater risk for

several biological and environmental factors work together to increase the risk of alcohol use and misuse in
Latino adolescents. Results from this project will allow for the identification of high-risk adolescents and provide

application_id: 10764018
activity_code: T32
title: CTSA Postdoctoral T32 at the University of Alabama at Birmingham
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: Project Narrative Dissemination and implementation (D&I) science is the study of how to effectively and efficiently spread evidence-based practices and interventions to the people who can benefit from them. It is important to integrate this type of science into the clinical and translational science spectrum because it helps ensure that new treatments and therapies are not just developed, but also effectively implemented and used in real-world settings. This can ultimately lead to improved health outcomes for individuals and communities. This postdoctoral training program will train new scientists in D&I science so we can take better care of people and improve inequities in health and health outcomes.

application_id: 10894637
activity_code: F32
title: Regulation of Male Germ Cell Development through DND1-Mediated Translation of Epigenetic Factors
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Reproductive health is dependent upon the formation of spermatogonial stem cells (SSCs) from its precursor cells, male germ cells (MGCs), and mechanisms governing this vital period of development are largely unknown. The primary aim of this study is to elucidate how an RNA binding protein (RBP), DND1, can post-transcriptionally regulate its target transcripts, thereby ensuring MGC-to-SSC development. Results obtained from this study can provide insight into why post-transcriptional regulation by RBPs play a critical role in MGC development across species and contribute to reproductive health.

application_id: 11113106
activity_code: K01
title: Equol Supplementation for Improving Vascular Function in Postmenopausal Women with CKD: Efficacy and Mechanistic Role of GPER
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed study is highly significant as: 1) cardiovascular disease is a major contributor to death in women
with chronic kidney disease (CKD) and 2) women’s vascular health is understudied in nephrology, as well as is
a high NIH research priority. The proposed project will investigate the efficacy and underlying mechanisms of
oral supplementation with equol, a unique phytoestrogen, to improve vascular function in both postmenopausal
women with CKD and a postmenopausal CKD mouse model. This study has the potential to identify equol as a
novel, safe, alternative compound to hormone replacement therapy for improving CKD-associated vascular
dysfunction in women.

application_id: 11214190
activity_code: K01
title: Using a community-engaged approach to design and pilot-test an intervention aimed at reducing ultra-processed food consumption in preschool children to improve cardiovascular health
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiometabolic risk factors begin developing in early childhood, and the high consumption of ultra-processed
foods (UPF) among young children is a significant contributor. This study aims to provide evidence on the
association between UPF consumption and cardiometabolic risk factors in toddlers and, using a community-
engaged approach, design and pilot test a family-based intervention. The results of this research will provide
valuable evidence to support early interventions that improve diet quality, reduce cardiometabolic risk, and
ultimately promote long-term cardiovascular health in children.

title: Subjective Cognition in Motoric Cognitive Risk Syndrome: Enhancing Cerebrovascular Risk Detection in Health Disparity Populations
org_name: CUNY GRADUATE SCH AND UNIV CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The global population of older adults with dementia is expected to increase to upwards of 132 million people by 2050, prompting the early identification of clinical presentations associated with increased risk for this disorder. The current project uses a novel, multifaceted approach to assess subjective cognition in Motoric Cognitive Risk Syndrome (MCR), an easily screened predementia condition that combines slow gait speed and subjective cognitive concerns, and predicts cerebrovascular-related risk in diverse older adult populations. This study will be the first to systematically characterize the nature and extent of subjective cognitive concerns in MCR, facilitating new screening tools with enhanced sensitivity for dementia in health disparity populations around the gl...

org_name: HISPANIC ACCESS FOUNDATION
phr: Project Narrative The Senderos a la Ciencia/Pathways to Science project is specifically designed to enhance workforce diversity in the health research sciences space. By offering role model talks featuring Hispanic health research professionals at a Hispanic-serving church, and by providing near-peer mentoring to Hispanic high school students interested in pursuing health science research careers, this initiative will: 1. Build awareness and value among Hispanic parents and youth in targeted under-resourced  Hispanic communities of careers in health research. 2. Engender an interest to pursue health research careers among youth in these same communities. 3. Trigger pursuing a college path in health research among high school students in these same communities. Besides initially serving His...

application_id: 11136732
activity_code: K01
title: Enhancing Clinical Terminology to Enable Precision Mental Healthcare
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research will contribute to the acquisition and clinical application of knowledge about the complex
interrelationships between mental, behavioral, social, and biological phenomena by enhancing standard,
interoperable clinical terminologies to include mental, behavioral, and social processes, findings, assessments,
and interventions. Unique identifiers and machine-readable definitions of, and relationships between, clinical
constructs, processes, findings, assessment instruments, and interventions will enable the use of powerful new
computational paradigms for knowledge discovery and improvement in care delivery.

application_id: 10823278
activity_code: K01
title: Alteration in the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and frailty in aging men with HIV
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE People with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) are at increased risk of age-related disease and complications such as frailty. The proposed research aims to determine 1) the associations of free testosterone and sex hormone binging globulin (SHBG) concentrations with incident frailty, 2) the association of diurnal variation in free testosterone with HIV serostatus and its association with systemic inflammation, 3) the association of novel SHBG glycans with frailty among men with HIV. This work will inform efforts to find new therapeutic targets for frailty via the improvement of alterations in the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis in men with HIV.

title: Bridging Gaps to Translate Plasma Alzheimer's Disease Biomarkers to Diverse Populations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCIENCE CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
As the global burden of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and related dementias (ADRDs) is expected to triple over the
next thirty years, there is an urgent public health need for scalable and cost-effective diagnostic tools that can
be applied across diverse, international populations. The proposed project is highly relevant to public health
by advancing promising ADRD plasma biomarkers towards appropriate clinical and research use in
populations that stand to benefit the most by supporting diagnosis, personalized interventions and public health
strategies. This project closely aligns with NIA research priorities to understand AD in diverse populations, the
impact of biology and personal factors on age-related disease prognosis, and special interest in fostering
ADRD research in ...

application_id: 11256269
activity_code: F31
title: Tracking B cell:Antigen Interactions for HIV-1 Vaccine Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite decades of research, no HIV-1 vaccine exists. The most successful HIV-1 immunization protocols that
elicit neutralizing antibodies against HIV-1 do not elicit a serum antibody response that is strong enough to
protect against infection. The goal of this proposal is to understand the cellular mechanisms that lead to durable
and high magnitude antibody responses upon immunization to identify guidelines for vaccine design.

application_id: 11053978
activity_code: K08
title: Macrophage Expression Quantitative Trait Loci and Tuberculosis Pathogenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although several lines of evidence suggest that genetic variation influences susceptibility to Mycobacterium
tuberculosis (Mtb) infection or tuberculosis (TB) disease, our understanding of the specific genes and variants
associated with TB susceptibility remains incomplete. This proposal aims to investigate the roles and
mechanisms of global human genetic regulators in Mtb-induced macrophage antimicrobial pathways. By
combining an epidemiological approach with the study of underlying immunogenetic mechanisms, we aim to
uncover potential targets for host-directed therapies and establish a means of TB risk screening.

application_id: 11177990
activity_code: F31
title: Impact of RNA splicing factor mutations on circular RNA biogenesis in leukemia
org_name: BECKMAN RESEARCH INSTITUTE/CITY OF HOPE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Blood malignancies are characterized by recurrent somatic mutations in RNA splicing factors that drive aberrant splicing to promote leukemogenesis. Recently, a novel species of RNA called circular RNAs (circRNA) was discovered to be aberrantly expressed in many types of liquid cancers, yet the role of RNA splicing factors mutations on the biogenesis of these molecules, if any, in promoting leukemogenesis is not known. Establishing a link between known RNA splicing factor mutations and circRNAs could define a novel regulatory axis that can be exploited for targeted therapy of these diseases.

application_id: 10786047
activity_code: K99
title: Phase Transitions in Chromatin Organization that cause Cancer Progression
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The functional role of phase transitions in biology are not well proven, but cancers exist that are caused by mutations in or mistargeting of phase-separating chromatin-binding proteins. I will utilize my understanding of these biophysical processes to mechanistically understand this previously undescribed class of oncogenic transformations and work toward building synthetic systems that can rescue defects in chromatin localization, enzymatic functionality and phase behavior of nuclear condensates seen in cancer progression.

application_id: 11127374
activity_code: F32
title: Enhanced cartilage formation of chondrocytes in viscoelastic ECMs under mechanical loading
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
 New approaches are needed to mitigate and treat osteoarthritis, a debilitating disease involving a progressive
erosion of the cartilage tissue, and one that lacks an effective treatment option barring a total surgical
replacement of the joint. Emerging research shows that biomechanical loading of the joint can be an effective
mitigation strategy of osteoarthritis and can accelerate the production of biomedically engineered cartilage tissue,
yet how such loading processes result in improvements in the joint health remains unclear. The proposed
research will establish the fundamental mechanism by which biomechanical loading stimulates the cells in the
joints to facilitate the growth and repair of cartilage tissue, which will advance clinical treatment of osteoarthritis
and...

application_id: 10824157
activity_code: F32
title: Reprogramming Antigen Presenting Cells in Lymph Nodes to Overcome Tumor Immune Tolerance
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: This fellowship application seeks to discover the immune cell population that is responsible for initiating the biological process that allows tumors to spread into distant organs, with the ultimate goal of developing a therapeutic strategy to treat metastatic disease. I will utilize state-of-the-art technology and computational analysis to identify these immune cells, and develop a therapy that reprograms them away from undertaking this undesirable role. Advances from the proposed study will broadly impact cancer patients since metastatic disease is a major cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide.

application_id: 11213375
activity_code: K08
title: From inhibitors to enzymatic deficiencies: exploring alterations in erythrocyte glycolysis as an antimalarial strategy.
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
Malaria represents an ongoing threat to global public health, and new antimalarial therapies are urgently needed.
Malaria parasites infect red blood cells, and red blood cell glycolysis is required for the parasites’ lifecycle. This
proposal examines the role of red blood cell glycolysis in the parasite lifecycle to determine if this host cell
pathway could serve as a target for new antimalarial drugs.

application_id: 11255123
activity_code: K08
title: Expanded evaluation of the role of antibody-dependent innate effector function in TB disease progression in HIV co-infected individuals
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative
HIV co-infection is the leading risk factor for development of active tuberculosis (TB), yet the
mechanisms underlying increased rates of progression are not fully understood. The proposed
research dissects the impact of HIV on the induction of antibody-dependent innate effector
functions and identifies key functional correlates of TB disease progression and control in HIV-
coinfected individuals. The results will provide mechanistic insights into antibody-mediated
immunomodulation, and inform the development of novel diagnostics, therapeutics, and vaccines
to specifically address divergent TB pathogenesis in people living with HIV.
