application_id: 11229973
activity_code: K08
title: Deciphering interactions between human T cells and cardiomyocytes in immune checkpoint inhibitor-related myocarditis
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: The proposed research is relevant to public health because it aims to mitigate severe immune-related
adverse events (irAEs) – the dangerous, morbid, and increasingly common side effects of immune
checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs). This project focuses on a particularly lethal irAE known as ICI-related
myocarditis (irMyocarditis) and uses patient samples and human models to develop a deeper
mechanistic understanding and targeted therapies to treat irMyocarditis while preserving anti-tumor
immune responses. The proposed research is directly relevant to the NCI’s mission to help cancer
patients live longer, healthier lives.

application_id: 10923481
activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Immunoglobulin Genetic Diversity in the Development of Neutralizing Antibodies Against HIV Env Protein
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In 2020 an estimated 1.5 million people acquired HIV, highlighting the urgent need for a prophylactic vaccine to combat the ongoing health crisis. This project will investigate how genetic variation at the immunoglobulin gene loci impacts the ability of rhesus macaques, the gold standard preclinical model for studying HIV, to develop high levels of neutralizing antibody titers against particular epitopes of HIV envelope immunogens. Such studies are essential to understand how humoral vaccine responses vary between individuals, to inform the design of HIV vaccine constructs, and to gain a deeper understanding of antibody responses to HIV proteins.

activity_code: K23
title: Advancing the Approach to Subclinical Diastolic Dysfunction in Youth with Chronic Kidney Disease
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative: Young people with chronic kidney disease (CKD) have a high lifetime risk for cardiovascular disease, including heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). However, youth with CKD have been largely excluded from studies that improve detection of HFpEF at the earliest stages and from trials of medications that prevent heart disease in adults with CKD. This study will leverage insights developed in adults with CKD to better define early and subtle changes in heart function in youth with CKD and to begin to test the reversibility of these changes using an interventional study.

title: Cutaneous opioid circuitry in skin inflammation and sensation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Endogenous opioid peptides regulate the cutaneous sensations of itch and pain and have critical homeostatic

title: Leveraging Causal Inference and Machine Learning Methods to Advance Evidence-Based Maternal Care and Improve Newborn Health Outcomes
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In light of unsettlingly high rates of adverse newborn outcomes – such as perinatal mortality, low birthweight, and preterm birth – in the United States, evidence-based approaches to improve maternity care are vital. Medicaid provider incentives for provision of immediate postpartum long-acting reversible contraception and antepartum fetal surveillance offer two potential strategies to enhance outcomes via improvements in birth spacing and early detection of fetal distress, respectively. In the absence of a robust evidence base, the proposed research will rigorously estimate the impact of each strategy on newborn outcomes to inform data- driven clinical practice around postpartum contraception and antepartum surveillance.

activity_code: F31
title: Post-Stroke Neurovascular Function and Repair within a Porous Hydrogel
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Ischemic stroke is the leading cause of disability in adults in the U.S., and there is no treatment that promotes recovery. The proposed predoctoral NRSA aims to examine innate cellular responses to a potential stroke therapeutic, a biocompatible hydrogel scaffold that establishes a unique neurovascular niche after stroke. This study will assess in vivo revascularization of the stroke infarct in mice, vascular-guided cellular migration, and axonal sprouting in the gel, infarct and peri-infarct with and without physical rehabilitation.

application_id: 11136111
activity_code: K08
title: Promoting patient portal use among Black women to lower barriers to breast cancer screening
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
Patient portals increasingly are used to facilitate breast cancer screening (e.g., through online mammogram
scheduling and screening reminders); yet Black patients use patient portals and receive breast cancer
screening less often than White patients. The proposed study will determine facilitators and barriers for patient
portal use to facilitate breast cancer screening among Black women; co-design with community members an
outreach intervention to encourage and support patient portal use for breast cancer screening; and then
implement and test this intervention in a large academic medical center. This research will result in the design
and evaluation of a culturally tailored intervention that subsequently can be tested in a large, multi-center
randomized trial.

title: Understanding Patient Level Implementation Determinants Related to Long-acting Injectable HIV Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis among Young Men Who Have Sex with Men Living in Rural Areas
org_name: MEDICAL COLLEGE OF WISCONSIN
phr: Project Narrative Rural young men who have sex with men (YMSM) have a significant burden of HIV, representing 20% of new HIV cases in nonmetropolitan areas of the United States in 2020. Implementation science will be used to understand how recently approved long-acting injectable pre-exposure prophylaxis (LAI PrEP) can be incorporated into the HIV prevention toolkit for rural YMSM. Using a convergent mixed methods design, we will determine salient patient-level implementation determinants and identify implementation strategies for LAI PrEP among rural YMSM.

application_id: 10785367
activity_code: K99
title: In situ mechanisms of biological nitrogen fixation
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE. Biological nitrogen fixation by nitrogenase sustains life on Earth through the production of metabolically available nitrogen for incorporation into essential biomolecules. The proposed research targets select aerobic and anaerobic diazotrophic bacteria with emerging cryoET methodologies to answer key questions about the nitrogenase enzymatic mechanism in the cellular context. Examining the structure function relationships of nitrogenase in this context will inform on other metalloenzyme mechanisms enzymes relevant to human health, and on the design of synthetic systems for the sustainable production of ammonia for agriculture.

application_id: 11113065
activity_code: K08
title: Immune Evasion through HLA-E in Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Defining Its Regulation and Cellular Interactions
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immune escape fuels cancer progression and therapeutic resistance. This proposed research will apply
functional genetics and immunologic methods to evaluate the emerging role of HLA-E as a mechanism of cancer
immune evasion in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, the most common aggressive lymphoma. This work will
advance our understanding of how HLA-E is independently regulated and its interactions with the immune
system, with the potential to yield new therapeutic opportunities for lymphoma and cancer more broadly.

application_id: 11138209
activity_code: K08
title: The Impact of Discontinuing Medications for Opioid Use Disorder After Surgery: Evaluating Predictors and Outcomes to Guide Perioperative Care
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it will identify risks and modifiable risk factors in the
growing population of surgical patients who use evidence-based medications for opioid use disorder. Given
national efforts to increase utilization of medications for opioid use disorder, this research is timely and will
provide a robust evidence base for targeted specialty care of those who live with opioid use disorder as a
chronic condition and may require surgery during their lifetimes. The project is relevant to NIH’s mission
because it aims to understand how to reduce morbidity and mortality in individuals who suffer from opioid use
disorder, thereby enhancing their health, longevity, and quality of life.

application_id: 11212032
activity_code: K08
title: The Interplay of Mesenchymal Stem Cells and Neutrophils During Rapid Maxillary Expansion
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Patients with maxillary transverse deficiency (MTD) demonstrate varied responses to rapid
maxillary expansion (RME), which is often more challenging in opening the mid-palatal suture in
skeletally mature individuals. This study aims to elucidate the biological role of mesenchymal
stem cells (MSCs) and investigate their interaction with neutrophils during maxillary
expansion/distraction osteogenesis. By uncovering previously unrecognized immune-MSC
modulatory targets, this proposal could pave the way for molecular modulation in RME and suture
growth, leading to improved patient management.

application_id: 10784394
activity_code: K99
title: Neural circuitry and population dynamics for perception of social vocalizations in the auditory pathway
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Social animals depend on vocal communication for survival in many contexts, and the brain must extract information from acoustic features of calls and communicate with circuits for appropriate behavioral responses. Depending on the call meaning, this processing must occur over time scales needed for guiding various behaviors. The research proposed here tests the overall hypothesis that top-down circuits in the auditory system facilitate vocalization-guided behaviors across contexts, using multiple methods for monitoring and manipulating activity in neurons and ensembles to dissect the circuits involved in driving behavior.

org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Fragile X Syndrome, the most common form of inherited intellectual disability and
monogenic cause of autism, is caused by the loss of the RNA-binding protein FMRP. FMRP regulates translation
by cooperating with microRNAs; however, the magnitude of microRNA dysregulation in human-specific models
of FXS remains unclear. This study will determine the extent of microRNA dysregulation in human models of
FXS and reveal whether RNA-binding proteins and microRNAs operate bimodally.

application_id: 11143090
activity_code: K22
title: New Proteomics Approaches to Study the Full Human Kinome, Inhibitor Resistance, and Kinase Degraders
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: Project Narrative
The ability to functionally characterize all human kinases, understand potential mechanisms leading to drug
resistance in cancer therapy and identify new compounds targeting them for degradation is severely hampered
by technological limitations to interrogate all expressed human kinases in a single assay. The objectives of this
proposal are to develop a new targeted kinome assay to enable simultaneous relative and absolute
quantification of all human kinases and then to apply the assay to chemical library screening.

title: Personalized Neuromodulation Targeting Dysregulated Motivational Responses Underlying Social Avoidance Behavior
org_name: AUBURN UNIVERSITY AT AUBURN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Social avoidance behavior (SAB) plays a key role in the development and maintenance of internalizing disorders such as Major Depressive Disorder and Social Anxiety Disorder, but is largely resistant to current treatments. The proposed research seeks to address this issue by using precision neuromodulation to regulate motivational and neural processes that contribute to SAB across internalizing disorders.

application_id: 11114613
activity_code: K08
title: Contribution of Urinary Phosphate to CKD Progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
 Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects approximately 15% of the U.S. population and is a leading cause
of morbidity and mortality due to an exceedingly high rate of cardiovascular, skeletal, hematologic, immune,
and cognitive abnormalities that accompany this disorder. Elevated levels of hormones that increase urinary
phosphate excretion are associated with more rapid CKD progression and adverse clinical outcomes, but the
mechanism for this association is undefined. The proposed studies will define the role of phosphaturia in
mechanisms of CKD progression and determine the therapeutic effect of increasing urinary phosphate
solubility, thus providing compelling data to support future development of innovative therapies to slow CKD
progression.

application_id: 11128325
activity_code: F30
title: The Role of TMEM106B in FTLD-TDP Pathogenesis
org_name: MAYO CLINIC  JACKSONVILLE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in TMEM106B have been reported to alter risk of frontotemporal dementia, and TMEM106B protein aggregates were recently discovered in postmortem brains of individuals with FTD. This proposal aims to improve our understanding of how TMEM106B SNPs alter protein function, and to define disease-specific interactions of TMEM106B fibrils. Together, these data will inform the development of new therapeutic strategies and/or protein targets to combat FTD.

application_id: 11214333
activity_code: K08
title: Novel Approaches to Identifying and Targeting Sepsis-Associated Endothelial Dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Endothelial dysfunction is a major factor in sepsis-associated organ failure, which is the leading cause of
sepsis-related deaths. This study aims to develop targeted therapies for endothelial dysfunction using
advanced human cellular sepsis models, including an endothelial microphysiological system and analysis of
circulating endothelial cells from sepsis patients. By performing RNA sequencing on these cells, we hope to
identify new mechanisms contributing to endothelial dysfunction and organ failure, paving the way for targeted
treatments for sepsis.

application_id: 11230084
activity_code: K08
title: Placental NAD and its effect on labor onset
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Preterm birth is the leading cause of death in children through age 5 worldwide, and our lack of effective
therapies for preterm labor reflects major gaps in our understanding of the mechanisms regulating labor onset,
both at term and preterm. This project will test the hypothesis that dysregulation of key effectors of placental
metabolism is coupled with labor onset. By characterizing the flux of these metabolites across the maternal-
fetal interface, and in elucidating mechanisms by which their dysregulation triggers labor onset in mice, this
project will offer insight into the therapeutic potential for supplementation of specific metabolites in preventing
preterm labor.

application_id: 10763844
activity_code: F30
title: Mechanisms of perivascular melanoma dispersal and survival in the brain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Melanoma has an unusually high propensity to metastasize to the brain and these brain metastases have extremely poor prognosis and limited treatment options. In the brain microenvironment, melanoma cells are frequently found invading deep into the cortex by traveling along the perivascular surface of blood vessels and this vascular-tropic pattern of growth is associated with poor survival in melanoma patients. The experiments in this proposal will explore the contributions of a perivascular-induced TGFβ-PDGFRβ signaling axis and how this signaling cooperates with PTEN loss to sustain invasion and survival in the brain; insights into these mechanisms provide an opportunity to guide the development of unique vascular-directed strategies to prevent or treat brain metastases.

application_id: 10906790
activity_code: F30
title: Using a Molecular Toolkit to Examine Potassium Channel Gating and Regulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative The IKS channel is a voltage-gated potassium channel that is associated with multiple inherited and acquired cardiac arrhythmias, including long QT syndrome, a leading cause of sudden cardiac death. Despite this, critical information about its regulatory mechanisms (both intrinsic and by the sympathetic nervous system) is still unknown, so development of more effective therapies for these arrhythmias is severely handicapped. The objective of this research is to elucidate the mechanism of the channel and how it is regulated by the sympathetic nervous system, with the goal of identifying new targets for drug development and disease prevention.

application_id: 11138344
activity_code: K08
title: Role of KLF6 in Lung Development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: NARRATIVE
 The proposed research is relevant to public health because it focuses on defining the unexplored role of
KLF6 in normal, healthy lung development. Elucidating the mechanisms underlying KLF6 function in lung
development will offer valuable insights into pulmonary diseases and may provide opportunities to advance
treatment approaches for lung diseases that impact individuals of all ages. Thus, the proposed research is
relevant to the part of the NIH’s mission that pertains to seeking fundamental knowledge to enhance health
and reduce illness and disability.

application_id: 11009941
activity_code: F31
title: The role of acetylated microtubules in cell migration
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Relevance to Public Health
Due to its significant involvement in biological processes, cell migration contributes to
disease progression in cancer and arthritis, while its impairment leads to anomalous
tissue development or regeneration. Our approach of combining genetic resources with
live-cell imaging and optogenetic operations will uniquely dissect the molecular
mechanisms of cell migration, and may offer novel mechanistic insights into the cell
migration-related diseases.

application_id: 10785561
activity_code: K08
title: Diversity generation in the gastrointestinal microbiome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Factors that promote microbial colonization in complex and dynamic communities such as the human gastrointestinal microbiome are poorly understood, but knowledge of their functions could lead to therapeutic interventions that reverse dysbiotic states. We predict that rapid gene diversifiers, such as Diversity Generating Retroelements (DGRs), provide bacteria with selective fitness advantages through rapid evolution of ligand binding proteins that facilitate colonization within the gut microbiome. By testing this prediction, we expect to generate a toolkit for engineering adaptative systems in beneficial microbes that allow efficient engraftment in the gut microbiome.

title: Robust and cost-effective computational methods for haplotype-resolved genome assemblies
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative De no!ID haplotype-resolved genome assembly is the most comprehensive solution to understand genomic sequences. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: NARRATIVE Low cardiorespiratory fitness and chronotropic incompetence, the inability to increase heart rate adequately during exercise, may be underrecognized and treatable contributors to cardiovascular disease, especially among people with HIV. application_id: 10804729
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating altered T-cell metabolism during chronic antigen encounter
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: NARRATIVE Novel strategies aimed at reinvigorating anti-tumor immune responses have shown promise for the treatment of cancer, but the majority of patients have only minimal or transient responses to these treatments. We have hypothesized that aberrant T-cell metabolism limits anti-tumor T-cell responses and have identified redox imbalance as a critical regulator of T-cell immunity in experimental systems as well as in primary patient samples. The proposed research will test the importance of oxidative stress as a driver of intratumoral T-cell dysfunction and determine whether metabolically rewiring T-cells to enhance redox homeostasis represents a broadly applicable strategy for improving immune responses in cancer.

application_id: 10763391
activity_code: K25
title: Noninvasive Repositioning of Kidney Stone Fragments with Acoustic Forceps
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative Kidney stones are very common, and most treatments break the stone into smaller fragments that can remain in the kidney and serve as nidi for future stone growth and symptoms recur within 5 years. The goal of this proposal is to overcome the challenges and discover the knowledge needed to develop an ultrasound device known as acoustic forceps to noninvasively target, trap, and reposition the stone from the kidney collecting system in order to facilitate their natural clearance with urine through the ureter. The development of this technology will introduce a fast noninvasive alternative that will eliminate surgical procedures, and reduce the cost associated with stone management protocols while the patient waits to relieve symptoms.

application_id: 11229400
activity_code: K08
title: Reverse translational multi-omics to identify treatment targets for atrial myopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Atrial fibrillation is a serious public health problem because it is the most common arrhythmia and its
prevalence is increasing in the aging population, but emerging evidence suggests that upstream to atrial
fibrillation, structural and functional abnormalities of the atria termed atrial myopathy is a critical factor driving
the adverse cardiovascular and neurocognitive outcomes previously solely associated with atrial fibrillation.
The current research will contribute to fundamental knowledge about proteins and metabolites that contribute
to atrial myopathy. It will also help train the principal investigator in molecular and statistical research
competencies that will enable his transition into an independently funded clinician-investigator.

application_id: 11037911
activity_code: F32
title: Precision Deaminases to Expand the Scope of Diseases Amenable to CRISPR-mediated Correction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Most genetic variants associated with disease are single point mutations which are potentially correctable via systems that exchange a single DNA base (base editors). However, the capacity of this approach is limited by editing promiscuity at neighboring bases which constrains target selection to a narrow range of mutations where nearby off-target edits would not negate the effect of the edit or induce additional pathogenic mutations. This proposal addresses an unmet clinical need for the advent of precision editors using a two-pronged approach that extends existing technology and creates an entirely new kind of editing enzyme with intrinsic properties that prevent off-target edits.

application_id: 11110171
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanical and Metabolic Pathways Underlying MYBPC3 Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) affects about 1 in 500 people, with variants of the myosin-binding protein-
C, cardiac-type (MYBPC3), being the most common genetic cause. MYBPC3-related HCM is characterized by
disordered heart contraction, cardiac hypertrophy, fibrosis, and metabolic dysfunction, but effective
interventions are unclear due to limited understanding of disease progression and mechanisms. The
overarching goal of this proposal is to determine how contractile and metabolic stress drive HCM progression
and test whether targeted contractile and metabolic interventions can prevent or reverse disease.

application_id: 11112773
activity_code: K08
title: Circadian Regulation of Epithelial Immune Reponses in Asthma
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Circadian rhythms underlie numerous cellular functions in the lung, including how airway epithelia respond to
viral infections in health and in diseases such as asthma. The aim of the proposed work is to provide
mechanistic investigations of circadian regulation of epithelial immunity in human airway cells and how altered
responses contribute to asthma and asthma exacerbations. The long term goal will serve an important need in
providing biological knowledge of how to provide the optimal drug, in the optimal patient, at the optimal time of
day.

title: Examining the Longitudinal Influence of the Physical and Social Environments on Social Isolation and Cognitive Health: contextualizing the role of technology.
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The proposed dissertation work will contribute to the fundamental knowledge with longitudinal empirical evidence on the influence of physical, social environmental factors on social isolation and cognitive health, and contextualized the role of technology use. The postdoctoral research will build knowledge on the behavioral and contextual pathways that could explain the association between social isolation and cognitive decline in later life. Knowledge to be built in the predoctoral and postdoctoral stages will be synthesized to inform the development of a technology-facilitated community-based intervention program to reduce social isolation and prevent cognitive decline among older adults.

application_id: 11114348
activity_code: K08
title: Cellular and molecular determinants of an injury-associated capillary endothelial cell state during acute lung injury and regeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
After a severe viral infection there are areas of the lung that never heal properly and do not allow oxygen to enter
the body normally. We have identified a group of lung capillary cells that remain inflamed for up to a year after
influenza infection, in parts of the lung that remain damaged. We hope that studying how these cells develop
and how they relate to immune and inflammatory responses will help identify new ways to promote healing of
chronic lung damage after a viral infection.

title: Integrating deep learning, magnetic resonance imaging and genomics to study myocardial fibrosis
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Myocardial fibrosis is a common feature of multiple cardiovascular diseases and is a hallmark of cardiovascular
aging. The emergence of large biorepositories, such as the UK Biobank, with cardiac magnetic resonance
imaging (MRI) and whole genome sequence data in 100,000 participants, combined with the advent of machine
learning tools capable of generating imaging-based phenotypes at scale, offers a unique opportunity to study
myocardial fibrosis. This proposal leverages cardiac T1 mapping, a validated MRI tool for myocardial fibrosis
quantification, to advance our understanding of the genetic and non-genetic factors influencing myocardial
fibrosis, thus, enabling the development of prevention and therapeutic strategies aimed at mitigating myocardial
fibrosis and its assoc...

application_id: 10804657
activity_code: F31
title: Shear stress-mediated Notch1 activation by intrinsic cell adhesive and cytoskeletal activity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysregulation of vascular barrier function, the selectively permeable barrier established by endothelial cells that line blood vessels to regulate the exchange of fluids, cells, and nutrients, contributes to a variety of cardiovascular, inflammatory, and neurodegenerative diseases. Despite a critical clinical demand, we lack interventional strategies to repair or modulate vascular barrier, largely due to a poor understanding of the master regulators within the vascular endothelium. This proposal aims to reveal new mechanisms of how the Notch1 receptor activates under shear stress, increasing barrier function, and potentially identify new therapeutic targets for modulating barrier function and other vasculopathies where Notch1 is implicated.

Patients with Pretzel Syndrome (also called Polyhydramnios, Megalencephaly, and Symptomatic Epilepsy;
PMSE), a severe disorder of brain development caused by a loss of STRADA protein, suffer frequent seizures
with no effective therapy. Inhibitory neurons serve to regulate brain activity, and these appear to be lacking in the
brains of Pretzel Syndrome patients, contributing to hyperactivity and seizures. This research investigates how
STRADA drives inhibitory neurons during brain development and examines how their loss leads to Pretzel
Syndrome disease characteristics, with the goal of informing the development of more effective treatment.

activity_code: R25
title: Attracting Talented and Diverse Students to Biomedical Informatics and Data Science Careers Through Short-Term Study at OHSU
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The main goal of the Oregon Health & Science University Biomedical Informatics and Data Science College Undergraduate Summer Internship Program is to provide a rich, full-time experience to students to graduate study and careers in biomedical informatics and data science. The funding from this R25 grant will be used to support summer experiences for students with backgrounds that are underrepresented in biomedical informatics and data science. With assistance and coordination from Portland State University, we will support nine students per summer in an experience that includes knowledge and skills development, participation in a research project, a health data ethics and equity seminar, and scientific and career mentoring.

application_id: 10923883
activity_code: K08
title: Targeting the DNA damage response with PARP and ATR inhibition to potentiate cytotoxicity and improve efficacy of immune checkpoint blockade in IDH mutant gliomas
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Immune checkpoint inhibitors have, thus far, been ineffective in the treatment of gliomas, largely due to the immunologically “cold” microenvironment and a low number of neoantigens. Our group recently discovered that IDH1/2 mutations, which are common in gliomas, induce homologous recombination defects and confer sensitivity to DNA damage response inhibitors. The goal of this project is to uncover strategies to exploit IDH1/2-induced DNA repair defects with DNA damage response inhibitors to induce synthetic lethality and generate an immunologically favorable tumor microenvironment thereby improving the response to immune checkpoint inhibition.

application_id: 11099279
activity_code: K12
title: Life-course, exposomics, and analytic program (LEAP) in Women's Health K12
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
For all of the advances we have made in medicine and science, we have seen little improvement
in women's health. Many conditions that burden women either have suboptimal therapies or
ineffective treatments due in part to a profound gap in knowledge regarding the environmental
influences and underlying biology that drives sexually-dimorphic health outcomes across the
lifespan. The Mount Sinai K12 Life-course, Exposomics, and Analytic Program (LEAP) in
Women's Health training initiative will develop transdisciplinary scientists integrating life course
theory, environmental sciences, data science, and translational sex-specific biology to begin to
address these gaps and advance women's health across the lifespan.

activity_code: K08
title: Personalized vasopressor therapy in patients with septic shock
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Septic shock develops when infection overwhelms the body, causing low blood pressure that requires treatment with life-saving medications called vasopressors. The best way to use vasopressors to treat blood pressure in septic shock is unknown. This project aims to better understand how patients respond to vasopressors and predict which medication is best for individuals, which may improve how patients with septic shock are treated.

application_id: 11193007
activity_code: K12
title: CTSA K12 Program State University of New York at Buffalo
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: The overall goal of the University at Buffalo Clinical and Translational Science Institute (CTSI) K12 Mentored Research Career Development Program is to launch translational science careers of rigorously trained researchers and clinician scholars from a wide range of clinical disciplines. The K12 Program will equip them with practical skills and knowledge and provide effective mentoring to address the biomedical, behavioral, clinical and translational science needs of Buffalo, Western New York and the Nation over the next decade. To improve population health and health outcomes, these scholars will translate their research findings into efficient, high quality healthcare for the patients in the city of Buffalo and rural population in Western New York.

activity_code: F31
title: ADAD1 and ribosome biogenesis in the post-meiotic male germ cell
org_name: RUTGERS, THE STATE UNIV OF N.J.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Protein translation is required for normal cell function and is reliant upon proper structure and function of the
ribosome, a macromolecule composed of protein and RNA. This work will inform on the role of a male germ
cell specific RNA binding protein in ribosome biogenesis and whether some male infertility may be due to
abnormal ribosome assembly.

application_id: 10866119
activity_code: K22
title: Identifying and characterizing new therapy targets in TDP-43 proteinopathies
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: As the global population continues to age, neurodegenerative disease is becoming an increasingly prevalent
societal burden for which effective therapies are lacking. While numerous genetic and environmental risk factors
for distinct disorders have been identified, broadly effective therapies must target key, convergent hallmarks of
neurodegeneration observed across patient populations – such as the nuclear clearance and cytoplasmic
aggregation of TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43). Here, I propose to study ATXN3 as a potent modifier of
TDP-43 pathology, to explore post-transcriptional regulation of MARK3 as a novel mechanistic link between
TDP-43 and tau pathology, and to develop a new cellular tool to investigate the mechanism by which TDP-43
mislocalization leads to neuronal death.

application_id: 10824676
activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating the spatially coordinated mechanisms of transcriptional silencing in fragile X syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Fragile X syndrome (FXS), the most common form of heritable intellectual disability, is caused by the expansion of a CGG short tandem repeat (STR) at the FMR1 gene on chromosome X where local DNA methylation silences FMR1 expression. At the FMR1 locus and on multiple autosomes, we have recently found Megabase-scale H3K9me3 domains that form inter-chromosomal contacts in FXS. Using CRISPR/Cas9 engineering of the CGG STR, genomic assays and multimodal single nuclei-imaging, I will elucidate how expansion of the CGG STR at FMR1 spatially coordinates heterochromatinization and 3D genome miswiring.

application_id: 11190346
activity_code: K22
title: Defining the single-cell epigenetic landscape of the HIV-1 reservoir for effective latency reversal
org_name: WISTAR INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Even with the successes of antiretroviral therapy, HIV infection remains a huge burden to persons living with HIV
as HIV infection can persist and rebound from a latent reservoir. A major goal of the field and of the NIH with
recent funding calls is to unlock ways to understand and eliminate the latent HIV reservoir. My proposal seeks
to address this by defining the epigenetic states of the provirus and developing novel ways of reversing latency
as a step towards ending the HIV epidemic.

application_id: 11138347
activity_code: K22
title: Reprogramming myeloid cells by JAK inhibition to enhance checkpoint blockade therapy
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immunotherapies have had a dramatic impact on the treatment of advanced cancers but most patients fail to
respond to currently available immunotherapies. A known mechanism of treatment resistance involves the
expansion of suppressive myeloid cells which interfere with antitumor immunity, but clinically approved
therapies specifically targeting these cells are lacking. Using mouse models of cancer and samples from a
clinical trial with high efficacy in Hodgkin lymphoma patients, the combination of JAK inhibitors with checkpoint
inhibitors will be evaluated as a treatment strategy to reprogram myeloid cells and improve clinical responses
to immunotherapy.

title: Use of donor-derived cell-free DNA for risk stratification and post-treatment monitoring of lung transplant patients at risk for chronic lung allograft dysfunction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it focuses on developing a novel strategy to the
approach of risk stratification and post-treatment monitoring of lung transplant patients based on the degree of
molecular evidence of lung allograft injury. Once such strategies have been developed, there is potential for
significant advancement in the routine monitoring and treatment of lung transplant patients resulting in
decreased mortality and improved quality of life. The proposed research is relevant to the part of NHLBI’s
mission to identify factors that account for individual differences in response to treatments and
develop/optimize novel diagnostic and therapeutic strategies to prevent and treat lung disease.

application_id: 11131220
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the Role of ROR2 in Right Ventricular Failure Pathogenesis
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE STATEMENT
Right ventricular failure (RVF) is a strong predictor of death for which there are no proven therapies and
limited prior work investigating molecular mechanisms of RVF. This career development proposal will allow
the candidate, a physician-scientist with a career objective to transition to an independent investigator of RVF
biology, to examine a novel RVF-specific target that he identified by characterizing the human RVF gene
expression signature. In this proposal, he will use complementary in vitro cardiomyocyte and in vivo RV gain
and loss of function models to determine the mechanistic role of ROR2 in RVF pathogenesis, which could
provide insight for novel RVF therapeutic targets.

title: Illuminating Proximal Relational Dynamics to Enhance Initial Recovery from Alcohol Use Disorder
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alcohol use disorder is accompanied by significant individual and societal costs, and, despite established
evidence-based care, return to alcohol use during initial recovery is common, highlighting the need to understand
novel, malleable factors modulating early recovery success to improve recovery outcomes. Recovery is marked
by shifts from substance-using to substance-free peers; yet the nature of discrete interpersonal interactions,
particularly with close supports such as romantic partners, are likely to modulate recovery outcomes, with critical
or unsupportive interactions serving as barriers in early recovery. This project will use dyadic ecological
momentary assessment (EMA) during initial recovery to illuminate proximal relational dynamics modulating
recovery succ...

title: A digital pill system to measure and support acamprosate adherence in individuals with alcohol associated liver disease
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative
Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a significant public health problem, particularly in individuals with alcoholassociated
liver disease (ALD). Medications, such as acamprosate, have been shown to decrease alcohol
craving, alcohol use, and prevent progression of liver disease; however, adherence is suboptimal. This project
seeks to develop and test a novel digital pill system (DPS) that measures real-time medication ingestion and
pairs it with a cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)-based intervention to provide personalized, data-driven
adherence support. By using direct, real-time medication use data and providing personalized support through
a smartphone intervention, this research has the potential to offer a new and improved way to improve
medication adherence for people with AUD an...

title: Pain Assessment, Treatment, and Functional Outcomes in Hospitalized, Older Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: The assessment and management of pain in older adults is a challenging and consequential task for clinicians, particularly when aging-related conditions or communication issues are involved. Findings from this study will help health leaders and policymakers understand how clinical complexity influences pain care, and clarify the relationship between pain, aging, and functional outcomes. This work will identify areas for systems-level improvement and support future efforts to enhance patient safety, treatment effectiveness, and responsiveness of pain care for older, hospitalized adults.

org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
While over 75% of older adults will visit an emergency department within 6 months of dying, few emergency
physicians incorporate patient values and preferences into their medical decision-making. The proposed
electronic health record-embedded serious illness communication intervention for the emergency department
(SIC-ED) will incorporate proactive patient identification using a prognostic mortality model I developed during
my NIA T32 and Harvard KL2 training as well as a stakeholder-informed, evidence-based communication
guide to support transdisciplinary ED team members including physicians, nurses, and social workers in having
conversations with seriously ill older ED patients to elicit their goals for future care. In alignment with the new

application_id: 11034049
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms Driving the Kinetics of Incretin-Mediated Beta Cell Responses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: The proposed research is relevant to public health because it closes a major gap in knowledge on how similar
incretins elicit starkly different signaling responses in primary beta () cells. This proposed study extends our
current understanding of incretin signaling within primary  cells and integrates this with differences between
incretin-mediated cAMP responses in  cells and the interplay of cAMP and Ca2+ dynamics that dictate the  cell
secretory response to nutrient and incretin co-stimulation. The proposed research is relevant to NIDDK’s mission
because it directly addresses the mechanisms by which incretins potentiate glucose-stimulated insulin secretion,
which can be leveraged to improve therapies for millions of Americans with diabetes.

title: Integration of RNA and Genome Sequences to Identify Genetic Risk in Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
The proposed research is relevant to public health because hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS) is a severe
form of congenital heart disease with 10-year survival of just 40%, and currently only 6% of HLHS patients
have a likely genetic cause identified on exome sequencing. As epidemiologic evidence suggests genetic risk
contributes to the majority of congenital heart disease, I propose that use of machine learning functional
assessment of extensive variants of unknown significance from genomic sequencing and is an important next
step in understanding HLHS. Identifying new genetic causes of HLHS can lead to better diagnosis and
treatment of HLHS patients, as well as individuals with other congenital anomalies, and thus this proposal is
relevant to NIH's goal of fosteri...

application_id: 11165200
activity_code: F31
title: The Joint Effects of Prenatal Pesticide Exposure and Psychosocial Factors on Epigenetic Age Trajectories and Child Psychopathology in a South African Birth Cohort
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Pregnancy is a critical time period for neurodevelopment, as the developing fetus is
uniquely susceptible to environmental neurotoxins such as pesticides and maternal
psychosocial factors. This project aims to investigate the joint effects of prenatal pesticide
exposure and maternal psychosocial factors on child psychopathology and biological
aging, as measured by epigenetic age acceleration trajectories. Identifying risk factors,
high-risk subgroups, and biological trajectories could provide novel insights for the early
detection of disease before developmental problems become apparent and inform early
intervention strategies to improve child brain health outcomes.

title: Novel diagnostic algorithms to improve antimicrobial management for Ugandan patients with chemotherapy-associated febrile illness
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Chemotherapy-associated febrile illness is a major complication of cancer treatment and is associated with
high morbidity and mortality, particularly for patients living in sub-Saharan Africa. The cause of fever in many
cases is unknown, especially in places where the burden of HIV infection is high. For this project, we will
evaluate the causes of fever among people with chemotherapy-associated febrile illness in an HIV and TB

application_id: 10923940
activity_code: K01
title: Impact of Lingual Endurance Exercise on Rehabilitation of Swallowing Impairments after lschemic Stroke
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CINCINNATI
phr: Project Narrative Swallowing impairments (dysphagia) frequently occur after stroke and have devastating consequences on overall health and quality of life. Long-term deficits in swallow function also increase risk of morbidity (e.g. depression) and mortality (i.e. aspiration pneumonia), as such, rehabilitation of swallow function to improve safety and efficiency of swallowing is essential in this population. This study aims to: 1) Provide preliminary data to evaluate the effect of a novel lingual endurance exercise on swallow function in individuals with post-stroke dysphagia; and 2) Collect preliminary data regarding changes in white matter tract diffusion and cortical thickness and from MRI data to better understand effects of lingual exercise training on neuroplasticity.

application_id: 11230423
activity_code: K23
title: Genetic Modifiers and Diagnostics for Bone Health in Cystic Fibrosis
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Osteoporosis afflicts people with cystic fibrosis (CF) at younger ages and higher rates than the general
population, increasing morbidity and mortality in people with CF. This proposal will identify genetic, clinical and
imaging data to predict fracture risk in people with CF to facilitate earlier identification of people with CF at
higher risk for osteoporosis. Using CF as a model to study osteoporosis, these data will reveal osteoporosis
mechanisms, suggest therapeutic targets, and refine osteoporosis screening algorithms.

application_id: 11033443
activity_code: K23
title: Inflammatory Responses, Fibrotic Mediators, and Comorbidity Burden as Determinants of Myocardial Dysfunction and Heart Failure in Rheumatoid Arthritis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Despite advances in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), a heightened risk of heart failure (HF) and HF-
related death persists among people with RA, owed to an incomplete understanding of the inflammatory
pathways and comorbid conditions underlying HF in RA. In this study, the effects of inflammatory and fibrotic
mediators on subclinical myocardial dysfunction will be identified in a new, prospective RA cohort led by the PI
while the impact of comorbidity development on clinical HF onset will be determined using complementary
large, real-world data. The results of this study will inform the development of personalized HF prevention and
management strategies in RA.

application_id: 10983804
activity_code: K99
title: Cross-region neural mechanisms that support conditioned taste aversion
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Humans and other animals learn the value of foods based on their postingestive effects and thereby develop preferences for the taste of foods that are nutrient-rich and aversions towards those that produce illness. This taste learning process is critical for survival but can become maladaptive in eating disorders and obesity and in clinical settings such as during chemotherapy. This proposal will uncover the brainwide neural representations of flavor and test how those representations are reconfigured by the arrival of postingestive feedback from the gut to ultimately shape our preferences and aversions for specific tastes.

application_id: 11010347
activity_code: F31
title: Single-cell optical phenotyping for diffuse gliomas using artificial intelligence and label-free microscopy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Label-free optical microscopy, combined with artificial intelligence (AI), has emerged as a promising method for
the rapid imaging of fresh, unprocessed surgical specimens, bedside brain tumor diagnosis, margin
delineation, and molecular genetic prediction. Existing AI methodologies are limited because they rely on
weak, slide- or patient-level annotations for model training, and are therefore unable to capture the cellular
complexity and spatial heterogeneity found in diffuse gliomas. The objective of this research is to develop an
innovative AI-based system to learn single-cell optical image features and identify patient-level clusters,
without weak supervision, in order to advance the role of AI in diffuse glioma research and enable better, and
more personalized care.

application_id: 11059160
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of cellular senescence and metabolic health by dietary protein
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Aging is the greatest risk factor for many age-related diseases that impair quality of life and is a growing public
health concern in the United States. One hallmark of aging is cellular senescence, which has been shown to
promote age-related pathologies. This proposal will examine how dietary protein and branched-chain amino
acids regulate healthy aging by affecting cellular senescence, with the goal of gaining mechanistic insight into
cellular senescence and aging that will aid in developing geroprotective interventions.

title: Host-pathogen interactions and carbon processing in glycerol kinase deficient Mycobacterium tuberculosis
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Drug tolerance, a non-heritable, reversible state of slow growth which enhances survival to antibiotic treatment,
is a major barrier to successfully treating Tuberculosis (TB), which remains the public health challenge of our
time. This study will identify unique drug targets in a clinically significant, drug tolerant Mycobacterium
tuberculosis (Mtb) strain lacking a key glycerol kinase (glpK), by assessing the role of lipid metabolism and host
macrophage phenotypes on drug tolerance under biologically relevant conditions. This innovative project has
the potential to greatly shorten the length of antibiotic regimens, reducing relapse as well as TB mortality by
producing highly translatable data that will unlock impactful new treatments against a clinically significant str...

application_id: 11161186
activity_code: K08
title: Measurement and Multimethod Validation of Alcohol Use Disorder Etiologic Mechanisms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEW MEXICO
phr: :
Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a major public health concern, yet current diagnostic systems often lack strong theoretical and empirical grounding, leading to issues with validity, heterogeneity, and comorbidity. This project aims to develop a reliable, valid, and efficient computerized adaptive assessment of dimensional AUD mechanisms that are grounded in contemporary theory and informed by individuals with lived experience. By reducing assessment burden while maintaining measurement quality, this tool has the potential to improve clinical decision-making and enhance precision medicine approaches to AUD treatment. Ultimately, this work may support earlier identification, better-targeted interventions, and improved outcomes for all people.

title: A translational evaluation of depressive symptomology and smoking cessation treatment mechanisms for just-in-time-adaptive-interventions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA HLTH SCIENCES CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Individuals with depressive disorders experience higher health burden and mortality from smoking-related
illness and are less likely to quit smoking with treatment. This K23 proposal will evaluate the processes that
may make smoking treatments less effective for individuals with elevated depressive symptoms using a highly
accessible mHealth intervention. This work will inform the development of novel interventions to target these
processes and improve smoking treatment among individuals with depressive disorders.

application_id: 11138143
activity_code: K23
title: Characterizing the transmural nature of ulcerative colitis and its associated disease-related outcomes using intestinal ultrasound: a paradigm shift in treatment targets
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease associated with significant morbidity and gaps in
treatment success. My proposal addresses the discordance between mucosal inflammation and the
observation that UC is actually a transmural disease – an observation which may explain persist symptoms,
comorbidities, and lack of disease control. In this proposal I will use intestinal ultrasound to characterize the
transmural nature of UC, which will lead to new treatment targets for clinical practice and new endpoints for
clinical trials.

application_id: 11230128
activity_code: K23
title: Human Models of Selective Insulin Resistance
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The dual metabolic pandemics of type 2 diabetes (T2D) and metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver
disease (MASLD) share a common basis in insulin resistance (IR). However, IR in the liver appears to be selective:
the compensatory hyperinsulinemia needed to overcome IR to restore euglycemia may drive hepatic triglyceride
synthesis to excess. We will directly test the selective-IR model in humans for the first time, and thereby seek to
demonstrate that liver “insulinization” imposes a tradeoff between controlling T2D and exacerbating MASLD.

application_id: 10906929
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the mechanism of inositol 1,4,5-triphosphate receptor-mediated metastatic liver colonization in colorectal cancer
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Metastasis is the overwhelming cause of death in solid organ tumors like colorectal cancer but remains poorly understood at the molecular level. We have discovered a critical role for the type 3 inositol 1,4,5-triphosphate receptor (ITPR3), a protein involved in controlling intracellular calcium signaling, in facilitating colorectal cancer metastasis to the liver. The proposed research will define the mechanisms by which ITPR3 allows colorectal cancer cells to overcome physiological barriers like extracellular matrix detachment to colonize distant tissues and test its potential as a therapeutic target, leading to innovative strategies to block cancer recurrence or progression.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Males suffering from non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) have limited options to have biological children. org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY

The COPE+ intervention is a promising behavioral/mental health and communication treatment for older adults with cancer and MCI, and their caregivers. activity_code: K08
title: Initiating Colorectal Cancer Screening in Unscreened Individuals 45 to 54
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Community health centers (CHCs) serve a predominantly low-income patient population and have disproportionately low colorectal cancer (CRC) screening rates compared to the general population. Even a delay of a few years for screening initiation can lead to increased CRC incidence and mortality. Using a rigorous mixed methods approach, the proposed research will identify systems-level implementation strategies to initiate CRC screening in previously unscreened individuals 45 to 54 at a CHC with a predominantly low-income patient population, and advance our understanding of strategies to increase CRC screening in the newly eligible population aged 45 to 49 at CHCs.

activity_code: F31
title: Exercised-induced modulation of insular cortex microcircuitry during alcohol abstinence
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alcohol Use Disorder is a major public health concern in the United States and the causation of relapse can be
explained by active avoidance of stress and anxiety. My project focuses on how exercise might manipulate a
specific brain region to prevent these feelings of stress and anxiety during alcohol abstinence. In doing so, we
hope to identify a simple treatment to prevent relapse during alcohol use disorder.

title: Whole-Person Health and Brain Health Outcomes of Mindfulness Training in Asthma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
Asthma is a highly prevalent chronic inflammatory disease of the airways that has been increasingly shown to
impact the brain; it is sensitive to changes in stress, often comorbid with anxiety and depression, and has been
associated with compromises in brain structure and function. The proposed project seeks to benefit public
health by investigating the impact of a Mindfulness Based Stress Reduction intervention on brain health and
whole-person health in a population with asthma. We will assess changes in a combination of neuroimaging
metrics of brain microstructural integrity, blood-based biomarkers of brain health, asthma symptoms and level
of airway inflammation, as well as a host of whole-person health indices such as changes in sleep quality, life
stress, and psychol...

application_id: 11035107
activity_code: DP1
title: Harnessing Highly Networked HLA-E-Restricted CTL Epitopes to Achieve a Broadly Effective HIV Cure
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The high level of HIV variability and the polymorphic nature of MHC-Ia alleles are major challenges to the development of a broadly effective CTL-based vaccine. This proposal aims to overcome these obstacles by leveraging the conserved MHC-1b allele HLA-E and mutation-resistant HLA-E-restricted CTL epitopes in a rationally designed, therapeutic CTL-based vaccine for HIV. Thus, the proposed work is relevant to the NIH’s mission to seek and apply knowledge to lengthen life and reduce illness.

application_id: 11112045
activity_code: K23
title: Use of Adjuvant Anti-Obesity Medication to Enhance Surgical Weight Loss
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative: Use of Adjuvant Anti-Obesity Medication to Enhance Surgical Weight Loss
 K23 Principal Investigator: Jason M. Samuels, MD
Obesity affects nearly half of the U.S. population, increasing the risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease
and mortality. While bariatric surgery (e.g., gastric bypass) stands as the most effective weight loss therapy,
40% of individuals experience persistent obesity after bariatric surgery. This study will determine whether
weight loss with surgery can be enhanced with the addition of medications, allowing for future studies to
determine the optimal measure and degree of weight loss needed for optimal health.

title: A multi-omic investigation of uremic toxin sources and accumulation in children with kidney failure
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 In children with kidney failure, understanding from where uremic toxins originate and which factors

contributes to uremic toxin burden in children with kidney failure needs to be characterized. Differences in toxin
profiles between children with acute versus chronic kidney failure need to be described.

title: CSHL PREP

phr: The Postbaccalaureate Research Education Program (PREP) at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory (CSHL) will provide participants with (1) rigorous research experience in genetics and genomics, cancer and molecular biology, neuroscience, quantitative biology, and plant biology, (2) professional skills development, and (3) development of a scientific identity and community through the efforts of a Research Mentor, Direct Mentor and a Near-Peer Mentor, and the immersive and supportive environment of CSHL. application_id: 11094684
activity_code: F31
title: Applying Harm Reduction to Binge Eating Disorder: An Investigation of Protective Behavioral Strategies Against Uncontrolled Eating
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CENTRAL FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The current proposal seeks to enhance the training of the applicant through formal and informal training
experiences in eating pathology, qualitative analysis, psychometrics, and advanced nested data methodology.
The research project seeks to develop a new measure of protective behaviors, derived primarily from non-binge
eating individuals, that can be applied in a binge eating sample to reduce the deleterious effects of this behavior.
The final set of protective behaviors will take a harm reduction approach and serve as intervention targets for a
fairly intractable psychological disorder with significant public health implications.

application_id: 11093308
activity_code: F31
title: The Relationship Between Historical Trauma, Substance Use, and Suicidal Ideation in Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander Adults: A Mixed-Methods Approach
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF RHODE ISLAND
phr: Project Narrative
 Due to small sample sizes and the persistent aggregation of Asian American and Pacific Islander data,
there is a significant dearth of research on substance use and suicidal ideation in Native Hawaiian/Pacific
Islander adults. The proposed project will: (1) provide essential training in conducting ethical research and
collaborating with this highly minoritized community and (2) use a sequential mixed-methods methodology
paired with community-based participatory research to explore the relationships between substance use,
suicidal ideation, and historical trauma among Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islanders. Results will fill significant
gaps in the extant literature related to health disparities within this understudied community.

application_id: 10925155
activity_code: F31
title: Molecular and cancer-related roles of ACLY exon 14
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative ACLY is a key metabolic and epigenetic mediator whose dysregulation has been implicated in diseases such as cancer. The full-length protein is preferentially expressed in tumors over another major splice isoform, but ACLY isoforms have not been closely studied. The goal of my project is to understand the structural, molecular, and biological impacts of ACLY alternative splicing, which will provide insight into the tumor-supporting roles of ACLY and potentially lead to new therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 11094933
activity_code: F31
title: Hitting Close to Home: A Multi-Method Investigation of Neighborhood Characteristics and Drinking Motives on Alcohol-Related Health Disparities
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Racial disparities in alcohol use behaviors and outcomes are a significant public health concern in the United
States, particularly among historically marginalized populations. This research will use an innovative mixed-
methods approach to examine the relationship between neighborhood characteristics (e.g., neighborhood
disorganization and deprivation), and the role of drinking motives, on alcohol consumption and alcohol-related
consequences among Black adults. Findings may encourage health researchers and professionals to address
community-related barriers maintaining alcohol use-related inequities and enable the development of pertinent,
culturally sensitive, community-driven interventions and policies to eliminate such disparities.

activity_code: R25
title: Cancer Aging and Metabolism research experience Program (CAMP)
org_name: BECKMAN RESEARCH INSTITUTE/CITY OF HOPE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Almost all human cancers have alterations in metabolic pathways associated with aging and cancer signaling, but undergraduates rarely receive cross-training in cancer, aging, and metabolism research. The proposed Cancer Aging and Metabolism research experience Program (CAMP) will address the need for a well-trained workforce by providing a unique 10-week hands-on learning experience for 20 undergraduates each summer. This first iteration of CAMP will also pilot the integration of school-year academic and research activities with California State University, Dominguez Hills in an effort to bolster the pipeline for undergraduates to thrive as future cancer researchers.

activity_code: F32
title: Glutamine Metabolism in Alveolar Macrophages following Influenza A Infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Influenza A-induced Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (IAV-induced ARDS) is associated with 45% mortality and current treatment is supportive. Glutamine metabolism plays a key role in immune function and viral replication but is understudied in IAV-induced ARDS. This project will target the glutamine pathway to identify key sites of metabolic regulation in alveolar macrophage effector function and viral replication, which will serve as the basis for my transition to become an independent investigator.

title: Cellular-molecular regulation of isoform-dependent tau dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hyperphosphorylation of microtubule (MT) associated protein Tau is tightly correlated with Alzheimer’s disease
progression; however, studies of isoform-specific effects of Tau function and dysfunction are largely unexplored.
I discovered that phosphorylation differentially regulates adult and fetal Tau isoforms: Tau isoforms have distinct
phosphorylation patterns in neurons, and specific phosphorylation sites impact Tau-MT interactions in an
isoform-dependent manner. I uniquely propose to use high-throughput pooled optical CRISPR screening and
CRISPR prime editing to contrast the effects of Tau phosphorylation (AIM 1) and regulators of Tau-MT
interactions (AIM 2) on its function and dysfunction between fetal and adult Tau isoforms, thus providing insight
into both loss-of...

activity_code: F31
title: The Role of Dedifferentiation in Basal like Breast Cancer
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Given the prevalence and disproportionate mortality associated with Basal-like Breast Cancer (BLBC) compared to other breast cancer subtypes, investigating its biology is of paramount importance to women’s health. This proposal investigates the BLBC founder cell and the aberrant dedifferentiation that promotes cellular plasticity and treatment resistance. Findings from these studies will enhance our understanding of gene regulatory networks responsible for multipotency reactivation in the adult mammary gland and could lead to advancements in the treatment of highly plastic malignancies.

title: Characterizing a mechanism of enhancer-promoter interaction in vivo
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Disrupted enhancer activity is associated with a wide variety of pathologies, from congenital disorders to cancer,
however, the mechanisms which regulate enhancer activity are still poorly understood. In this proposal, I seek
to characterize a newly identified regulatory element that has been shown to be necessary and sufficient for
long-range enhancer activity. My findings should provide insight into a new mechanism of gene regulation during
development.

application_id: 11162320
activity_code: K23
title: Comprehensive Deep Phenotyping and Multi-omics to Develop Clinical and Molecular Biomarkers for MeCP2-related Diseases
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project narrative
MECP2 Duplication Syndrome (MDS) and Rett Syndrome (RTT) are allelic diseases,
caused, respectively, by too much and too little MeCP2. MDS is now poised for clinical
trials of disease-altering treatments such as anti-sense oligonucleotides (ASO), yet we
lack reliable biomarkers to prevent over-suppression of MeCP2 (which would produce
Rett symptoms). We have identified several molecules that track with MeCP2 levels in
both mice and human MDS subjects, and will validate these biomarkers and our clinical
measures in the largest cohort of MDS subjects collected to date.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cellular immunotherapies such as transgenic T-cell receptor (TCR) T-cells are revolutionizing the treatment of multiple cancer types, but there remain significant incidences of treatment failure or relapsed disease after an initial clinical response. title: Accurate interaction detection in combinatorial screens with complex phenotypes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Detecting interactions between cellular perturbations requires an appropriate model for non-interaction (i.e., a null interaction model). A key challenge is the design of null interaction models for biological assays with high- dimensional phenotypes, in which individual features exhibit unknown, non-linear behavior. I propose to establish an analytical approach to construct null interaction models for high-dimensional phenotypes and perform drug target identification using high-dimensional, chemical-genetic interactions.

title: REACH BP: REmote physical ACtivity intervention for High Blood Pressure Postpartum
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: This research is relevant to public health because many young women have high blood pressure in pregnancy, and young women who have high blood pressure in pregnancy are more likely to develop heart disease in their lifetime. While physical activity is a well-established strategy to lower blood pressure, only one-quarter of women meet recommended physical activity levels in the first-year after delivery. This study will develop and pilot a novel postpartum physical activity intervention that links FitBit data with the Electronic Health Record to prevent hypertension, thus aligning with federal funding priorities to reduce the burden of chronic disease.

application_id: 10763818
activity_code: F30
title: Copper Sensing in Uropathogenic Escherichia coli
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal seeks to determine how bacteria sense and respond to changes in copper levels during urinary tract infection (UTI) and intestinal colonization. Copper levels increase in the urinary tract during infection as a protective mechanism against bacterial pathogens, yet the primary cause of UTIs, the bacteria called Escherichia coli have mechanisms to overcome copper stress and persist in the host. Our work will illuminate the pathways used by Escherichia coli to tolerate copper, opening new avenues for targeting bacterial copper detoxification mechanisms as a novel antimicrobial strategy.

title: The Impact of Positive Airway Pressure (PAP) Therapy on Myocardial Blood Flow in Adults with Obstructive Sleep Apnea at High-Risk of Cardiovascular Disease: The PAP-Flow Study
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Damage to the small blood vessels of the heart, also known as coronary microvascular dysfunction, is common
in people with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and contributes to the development of heart failure. This study
will determine if low oxygen and abnormal heart rate changes measured during a sleep test are linked to
coronary microvascular dysfunction. It will also determine how soon coronary microvascular dysfunction
improves after starting OSA treatment with positive airway pressure therapy and whether this improves heart
function.

application_id: 10925254
activity_code: R25
title: Minnesota Cancer Research And Teaching Excellence: M-CREATE
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: University of Minnesota Cancer Research And Teaching Excellence: M-CREATE is a theoretically guided, evidence driven program designed to support the academic persistence of high school and undergraduate students underrepresented in science, and promote their developmental progress towards future careers in cancer research. M-CREATE will combine best practices with innovative approaches from education, mentorship, service learning, and parent education within an equity-oriented frame to engage, educate, and support students to achieve the knowledge, skills, science identity, and self-efficacy required to support academic persistence and long-term success within the biomedical and behavioral sciences. org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Primary aldosteronism (PA), although once considered a rare disease, is estimated to affect 5-10% of patients with hypertension. title: Multispecies investigation of early pregnancy loss mechanisms using bioengineered stem cell models
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Early pregnancy loss can be a devastating outcome for people with the ability to become pregnant, and nearly two-thirds of all losses occur prior to and around the time of implantation. Extrapolation of data from animal models of implantation is challenging at this stage owing to the lack of knowledge regarding conservation or divergence of these mechanisms across key mammalian species. Through understanding how morphogen and mechanical cues via WNT/β-catenin signaling orchestrate correct embryo development at this period across species, we will be able to confidently extrapolate data from animal models towards human health applications to better understand the root causes of pregnancy loss.

activity_code: K99
title: Nuclear and chromatin aberrations during non-apoptotic cell death in C. elegans and mammals
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Dysregulation of programmed cell death accompanies many disease states, where abnormal death leads to organ and tissue degeneration, while insufficient death leads to cancer. Patients with Huntington’s disease, aggressive cancers, progeria and laminopathies display morphological features of novel non-apoptotic Linker Cell-type Death (LCD) discovered in C. elegans. The discovery of mammalian LCD genes would have immediate applications, from development of new markers to distinguish when LCD is employed in vertebrate development and disease, to suggesting methods for inducing and inhibiting LCD, with a long-term goal of providing in-roads for therapeutics development.

phr: Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) is the most common cause of surgically-remediable, pharmacoresistant

activity_code: K23
title: Outcome measures and quantitative EEG biomarkers in SYNGAP1-related disorders
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health as rare genetic epilepsies, when taken together, present a
significant burden to human disease. Precision medicine therapies are currently under development for many of
these conditions, but most lack the detailed natural history data, well-defined outcome measures, and biomarkers
necessary for clinical trial readiness. This research is relevant to the purpose of the NIH K23 Award and mission
of the NINDS by providing a tailored program to enhance the applicant’s skills in prospective clinical
assessments, digital health tools tracking seizure outcomes, and quantitative EEG in a large cohort of individuals
with SYNGAP1-related disorders in order to use this knowledge to reduce the burden of neurological disease.

application_id: 10994164
activity_code: F31
title: Exploring corticostriatal dynamics associated with action control deficits in Neurexin1 alpha mutant mice
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Many prevalent neurodevelopmental disorders such as autism spectrum disorder, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, and Tourette syndrome share common characteristics including impulsivity and repetitive action patterns. These behaviors may originate through the dysregulation of action control mechanisms, however, the circuit alterations that give rise to these action control deficits are not well understood. This proposal aims to provide valuable insight into the neural pathology involved in these disorders, as well as elucidate the neural mechanisms involved in normal action control regulation.

title: Dissecting the Heterogeneous Molecular Mechanisms Underlying Multiple Sclerosis Progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
The driving mechanisms behind the irreversible progression in multiple sclerosis remain largely unknown.

involved during the crucial period before the onset of disease progression. The insights gained from this study
can help to tailor targeted and personalized therapeutic strategies.

activity_code: F31
title: HMGB1 in EB-Associated Squamous Cell Carcinoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Public Health Relevance
Squamous cell carcinoma arising in chronic wounds is the leading cause of death in patients with recessive
dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa. This project aims to elucidate the mechanisms underlying cancer initiation in
these patients by interrogation of HMGB1, a protein involved in both inflammation and genome maintenance.
The results from this study will provide a foundation for new drug targets in this fragile patient population and will
shed new light on carcinogenesis in the broader context of inflammatory disease.

application_id: 11283030
activity_code: K23
title: Quantitative Gait Metrics to Assess and Predict Gross Motor Impairment in Young Children with Neurofibromatosis Type 1
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) is one of the most common hereditary neurological disorders in the United
States and is associated with early and persistent gross motor difficulties. This study will evaluate office-based
measures of gait speed and interlimb coordination computed from videos of children with NF1, to develop
objective, accessible metrics of gross motor impairment. These measures may be used to improve NF1
developmental monitoring, shape counseling on motor outcomes, inform decisions about physical therapy or
existing interventions, and serve as potential endpoints in precision therapy clinical trials.
