application_id: 11125977
activity_code: K99
title: Ribonucleoprotein complexes involved in the late phase of HIV-1 replication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE COUNTY
phr: Projective Narrative
During HIV-1 virus assembly, two copies of the genomic RNA are selectively packaged by a small number of
viral Gag polyproteins. Understanding the molecular determinants of this complex that functions as a nucleant
for further virus assembly, as well as the understanding of its interactions with host cell factors will extend not
only our current understanding about the late phase of infection of HIV-1, but also lead to the development of
novel strategies for the treatment of AIDS and potentially even improved gene delivery vectors with greater
packaging efficiencies.

application_id: 11252910
activity_code: K24
title: Mentoring the next generation of patient-oriented translational virologists
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The primary goal of this project will be to provide mentorship to trainees and early career investigators in
clinical studies and cutting-edge translational virology techniques in key priority areas of HIV research.
Trainees will help lead research to 1) Determine the virologic features of HIV persistence, 2) reservoir
mediators of post-treatment HIV control, and 3) Assess the impact of emerging HIV resistance to new
antiretroviral agents. The results of the studies will also advance our understanding of the mechanisms of HIV
persistence, post-treatment control, and the clinical impact of antiviral drug resistance.

application_id: 11003665
activity_code: F31
title: The Roles of Rnt1 and Putative Endoribonucleases in mRNA Processing and Degradation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mutations in RNA processing and degradation enzymes (RNases) have been shown to cause human disease. Yet, there are many proteins involved in RNA degradation and processing pathways that have not been previously identified as RNases, and for some enzymes that have been identified, their targets and mechanisms of action remain obscure. This study will characterize a group of RNases to better understand the link between dysfunctional RNA processing and degradation, and the development of human disease.

application_id: 11018517
activity_code: K01
title: Evaluating Biologic and Sociodemographic Factors to Improve Diabetes Prevention in Adults
org_name: KAISER FOUNDATION RESEARCH INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Engagement in evidenced-based diabetes prevention programs has historically been low, particularly in the
Latino population, who are disproportionally affected by type 2 diabetes. Identifying both clinical and social
determinants of health factors that increase diabetes risk and act as barriers for participation in prevention
programs is crucial to improve equitable access to diabetes prevention programs. The proposed research aims
to use clinical and social determinants of health data readily available in the electronic health record to develop
and validate a diabetes prediction model specific to the needs of Latino patients, and engage with patients,
clinicians and health care systems to increase enrollment and engagement in diabetes prevention programs.

application_id: 11023129
activity_code: K01
title: Identifying patient subgroups that are most likely to benefit from medications used to treat alcohol use disorder
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alcohol use disorder is a debilitating disease, affecting nearly 16 million people in the United States, for which medication treatment is available but underutilized. To support alcohol use disorder medication treatment, this study will utilize multiple data sources, including clinical trial and real-world data, outcome measures, and methodology to identify patient subgroups that are most likely to benefit from each medication used to treat alcohol use disorder. The goal of this award is to clarify uncertainties among clinicians and patients about whether and for whom treatment may work, which can inform clinical practice and increase the adoption of alcohol use disorder medications.

application_id: 10763858
activity_code: K24
title: The effects of neural modulation on phonatory function in laryngeal dystonia
org_name: MGH INSTITUTE OF HEALTH PROFESSIONS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Adductor laryngeal dystonia (AdLD) is a disorder that causes uncontrolled spasms of the `vocal cords' during speech, causing severe speaking difficulty and a reduction in quality of life. Although the cause of the disorder is not fully understood and there are no treatments for AdLD that produce long-term benefits, our recent findings have discovered that AdLD is associated with a lack of neural inhibition that may be linked to the spasms. The current study builds on these findings to determine if repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is effective for increasing neural inhibition, enhancing voice function and improving quality of life for people with AdLD and other voice disorders or types of dystonias.

activity_code: T32
title: Training in Translating Neuroscience to Therapies
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative The number of people in the world suffering from neurological disorders is increasing rapidly, and many of these diseases have no good treatments much less cures. application_id: 10805404
activity_code: F31
title: Identifying the Role of Serotonergic Signaling in the Maturation ofDendritic Spines
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Cognitive disabilities, such as those observed in developmental disorders like Autism, are often marked by improper synaptic connections between neurons. Although there are clear links between abnormal synaptic development and behavioral deficits persisting into adulthood, the specific cellular and molecular mechanisms that underly these deficits are not well understood. Disbalances of the neuromodulator serotonin have been associated with improper circuit formation in the brain and an increased likelihood of developing cognitive disabilities; characterizing the role of serotonin in proper synaptic development will help further our understanding of the cellular and molecular etiologies of developmental disorders.

application_id: 11114656
activity_code: K24
title: ReMOTE-BP: Research and Mentorship for Optimizing Treatment and Evaluation of Blood Pressure
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: NARRATIVE
Team-based, remote-accessible care for chronic hypertension promises to better meet the needs of patients and
improve clinical outcomes, particularly among those served by safety net health systems. This work proposes a
systematic analysis of the costs of implementing such interventions and considers the relationship between
providing behavioral care for depression or anxiety and the clinical outcomes of those with chronic hypertension.
The results will inform how novel team-based interventions are implemented within the unique context of
resource-constrained safety net health systems. Conducting this research will enhance the skills of early-career
investigators committed to using innovative digital tools to address health disparities.

activity_code: K25
title: Impact of Local vs. Systemic Senescent Bone Cell Accumulation on Healthy Osteocyte Mechanosensation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project investigates how local and systemic accumulation of senescent cells (SnCs) impairs osteocyte
mechanobiology within the aging bone microenvironment. By elucidating the specific mechanisms through which
SnCs disrupt the mechanobiology of healthy osteocytes and bone homeostasis, this research will pave the way
for therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring osteocyte function to combat age-related bone loss. The training
provided through this award will also equip me with advanced experimental skills in cellular senescence, enabling
me to extend this research into future R01 projects focused on innovative treatments for skeletal aging.

activity_code: K43
title: Clonotyping Airway T-cells to Uncover Microbiome-Specific Inflammotypes in HIV-associated COPD
org_name: MAKERERE UNIVERSITY LUNG INSTITUTE LIMITED
phr: Project Narrative
Sub-Saharan Africa, with the highest density of people living with HIV, has experienced dramatic increases in
COPD mortality, driven by persistent airway inflammation, despite virologic suppression. Mechanistically, the
drivers of this persistent inflammation are not fully known. In this proposal, we will characterise airway
microbiome-induced immune responses to underpin specific microbiome-immune signatures (referred to as
inflammotypes) driving persistent inflammation in COPD among HIV-infected individuals in a rural Ugandan
cohort.

title: Hydrogen Sulfide for the Prevention of Cisplatin-Induced Atherosclerosis
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Cisplatin is a commonly used chemotherapeutic whose use is associated with acute coronary syndrome (ACS)
in cancer survivors. Our proposed study examines the molecular mechanism by which cisplatin induces foam
cell death and its effects on plaque instability in vivo. Furthermore, we will assess the therapeutic potential of
H2S against cisplatin-induced ACS, thus making strides to increase the quality of life and longevity of cancer
survivors.

application_id: 11085299
activity_code: F31
title: Investigation of acute and long-term neuroimmune changes induced by early-life opioid exposure and withdrawal
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
The number of infants diagnosed with Neonatal Opioid Withdrawal Syndrome (NOWS) continues to rise,
though the full extent of long-term molecular and behavioral changes induced by NOWS is still unknown.
Opioids are known modulators of the immune system, and clinical data supports immune dysfunction in
patients previously diagnosed with NOWS. This proposal aims to fully characterize the molecular and
behavioral manifestations of neuroimmune alterations induced by NOWS, and to investigate the novel
pharmacologic application of a microglial-targeting drug in treating symptomology.

application_id: 10764254
activity_code: F31
title: Bacterial metabolism of catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors alters drug efficacy and toxicity
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Parkinson’s disease (PD) is a devastating disease with no cure, and patients rely on a combination of drugs to reduce symptoms and maintain quality of life. One class of drugs used in PD therapy, catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors (COMT-I), exhibit interindividual differences in drug efficacy, adverse side effects, and microbiome disruption. This project will dissect how metabolism of COMT-I drugs by the gut microbiome impacts drug efficacy, toxicity, and microbiome disruption.

title: Exploiting Pf phage superinfection to lower Pseudomonas aeruginosa virulence via evolutionary tradeoffs
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal aims to understand the evolutionary tradeoff between bacterial fitness and phage intracellular competitiveness against other phages when multiple phages infect the same cell. application_id: 11179205
activity_code: K99
title: Non-alcoholic beverages: A foundational assessment of their potential utility in reducing alcohol use
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative. While alcohol misuse remains a leading cause of preventable death and disability, non-alcoholic
beverages that seek to mimic alcohol (e.g., non-alcoholic beers, non-alcoholic wines, non-alcoholic liquor, and
“mocktails”) are drastically increasing in popularity, especially among people who consume alcohol. This project
will examine whether non-alcoholic beverage consumption helps to reduce alcohol use or paradoxically stimulate
it. Clarifying the potential utility of non-alcoholic beverages and whether their effects differ based on alcohol use
history will ultimately inform clinical recommendations for people with alcohol use disorder and public health
messaging at large.

Dementias (AD/ADRD) at the population level. Social policies have been designed to reduce poverty, but
their impact on AD/ADRD risk and disparities remain under explored. The proposed research will capitalize on
policy variation across states and countries to quantify the effects of poverty alleviation policies on AD/ADRD
burden and disparities, bridging the gap between social epidemiology and policy to advance AD/ADRD
prevention.

application_id: 11159311
activity_code: F31
title: Hippocampal dopamine signaling and food-related memory: implications for obesity
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative
Over 40% of American adults are obese and, due to a lack of effective and affordable treatments for weight loss,
the obesity epidemic remains a critical public health crisis. Novel and effective obesity prevention and
treatment strategies are aided by basic science research identifying specific brain chemical systems that reduce
food intake, and one hypothesis about obesity etiology is it is driven by impaired dopaminergic reward
networks engaged during the consumption of highly palatable food. Here, we propose to investigate how
dopamine signaling in the dorsal HPC is connected with the higher-order control of food intake and how
presynaptic DA release and postsynaptic DA receptor signaling in this region may be impaired in models of
obesity.

application_id: 11138208
activity_code: K99
title: B cell dynamics and immune memory formation in human vaccination
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Vaccination prevents millions of deaths each year by generating protective immune memory against viruses or
bacteria. Most of what we know about the long-lived immune cells that provide this protection has been learned
from studying mice, but the many differences in human and mouse biology have limited our ability to translate
mouse research findings into human vaccination strategies. We are now applying a broad range of cutting-
edge experimental techniques to rigorously analyze how human immune cells generate immune memory after
vaccination and how that immune memory formation can be improved by including different vaccine
components.

application_id: 10927436
activity_code: K22
title: Prognostic Radiomic Signatures in Prostate Cancer Patients on Active Surveillance
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Prostate cancer is the second most common cancer diagnosed in males worldwide, and a significant cause of morbidity and mortality. At this time, there is a significant unmet need for identifying men at initial diagnosis who will develop more aggressive disease possibly leading to symptoms, metastases, or death. This project proposes the combination of clinically established prognostic biomarkers and radiomic signatures extracted from magnetic resonance imaging of the prostate gland, co-registered with diffusion basis spectrum imaging (DBSI), and matrix- assisted laser desorption ionization (MALDI) imaging, to optimally select candidates for active surveillance from males being assessed at initial diagnosis.

application_id: 11230539
activity_code: K99
title: A Novel Strategy to Identify Protective T cell Epitopes for HPV Therapeutic Vaccine
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Given the large burden of existing global HPV infection cases (>200 million), the
development of a therapeutic vaccine that is able to clear HPV infection in infected females would have a major
health impact and is highly aligned with part of the NIH’s mission. T cell-based vaccine is a promising strategy
to design therapeutic vaccines against HPV and the identification of protective T cell antigenic epitopes is the
basis of the rational design of HPV T cell-based therapeutic vaccines. This proposed study will establish a novel
strategy with cutting-edge technologies to pave a path to identify protective T cell epitopes for T cell-based
vaccines, not only for HPV infection but also for viral infection in general.

application_id: 10789878
activity_code: R25
title: Research Innovation in NeuroScience Education for Underserved Populations (RISE UP)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CINCINNATI
phr: The Research Innovation in NeuroScience Education for Underserved Populations (RISE UP) program will support our efforts to equip outstanding undergraduate students from diverse and/or under-privileged backgrounds with research, professional, and social-emotional skills to thrive in cutting-edge neuroscience graduate programs. RISE UP scholars will be engaged in a rigorous summer program in neuroscience that will not only allow them to strengthen their research and professional development skills but will also address socio-emotional and cultural issues that may be unique to them. This innovative approach allows us to intervene early on in the educational pipeline and arm our undergraduate scholars with the necessary tools to adjust to the diverse rigors of graduate school.

application_id: 11242474
activity_code: F31
title: The role of VacA in Helicobacter pylori-host cell cholesterol homeostasis
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Infection with Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium that colonizes the stomachs of half the world’s population, is the
primary risk factor for the development of peptic ulcers and gastric cancer. This proposal will examine how H.
pylori uses its secreted toxin, VacA, to manipulate host cell cholesterol metabolism in order to acquire an
essential nutrient. By uncovering mechanisms of VacA-induced cholesterol dysregulation, this research will
provide fundamental insights into the pathogenesis of H. pylori infection and H. pylori-mediated gastric diseases.

application_id: 10789997
activity_code: K25
title: Agitation in Alzheimer's Disease: Identification and Prediction Using Digital Behavioral Markers and Indoor Environmental Factors
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Agitation is one of the most common and unmanageable neuropsychiatric symptoms among persons with dementia, affecting 45-83% of this ever-growing population. Pharmacological treatment of agitation often has adverse side effects, and time-constrained caregivers cannot reasonably be expected to monitor the patients and take continuous note of the environment to identify precipitants of agitation. The research and training for this Mentored Quantitative Research Development Award will be centered on identifying digital behavioral markers both during and preceding agitation, identifying environmental precipitants of agitation, and building risk prediction models of agitation using data collected from patients with later-stage dementia and their living environment.

activity_code: K99
title: Differential sorting and trafficking of Plasmodium falciparum secreted proteins.
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: During its replication within human erythrocytes, the malaria parasite Plasmodium
falciparum releases hundreds of proteins into the host cell, and these proteins play a crucial role in the
development of diverse clinical complications associated with severe malaria. This study will investigate the
mechanism governing the selection and trafficking of those proteins at the host-parasite interface, thus
enhancing our understanding of this critical pathway for malaria pathogenesis. In the long term, insights gained
from this research will inform the efforts of designing therapeutic interventions, ultimately improving our ability to
manage and treat malaria.

title: Determining the role of maternal gut associated metabolites in thalamocortical circuit development and adolescent somatosensory behavior
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Public Health Relevance
NDDs arise from gestational and early life disruptions in key processes including axonogenesis and synaptic
remodeling, leading to significant neurological disabilities like sensory processing disorder. As deficits in
sensory processing emerge as early as 6 months old and become most pronounced during adolescence, the
most effective therapies for NDD rely on early identification and intervention. This proposal will provide much
needed insight into the mechanism of prenatal neurodevelopmental and postnatal neurobehavioral
consequences following disruptions to the gestational maternal microbiome by identifying microbiome-based
interventions.

title: Impacts of non-antibiotic medications on microbiome-mediated protection against intestinal pathogens
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A fundamental understanding of how intestinal bacteria and the host immune system resist gut pathogen
colonization is essential in developing new therapeutics against gut infections. Non-antibiotic medications can
disrupt the microbiome-mediated protection barrier and lead to intestinal diseases. The proposed studies will
identify mechanisms through which non-antibiotic prescription medications can disrupt the gut microbiome and
will provide avenues to generate microbiome-based therapeutics against infectious gastrointestinal diseases.

application_id: 10789873
activity_code: F31
title: Reversal of Age-Associated Damage in the Planarian Germline
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Age-related conditions are among the most devastating and agonizing diseases worldwide, with age- related declines in fertility being among the earliest and most frequent of these conditions. My proposed research studies the mechanisms by which aging damages the germline of the planarian S. mediterranea, and determines how regeneration reverses this damage and restores the flatworms to youthful levels of fecundity. This research will help us understand why humans and other organisms are unable to reverse germline aging, and could lay the groundwork for new treatments that preserve and restore fertility even into advanced age.

application_id: 10927407
activity_code: K01
title: Advancing Equity in Early Childhood Developmental Screening Through Item Response Theory and User-Centered Design
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative Beginning in early childhood, children living in low-income circumstances are twice as likely to experience developmental delay in at least one domain including motor, cognitive, communication, and social-emotional; for Black and Latino children, this risk is compounded by structural racism. Further, racial and linguistic disparities exist in both diagnosis of delay and services received. To address these inequities, I will utilize Item Response Theory to design a new developmental assessment computer adaptive test that serves two functions: 1) determination of a child’s development status, and 2) inform primary care-based developmental support interventions and can be delivered in team-based care models.

activity_code: K01
title: Functional validation of the SMOC1 matrisomal protein network in Alzheimers disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative Large scale proteomic profiling of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) patients has identified an interconnected network of proteins that change in levels as the disease advances. To determine the function of these proteins in the brain and link them to specific causes of AD, this project will dissect the role of SMOC1, a key protein predicted to drive changes in the network as a whole, and link individual network components to specific triggers that cause AD. The completion of this study will identify targets for drug development, uncover biological processes underlying AD, and elucidate the role of a predicted AD protein network with high translational potential.

application_id: 11162876
activity_code: F31
title: Separating the molecular determinants that govern the evolution of Orthoflavivirus NS5-mediated IFN antagonism 
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Flaviviruses that pose significant public health threats are those that evolved to successfully block their host’s
innate immune response while not sacrificing viral replication function. The proposed research will investigate
the structural and functional determinants that govern flaviviruses ability to utilize various modes of non-structural
protein 5 (NS5)-mediated interferon (IFN) antagonism as well as their evolution. Therefore, this work will provide
important information about not only potential sites in NS5 that could serve as drug targets or create attenuated
viruses to use in vaccine strategies, but also clues about the evolutionary potential of emerging flaviviruses.

application_id: 10789869
activity_code: F32
title: Visual cortex circuits mediating arousal and visual discrimination
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative: Surprising or startling events activate the locus coeruleus, a region of the brain that controls arousal and is thought to aid subsequent perception and discrimination through modulation of sensory processing in the cortex. This modulation of the cortex is normally beneficial, but it can become dysregulated in disorders such as insomnia, depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder. By exploring the mechanisms through which arousal shapes sensory processing, we hope to better understand the normal and pathological roles of arousal and highlight novel targets for therapeutic interventions.

title: The multifaceted roles of PI(4,5)P2 on HIV-1 assembly
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite significant advances in treatment and care, HIV continues to pose a global health threat. This research
will deepen our understanding of both viral and host factors that drive HIV-1 replication, with a particular focus
on the crucial role of phosphatidylinositol (4,5)-bisphosphate [PI(4,5)P2] in HIV-1 assembly. Ultimately, this
work will also explore the potential of PI(4,5)P2 inhibitors in preventing HIV-1 spread, contributing to global
efforts aimed at ending the HIV/AIDS pandemic.

application_id: 11137406
activity_code: K99
title: Deconstructing pathogenic myelopoiesis in cancer immunosuppression and treatment resistance
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Bone marrow-originating myeloid cells are often responsible for immunosuppression in cancer tissue,
frequently being associated with treatment resistance and cancer relapse. This proposal aims to understand
the genetic, epigenetic, and metabolic changes that occur in bone marrow myeloid cell precursors due to tumor
inflammation. This proposal will also establish an independent program studying mechanisms by which bone
marrow precursors are epigenetically distorted during therapy and, in turn, impact therapy resistance and
cancer relapse.

activity_code: F30
title: Uncovering Nodal signaling and transcription factor interactions in somitic mesoderm development using single-cell deep learning methods
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed research will create a cis-regulatory map of how Nodal signaling and T-Box transcription factors, affect cell-type specification during somitic mesoderm development. Improvements to high-throughput single-cell technologies and computational methods will be developed to uncover TF interactions during the differentiation of somitic mesoderm in zebrafish. The success of deep learning methods for in silico experiments to predict TF-TF interactions paired with high throughput CRISPR mutagenesis of F0 zebrafish would represent a significant advance toward understanding gene regulation required for development.

title: Deciphering the Transcriptomic Basis of Chemo Fog
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Although chemotherapy dramatically improves cancer survival rates, treatment side effects such as cognitive
impairment can greatly diminish the quality of life of cancer survivors. This project aims to test whether perturbing
key transcriptional regulators in the brain modifies cognitive decline observed upon chemotherapy treatment.
The findings of this work will motivate the development of interventions to protect cancer survivors against
chemotherapy-induced cognitive decline.

application_id: 11229141
activity_code: K99
title: Impact of Clonal Hematopoiesis on Solid Tumor Development and Response to Therapy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project aims to identify the role(s) of mutant hematopoietic cells, often associated with clonal hematopoiesis
(CH), in solid tumors. As hematopoietic involvement is a main driver of tumor formation, development, and
therapy response, solid tumors which arise in the background of a hematopoietic landscape harboring CH-
associated mutations could have a variety of unique consequences. Identifying the fate and function of these
CH-associated mutant cells in solid tumors could provide insights towards how to improve disease surveillance,
treatment options, and ultimately patient outcomes.

application_id: 11036124
activity_code: K99
title: Chromosomal instability and radiation sensitivity in meningioma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative: New biomarkers and treatments for meningioma to predict and overcome
radiation resistance are urgently needed to improve the lives of patients. Our work has identified
aneuploidy and chromosomal instability as key mediators of meningioma development and
resistance to radiation. I propose a multipronged approach to address our gaps in knowledge in
how these factors drive meningioma biology, and how we can bring our knowledge to bear to
provide meningioma patients with new biomarkers and treatments.

application_id: 11033384
activity_code: K99
title: The Role of Histone Cleavage in Breast Cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Perturbed epigenetic programs are key drivers of tumor initiation and progression. This proposal aims to
delineate the functional and clinical relevance of proteolytic cleavage of histone H3 in breast tumors dependent
on the KDM4C histone demethylase. We aim to provide new insights into this poorly understood epigenetic
regulatory mechanism that could serve as basis for the design of novel cancer therapies.

application_id: 11117591
activity_code: K99
title: Engineered Living Materials as Programmable Therapeutic Implants for Pancreatic Cancer
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A long-standing challenge of pancreatic cancer is poor drug delivery and immunosuppressive environment, con-
tributing to reduced drug efficacy and a low survival rate. Implantable drug depots enable direct therapeutic
targeting of tumors but often lack the precise dosage and kinetics control necessary for effective treatment. This
proposal integrates genetically engineered bacteria within implantable materials, creating novel engineered living
materials that locally and dynamically deliver anticancer therapeutics to orchestrate cytotoxic effects and anti-
tumoral immunity against pancreatic cancer.

application_id: 11114756
activity_code: K99
title: Dynamics and function of peritoneal myeloid cells during colorectal cancer peritoneal dissemination and implications for immunotherapy
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The peritoneal cavity is an immune cell-enriched environment, and its immunity plays a crucial role in
addressing the clinically challenging and devastating diseases such as gastrointestinal cancer peritoneal
metastasis. Investigations proposed in this project employs a translational approach, integrating human
immune response profiling with functional studies in mouse models to deepen our understanding of peritoneal
immune cell dynamics and functions, particularly macrophages and dendritic cells, during cancer peritoneal
progression and treatment. Insights gained from this research will revolutionize our approach to gastrointestinal
cancer peritoneal metastasis, paving the way for novel immunotherapeutic strategies to effectively tackle this
formidable disease.

application_id: 11195974
activity_code: K01
title: Defining the mechanisms of hemoglobin switching and genotoxicities associated with its manipulation
org_name: VERSITI WISCONSIN, INC.
phr: Project Narrative 8. Project Narrative Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (HPFH) mutations reduce the severity of β-hemoglobinopathies and can be introduced experimentally and for therapeutic benefit. The aim of this proposal is to understand mechanisms of naturally occurring and therapeutically induced HPFH. Once the mechanisms are better understood, it will be possible to develop safe and effective precision medicine-based treatments for β- hemoglobinopathies.

application_id: 11212666
activity_code: K99
title: Engineering novel CAR-T cell therapy to overcome the cancer cell glycocalyx
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Cancer cells encase themselves in a soft material coating, termed the cellular glycocalyx, with specific properties
that protect them from recognition and destruction by the immune system, including engineered CAR-T cell
therapy. This research project aims to develop new strategies to equip CAR-T cells to effectively bind to or
penetrate the cancer-associated glycocalyx barrier in solid tumors. These strategies are expected to advance
CAR-T therapy for solid tumors by addressing a key mechanism of immune evasion.

application_id: 11215751
activity_code: K99
title: The role of loss of Y chromosome across the prostate cancer continuum
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research aims to elucidate the role of Y chromosome loss (LOY) in prostate cancer etiology, progression,
survival, and treatment outcomes across patient cohorts. By investigating LOY in both blood and tumor tissue,
the study will shed new light on our understanding of genetic risks of prostate cancer and inform more
personalized strategies for early detection, prognosis, and treatment, particularly in high-risk populations.
Ultimately, this study will contribute fundamental insights into genetic alterations in cancer, supporting the
development of targeted interventions and personalized treatment strategies that could improve patient care and
health outcomes.

activity_code: K99
title: Enhancing TIL efficacy in NSCLC through epigenetic reprogramming and computational modeling
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The short-term goal of this K99/R00 application is to develop the PI as an independent investigator, skilled in
cancer informatics, tumor immunology, and modern experimental science; the long-term goal is to enhance
tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy in lung cancer patients. The proposed research will test the efficacy
of epigenetic modulation paired TILs in lung cancer through in vivo and in vitro studies with de-identified human
samples. The project will also use modern sequencing data and methods from mathematical biology to discover
mechanisms of T cell suppression, and suggest novel targets for TIL-combination treatment.

application_id: 10826835
activity_code: F32
title: Diagnosing disparities in existing and proposed allocation systems for kidney transplantation
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To address inequitable allocation, the kidney allocation system was changed in early 2021, and given the complex interplay of disparities in allocation, this change may have unintended consequences for vulnerable populations. Our goal is to develop robust equity metrics to study the impact of the new allocation system, both in ensuring equitable distribution of organs to transplant centers across the US based on patient priority on the waitlist, which was the aim of the policy change, and the new policy’s impact on kidney transplant rate for vulnerable populations who have historically been disenfranchised by allocation policy. Our results will guide the development of a more equitable allocation system.

application_id: 11002007
activity_code: F30
title: Investigating the epidermal microenvironment in melanoblast migration and invasion: a novel approach to understanding invasive melanoma
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative Invasive melanoma is a potentially deadly medical condition, with an incidence of 100,000 new cases in 2021 alone. Melanoma research has mainly focused on melanocyte dysregulation to understand the disease; yet, the epidermis is an important microenvironment for healthy melanocytes, which adhere to and communicate with keratinocytes to maintain homeostasis. In this project, we will test hypothesis that melanocytes and epidermal cells form specialized adhesions important for melanocyte migration and retention within the epidermis, thereby contributing to the suppression of melanoma metastasis.

application_id: 11213790
activity_code: K99
title: Unraveling myeloid-mediated mechanisms of immunotherapy resistance in prostate cancer bone metastases at single-cell resolution
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Prostate cancer is the most common male malignancy, with bone being the predominant site (>70%) of
metastases in patients with advanced prostate cancer. Patients with bone metastases tend to have poorer
responses to immunotherapy compared to those without bone involvement, suggesting the presence of an
immunosuppressive niche within bone. To discover molecular targets for improving immunotherapeutic
strategies, we will focus on identifying the specific cells and corresponding signals that contribute to
immunotherapy resistance in the tumor microenvironment.

application_id: 11125948
activity_code: T32
title: Predoctoral Training in Cross-disciplinary Molecular and Cellular Biology
org_name: BRANDEIS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The goal of this new graduate student training program is to produce rigorous,
quantitatively-minded scientists equipped to solve increasingly complex problems in
Molecular and Cellular Biology using integrated, cross-disciplinary approaches. In
addition to rigorous coursework and research training, students are provided with
layered, individualized career development activities to help prepare for diverse
scientific research and other science-related careers.

application_id: 10826420
activity_code: F30
title: Role of interleukin-6 signaling on T cell potential of aged hematopoietic stem cells and thymic progenitors
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Older adults have increased rates of infections and cancers due to declining immune function, which is likely due to alterations in T cell progenitors and inflammatory signaling pathways in the aged microenvironment. We will investigate the causes of T cell decline with age using a newly described mutant mouse model and a novel, in vitro Artificial Thymic Organoid system, which reproducibly produces all stages of T cell development from a single stem cell. Our proposed studies will provide insights into the aging immune system and inform future therapies for regenerative medicine and age-related diseases.

application_id: 11213766
activity_code: K99
title: Utilizing patient-derived premalignant colon organoid biobank to interrogate existing and novel chemoprevention strategies
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: This study aims to improve human health by identifying new ways to prevent the development of colorectal
cancer in patients with familial adenomatous polyposis, a genetic condition that greatly increases cancer risk,
with potential broader implications for sporadic colorectal cancer. By using advanced technologies and highly
relevant patient-derived models, this project will uncover why current treatments in patients vary in effectiveness
and what new strategies are likely to succeed. Ultimately, this research could lead to more effective therapies,
reducing the need for invasive surgeries and improving long-term outcomes for patients.

application_id: 11159564
activity_code: K99
title: Adaptation and pilot testing of a couples-based intervention to prevent perinatal depression in rural India
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
One in four women in South Asia, including India are affected by perinatal depression. Marital-level relationship
risk factors such as poor relationship dynamics, limited spousal support, and women's low decision-making,
particularly in the context of early marriage and childbearing, increase women’s vulnerability to perinatal
depression, yet trials investigating couples-based interventions for prevention of perinatal depression remain
unexplored. The project aims to culturally adapt and pilot test a couples-based intervention to prevent perinatal
depression among primiparous (first-time pregnant) couples in rural India.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Despite the longstanding inequities in rates of low birthweight births among women of minority race, many public and private payers are unable to identify and mitigate disparities in adverse perinatal outcomes because of a lack of information on race and ethnicity. title: A mixed-methods assessment of decisional needs and development of health communication intervention for anal cancer screening among people with HIV
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

title: How Does Attention and Arousal Interact to Modulate Vision?
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will use functional neuroimaging techniques and computational modeling to investigate how arousal states and attention independently and interactively influence basic visual responses in humans. Results will provide invaluable information in our understanding of how attention and arousal alter neuronal communication, shaping how we see. By advancing our understanding of such brain functions, results from this proposal could further our understanding of the cortical and subcortical function of people with abnormal arousal levels (e.g., attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, general anxiety disorder), with potential applications down the road in guiding the diagnoses and treatment of disorders that involve atypical attention and arousal levels.

application_id: 11121845
activity_code: K01
title: Improving value-based healthcare delivery for opioid use disorder
org_name: BOSTON MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Improving opioid use disorder outcomes depends not only on developing evidence-based treatments and
prevention strategies, but also on optimizing the healthcare delivery and payment systems in which these
interventions are deployed. Though value-based care is one of the most widespread healthcare delivery
system innovations in the US today, large knowledge gaps remain in our understanding of whether – and how
– these models promote better care quality for people with opioid use disorder. This career development award
application proposes to use Massachusetts Medicaid accountable care organization implementation as a
natural experiment to understand the effect of value-based care reform on quality of care and adequacy of
reimbursement for opioid use disorder.

application_id: 11301612
activity_code: K99
title: Noradrenergic mechanisms underlying stress-induced opioid reinstatement
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA AT COLUMBIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Opioid misuse is often initiated following the experience of a traumatic event in an attempt to alleviate the high
levels of arousal and anxiety that subsequently occur. The noradrenergic locus plays a major role in facilitating
such anxiety and is dysregulated in conditions including posttraumatic stress disorder that have a high rate of
comorbidity with substance misuse. The experiments proposed herein begin to address the underlying etiology
of stress- and substance use disorders by examining the underlying interactions between neuroimmune signaling
and norepinephrine release to identify novel targets for the treatment of these conditions.

title: Community Pharmacy-Based Adaptation and Pilot Testing of Integrated HIV and Substance Use Disorder Care
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
HIV and substance use disorder (SUD) are interconnected public health challenges, yet care remains
fragmented despite the availability of evidence-based prevention, treatment, and harm reduction services.
This project will adapt and co-design evidence-based integrated care to expand access to HIV testing, pre-
exposure prophylaxis (PrEP), substance use screening, linkage to care, and harm reduction resources such
as sterile syringes and naloxone through community pharmacies. This pharmacy-led model will reach people
with intersecting needs for HIV and SUD prevention and treatment.

application_id: 10996033
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating SMARCE1 in Regulating Cohesin Activity, Chromatin Folding, and Gene Expression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRITIVE Cornelia de Lange Syndrome (CdLS) and Coffin-Siris Syndrome (CSS) are clinically similar, multi-system genetic disorders caused by mutations in the cohesin and SWI/SNF protein complexes, respectively. We identified a SWI/SNF component, SMARCE1, as a regulator of cohesin activity and chromatin folding suggesting that cohesin dysfunction underlies an unknown shared pathophysiology between CdLS and CSS. Elucidating the role of SWI/SNF and SMARCE1 with respect to cohesin-mediated chromatin folding and gene expression will be paramount in understanding whether shared cohesin dysfunction leading to a similar signature of gene misregulation could serve as either a biomarker or future therapeutic target across both CdLS and CSS patients.

activity_code: K99
title: GABAPVp-originated neural circuits mediate metabolic benefits during cold exposure
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The unprecedented prevalence of obesity and related metabolic diseases highlights the urgent need to develop
novel therapeutic strategies. This proposal aims to unravel the role of cold-activated neurons in the posterior
periventricular hypothalamic nucleus in regulating energy and thermal homeostasis, as well as identify the
downstream mediators and potential cold sensors. By uncovering the complex neural mechanisms that
contribute to the regulation of energy and thermal homeostasis, my research will provide necessary groundwork
to facilitate development of new treatment strategies for obesity and associated metabolic disorders.

title: Unlocking the Diagnostic and Prognostic Potential of 5hmC in Urine cfDNA: Characterization of Genome-wide Landscape in Healthy Urine and Biomarker Development in Localized Bladder Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE 5-hydroxymethylcytosine(5hmC) is an epigenetic modification that serves as a stable marker of DNA demethylation and transcriptional activation. activity_code: K99
title: Role of lipoxygenase-derived lipid mediators in the hematopoietic niche
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
I aim to characterize the role of lipoxygenase-derived lipid mediators in the hematopoietic niche. I will utilize
genetics, chemical biology, and confocal live cell imaging to determine and visualize the dynamic cellular and
niche alternations mediated by lipoxygenase-derived lipid mediators. These studies will provide novel insights
into our understanding of how lipoxygenase-derived lipid mediators in the niche modulate hematopoiesis and
will lead to novel therapies targeting the hematopoietic stem cell niche.

application_id: 11114952
activity_code: K99
title: Dynamically reconfigurable materials for 4D bioprinting of a human gut-brain axis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Disorders of gut-brain interaction (DGBIs) impact tens of millions of Americans, yet their etiology is poorly
understood partly due to the difficulty in modeling complex multi-tissue systems in vitro or in vivo. 4D bioprinting
is a technique that synergizes 3D spatial conditions and developmental time using living materials that provide
mechanical and biochemical cues to guide self-organization. This conceptualization will enable construction of
a human gut-brain axis with functional epithelium and enteric nerve by mimicking in vivo development.

activity_code: F31
title: The role of iron in retinal degeneration during bacterial infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Endophthalmitis, a bacterial infection inside the eye, can quickly lead to permanent vision loss. Bacteria need iron to survive, so immune cells may overload themselves with iron to keep it away from them. This proposal examines whether iron chelation could be an effective anti-bacterial and anti-inflammatory treatment for endophthalmitis.

application_id: 11215373
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating the mechanism of corneal lens development
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
 The human cornea is a curved structure that focuses light onto the retina, and abnormalities in corneal
curvature lead to visual disorders like myopia, hyperopia, astigmatism and keratoconus. Using a simple model
system, the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster, I will investigate how the shape of the corneal lens, structure similar
to human cornea, is controlled by the cells that secrete it. I will also establish the Drosophila corneal lens as a
model to study and develop treatments for glaucoma.

Nuclear DNA organization is deliberate. It inﬂuences transcriptional regulation, and structural deviations are

activity_code: K99
title: Revealing Cellular Determinants of LINE-1 Retrotransposition Outcomes
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Narrative
L1 retrotransposition is a complex process orchestrated by L1-encoded proteins, their interacting partners,
and cellular host factors, including DNA repair mechanisms, which together produce diverse genomic insertion
outcomes. Dissecting key determinants of L1 mutagenesis is crucial to understand retroelement propagation
and roles for L1 in human health and disease. The proposed studies will be the first to leverage state-of-the-art
long-read sequencing technologies to identify critical retroelement and host factors that affect L1 insertion
outcomes and will probe the contribution of L1-mediated somatic mosaicism in genetic diseases.

application_id: 11160067
activity_code: F32
title: Role of dietary fatty acids in shaping lung macrophage homeostasis and inflammatory function
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research investigates the interplay between dietary saturated fat-induced alterations in
immune cell function and allergic inﬂammation, with a focus on tissue-resident macrophages (TRMs)
and other myeloid cells in the lung. Understanding how dietary fatty acids modulate myeloid functions
holds signiﬁcant promise for unraveling the mechanisms driving obesity-associated asthma (OAA), a
condition causing an estimated one million preventable asthma cases annually. These insights will pave
the way for targeted interventions by identifying candidate pathways for dietary and pharmacological
interventions to mitigate obesity-related immune dysregulation and associated respiratory ailments,
ultimately aiming to alleviate the burden of obesity on public health.

application_id: 10928741
activity_code: K08
title: Harnessing treatment-induced tumor evolution and collateral sensitivities using a human rectal cancer co-clinical platform
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Advanced rectal tumors are treated with combined chemotherapy and radiation therapy (chemoradiation) prior to surgery, but there is wide variation in the magnitude of tumor response. Optimizing non-surgical chemoradiation-based treatments could spare some patients the need to undergo a morbid surgery, but will require a detailed understanding of how chemoradiation causes evolution of tumor subpopulations as well as the development of unique approaches to therapeutically target these evolving subpopulations. This project will use cutting-edge techniques to dissect tumor responses to chemoradiation as well as an innovative approach to identify new treatment strategies for locally advanced rectal cancer.

application_id: 11212106
activity_code: K99
title: Impacts of copy number variation on gene regulatory networks and fitness in Candida
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: Project Narrative
Pathogenic yeast that are resistant to antifungal drugs are a serious public health concern. To combat the
evolution of drug resistance we need to understand the mechanisms by which copy number variants produce
resistance and the pleiotropic effects they have on fitness and gene expression. Applying high-throughput
CRISPR-based and single-cell sequencing technologies to assay fitness and gene expression across multiple
genetic backgrounds will identify trade-offs that are robust to standing genetic variation and can be exploited
for combination therapy and drug development.

application_id: 11158834
activity_code: K99
title: The Molecular Basis of PIEZO Ion Channel Function
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
PIEZOs are mechanosensitive ion channels that convert force into an electrical signal, and they play critical
roles in diverse physiological processes including respiration, touch, proprioception, and vascular
development. The goal of this proposal is to determine the fundamental structural mechanics underlying
PIEZO ion channel function. This research will advance our understanding of the molecular mechanisms of
PIEZO ion channel activation and provide insights into how channel dysfunction causes disease.

title: Cell Fusion and Gene Expression in the C. elegans Epidermis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WYOMING
phr: This project explores how cell fusion shapes gene expression and developmental progression using the C.
elegans epidermis as a model system. Single-nucleus RNA sequencing, CRISPR-engineered mutants, and

activity_code: K99
title: Spatially-resolved, integrated cell state and lineage tracing to define progenitor cell dynamics in early mouse development
org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: Project Narrative
Genetic, epigenetic, and environmental factors drive the coordinated non-deterministic, programs of progenitor
cell division and diversification that define mammalian development. This proposal describes the use of cutting-
edge, paired imaging- and sequencing-based cell state and lineage recording technologies to describe and probe
progenitor cell dynamics during mammalian development using both in vitro and in vivo model systems.
Developing a quantitative framework of understanding cell fate determination and tissue patterning will provide
novel insights into developmental programs in health and disease.

application_id: 11212991
activity_code: K99
title: Investigating nuclear plasticity in the human placental syncytium
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
During human pregnancy, the syncytiotrophoblast (STB) is a tissue-sized, single cell that forms the interface
between maternal and fetal vasculature and performs many essential functions. Systemic STB stress is a key
driver of maternal pathology in preeclampsia, a leading cause of maternal and fetal mortality that affects 5-10%
of pregnancies. This proposal aims to understand the molecular mechanisms underlying the STB's stress
response, with the long-term goal of identifying therapeutic targets to mitigate STB stress and treat pregnancy
disease.

application_id: 11031585
activity_code: K99
title: Integrating Genetics and Socio-Environmental Factors to Predict Disease Progression
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal aims to deepen our understanding of disease progression by integrating genetic predispositions
with socio-environmental factors, leveraging advanced statistical frameworks and large-scale biobanks. By
developing more accurate and comprehensive predictive models of disease progression, this work has the
potential to inform targeted interventions and therapeutic strategies. Ultimately, this project addresses significant
gaps in precision medicine, promising to advance public health by enabling more personalized and effective
secondary prevention strategies.

application_id: 11110680
activity_code: K99
title: Developing Single-Cell Functional Genomic Technologies to Investigate DNA Methylation Changes in Human Diseases
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Abnormal DNA methylation changes are observed in many human diseases, yet the causes and
consequences of these changes are not well understood. To address this gap, I will develop single-cell
functional screenings that introduce site-specific changes in DNA methylation to study their functions.
This will help to illuminate the epigenetic mechanisms of many diseases and may identify precise DNA
methylation targets for therapeutic development.

application_id: 11180964
activity_code: F31
title: Impact of developmental serotonin on prefrontal cortex parvalbumin interneuron excitability and cognitive behavior
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Prefrontal parvalbumin (PV) interneuron activity during early development is required for shaping prefrontal
cortex connectivity, function, and cognitive flexibility, which go awry in psychiatric disorders like depression,
schizophrenia and attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder; however, the factors responsible for altering PV
interneuron activity are largely unknown. Here, we aim to elucidate the neurodevelopmental impact of serotonin
on PV interneuron excitability and cognitive behavior by employing a combination of cellular, circuit and
behavioral approaches. This proposed work would have tremendous public health relevance, as the research
outlined will 1) provide insight into the development of neural circuitry supporting cognitive function; 2) promote
newfound und...

application_id: 11128661
activity_code: K08
title: Mitochondrial Metabolic Regulation of GABAergic Interneurons
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative
Mitochondria regulate metabolism, the process that changes food to energy and if people have abnormal or
missing copies of the genes required for mitochondrial function they will develop primary mitochondrial disease
for which there are neither cures nor treatments. Many patients with mitochondrial disease have neurologic
symptoms, so to understand how mitochondrial dysfunction causes neurologic problems, this proposal studies
mitochondrial metabolism in neurons. Successful completion of the proposed studies would result in advancing
both the understanding and methods for studying mitochondrial metabolism in the brain, with the goal of
translating these findings into treatments for patients with mitochondrial diseases.

application_id: 11136177
activity_code: K99
title: Metabolite Transport Modulation to Mitigate Ischemic-Reperfusion Injury
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative
This research is pivotal for public health as it addresses the critical challenge of ischemic-reperfusion (I/R) injury
in patients suffering from a “heart attack” or acute myocardial infarction (AMI), a prevalent condition with high
mortality rates. By exploring innovative therapeutic strategies centered on metabolite transport modulation, this
study aims to alleviate I/R injury, thus potentially reducing the incidence of chronic heart failure and improving
the life expectancy and quality of life for heart attack survivors. The research approach holds significant promise
for enhancing clinical practices and mitigating the substantial healthcare costs associated with post-AMI care.

application_id: 11145820
activity_code: K08
title: Unraveling the role of the molecular co-chaperone DNAJC7 in protein homeostasis and neurodegeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The accumulation of misfolded proteins represents a common feature in age-related neurodegenerative
diseases, such as Huntington's disease. This project aims to uncover the role of specific molecular chaperones
in neurons in mitigating protein aggregation in Huntington's disease. These findings hold the promise of
guiding therapeutic strategies that harness the appropriate molecular chaperones to alleviate protein
aggregation in Huntington's disease and potentially other neurodegenerative disorders.

application_id: 11126527
activity_code: F32
title: The Role of Hippocampal Differentiation in Visual Object-based and Feature-based Attention
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The hippocampus is traditionally considered a brain region for memory, but emerging findings suggest that it
may play a role in key aspects of vision. This proposal investigates the original hypothesis that the unique ability
of the hippocampus to differentiate related visual stimuli may bias the allocation of object-based and feature-
based attention. If confirmed, this would impact our understanding of the brain systems that control attention,
reveal candidate neural sources of attention deficits in visual and other disorders, and suggest learning protocols
that may have potential for rehabilitating central visual processing through hippocampal plasticity.

application_id: 11162993
activity_code: F32
title: Investigation of factors involved in assembly of the mycolata envelope
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT SUMMARY
Bacteria within the Corynebacterineae suborder, such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Corynebacterium
diphtheriae, pose significant global healthcare challenges, partly due to the distinct cell envelope of these
bacteria, which plays a crucial role in their pathogenicity and antibiotic resistance. My research focuses on
understanding how the complex mycobacterial cell envelope layers are synthesized to identify new weak points
to exploit for antibiotic development.

application_id: 11021067
activity_code: K01
title: Integrated multi-omics approach to identify early protein biomarkers in Alzheimer's disease and cognitive decline
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: NARRATIVE/PUBLIC HEALTH STATEMENT:
 Alzheimer’s disease affects 6 million Americans, and yet nearly all clinical trials for new treatments have failed.
This project is designed to identify plasma proteins associated with Alzheimer’s disease and with rate of
cognitive decline, which may help identify people at risk for future Alzheimer’s disease and may inform targets
for novel therapeutics.

application_id: 11229812
activity_code: K99
title: Exploring the role of Hedgehog pathway-Primary cilia axis in human lung alveologenesis and disease
org_name: LUNDQUIST INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL INNOVATION AT HARBOR-UCLA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Premature babies born before 34 weeks of pregnancy take their first breaths using immature lungs and often
rely on mechanical ventilation and oxygen therapy to survive, leading to the development of lung diseases such
as Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia (BPD). The goal of this proposal is to determine how Hedgehog (HH) pathway/
primary cilia (PC) axis participate to human alveologenesis and how it is disrupted in BPD. The findings may
contribute to the development of targeted interventions that could improve the respiratory outcomes of preterm
infants and enhance our overall understanding of lung development in the context of prematurity.

application_id: 10995145
activity_code: F31
title: Mechanisms of regenerative decline in aged alveolar type 2 cells
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY D/B/A HARVARD SCHOOL OF PUBLIC HEALTH
phr: Project Narrative Lung diseases represent an enormous worldwide health burden and most share old age as their primary risk factor, yet age is rarely studied as a variable in the lab. Age negatively impacts the repair function of alveolar type 2 cells, the crucial cells that protect the gas exchange surface, but it is unclear why. This project will improve our understanding of the contribution of immune signals in the aging lung to the repair dysfunction of alveolar type 2 cells; knowledge crucial to understanding the causation of lung disease and developing more effective medical care in older adults.

title: Influence of Caffeine on Melanoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: SUMMARY: This project will define the complete A2A G-protein activation profile at single pathway resolution, investigate A2A signaling in melanoma, and determine if A2A is altered by caffeine. Additionally, it will delineate the anti-melanoma effects of caffeine, providing mechanistic and biomolecular insight to the many epidemiological observations, potentially leading to new therapeutics. activity_code: K23
title: Using real-world evidence to define safe pain management strategies in cirrhosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pain is a highly prevalent and undertreated symptom in patients with cirrhosis, a population which may also be particularly vulnerable to adverse outcomes related to pain medications. Currently, there is limited understanding of how pain is managed or the actual, real-world harms associated with use of pain medications in this population. This proposal aims to quantify the harms of specific types of pain medications in cirrhosis patients, using these data to develop evidence-based guidance to help clinicians feel more comfortable managing pain, improving quality of life and outcomes for patients with cirrhosis.

application_id: 11222641
activity_code: F31
title: Cerebrovascular and neurophysiological mechanisms of sleep-dependent memory impairment in older adults at risk for Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Over 6.5 million Americans over the age of 65 currently live with Alzheimer’s disease: a debilitating
neurodegenerative disorder characterized by significant cognitive deficits. Research shows that up to 40% of
dementia risk is due to modifiable risk factors, including deficient sleep and cardiovascular risk factors that
exacerbate Cerebral Small Vessel Disease (CSVD). This project examines the mechanisms linking CSVD to
sleep and sleep-dependent memory deficits related to Alzheimer’s disease, and lays the groundwork for
advancement of behavioral interventions, refinement of screening, and development of public health
recommendations for patients with disordered or deficient sleep.

application_id: 11213546
activity_code: K99
title: Dissecting Lung Spatial-Biological Niches in Early Fibrogenesis
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Interstitial lung diseases affect >5,000,000 patients globally and often result in a mixture of (1) healthy tissue, (2)
early scar tissue, and (3) mature scars – even within the same patient. Each of these different types of tissue
has a unique assortment of cells and proteins, but traditional biological methods that examine lung tissue in bulk
cannot distinguish the factors unique to each tissue type. This project examines how the cell/protein composition
of early scar regions differs from both healthy tissue and mature scars, enhancing our understanding of the onset
of scarring and our ability to target early disease progression.

application_id: 11112253
activity_code: K99
title: Compartment-Specific Synaptic Organization and Plasticity During Learning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: NARRATIVE
Dendritic and synaptic dysfunction has been linked to a variety of neurodevelopmental, neurodegenerative, and
neuropsychiatric disorders, including autism, depression, addiction, and Alzheimer’s disease. This proposal aims
to address fundamental questions about dendritic and synaptic function in behaving animals with the use of
cutting-edge imaging approaches and causal manipulations. The expected outcome of this work will help provide
foundational knowledge on dendritic and synaptic function in the intact brain, laying the foundation for a deeper
understanding of how synaptic abnormalities associated with disorders lead to behavioral deficits.

application_id: 11178662
activity_code: K99
title: Using the continuum of genetic causality to investigate trans regulatory mechanisms.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: NARRATIVE
Large-scale human studies have pinpointed thousands of regions in the human genome that are associated with
susceptibility to diseases such as type 2 diabetes, asthma, and cardiovascular disease. Understanding how
genetic variants are linked to specific disease processes is needed to establish effective, novel therapies. The
goal of this project is to develop and apply new tools to map out gene regulatory networks and connect their
activities to disease-associated genetic variation.

activity_code: K01
title: Sex Differences in Alzheimer's disease-Related Pathology: Role of Mitochondrial Function and Metabolic Health
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Narrative
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is one of the most important public health challenges of our time, with
women disproportionately impacted. We will uniquely examine whether mitochondrial function
plays a critical role in understanding sex differences in memory circuitry function and the early
emergence of AD-related pathology. This study will advance our knowledge on the long-term
implications for the co-occurrence of brain and metabolic disorders, and the sex differences
therein, and highlight potential mechanistic targets for therapeutic strategies.

application_id: 11236562
activity_code: F31
title: A multilevel examination of attention in social anxiety disorder
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project examines the role of the attentional system in the maintenance of social anxiety disorder. By
leveraging an experimental design, ecological momentary assessment, and the multi-method measurement of
outcomes across cognitive, behavioral, and subjective levels of analysis, this project aims to delineate how
internally biased attention may causally contribute to social anxiety. A better understanding of what patterns of
attention underlie disorder maintenance, the mechanisms that explain how biased attention causes ongoing
anxiety, and the moderators that magnify or attenuate this effect has important implications for informing novel,
effective treatments for social anxiety disorder and similar translational work in other internalizing disorders.

activity_code: K99
title: RNA-Related Function and Dysfunction of Tau in FTD
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Dysregulation of RNA processing and metabolism are well-established properties of some neurodegenerative diseases. Tau is an FTD disease-causing factor and an RNA binding protein, but there is little known about RNA dysregulation in tauopathies. Completion of this proposal will transition promising molecular data to an interdisciplinary characterization of a novel and sometimes toxic tau function, supported by patient-derived data and directly relevant to therapeutic testing.

application_id: 11112781
activity_code: K99
title: Bioactive Material Interventions for Ischemic Stroke
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Stroke impacts about 800,000 people annually in the United States, is associated with progressive cognitive
loss attributed to breakdown of the blood-brain-barrier, and survivors have a double risk of dementia in the
decade following injury. This proposal aims to develop an injectable hydrogel system to target neurovascular
dysfunction by restoring astrocyte and pericyte coverage to restore stabilization and functionality. Developing
and deploying strategies to target astrocytes and pericytes could enable novel methodologies for preventing
the development of cognitive deficits in stroke-impacted patients.

application_id: 10791731
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the function of subclonal cooperation in breast cancer progression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative Tumor heterogeneity, the evolution of tumor subclones driven by distinct oncogenes, is a universal phenomenon across solid tumors that is correlated with poor prognosis in patients. In this proposed work, I model tumor heterogeneity by co-culturing cells either overexpressing MYC or expressing oncogenic RAS, to characterize how the unique behaviors of tumor subclones expressing different oncogenes elicit emergent tumor properties and promote tumor progression. Understanding this cooperation will provide key insights into the functional significance of tumor heterogeneity and will be critical towards uncovering future therapeutic interventions to treat heterogenous tumors.

activity_code: F32
title: Neural Representations of Abstract Sequences
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will investigate the properties of generalization in neural networks, using abstract sequence representation. Such sequences are necessary in daily life, and their neural underpinnings remain unknown. These results will provide a foundation for explicating and addressing the neural mechanisms behind impaired sequence processing and generalization in conditions such as Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder.

activity_code: K99
title: From Exploration to Expertise: Mechanisms of Motor Learning in Cortico-Basal Ganglia Circuits
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The ability to learn new movements in pursuit of a goal is a defining feature of animal behavior and generates
the great diversity of behavioral repertoires, yet how neural circuits perform the computations underlying
specific learning algorithms remains unclear. This project examines this question by investigating the
mechanisms in cortico-basal ganglia circuits that support specific steps of trial-and-error learning by combining
insight from learning in artificial networks with modern tools including markerless pose-tracking, long-term
electrophysiology, and circuit manipulation. The findings will shed light on how diseases of the basal ganglia –
such as Parkinson’s Disease and Huntington’s Disease – result in devastating motor control and learning
deficits and potentia...

application_id: 11282327
activity_code: K99
title: Elucidating the molecular and functional diversity of dorsal root ganglia somatosensory neurons across mammals
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: Project Narrative
The somatosensory system detects information from the body’s surfaces using a diverse repertoire of somatosensory neuron
types that each express one or more of a small collection of sensory receptor genes that transduce mechanical, thermal,
and/or chemical stimuli. Understanding the primary somatosensory neuronal repertoires of different mammalian species
will reveal how each species’ somatosensory system uniquely encodes mechanical, thermal, and chemical somatosensory
modalities and guide efforts towards treating somatosensory diseases such as neuropathic pain and itch.

application_id: 11033844
activity_code: K08
title: Lens Capsule Based Extended Drug Delivery Device
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it provides characterization of two techniques to
manage ocular inflammation without requiring localized steroids or systemic therapy. The proposed therapy
uses an implanted microdevice to provide extended local delivery of biologics and immune modulating
peptides. Thus, this proposal is relevant to the NEI’s mission that pertains to designing new therapies to
restore tissue homeostasis, and of developing steroid therapy alternatives without side effects such as
cataracts or glaucoma.

application_id: 11239598
activity_code: F31
title: Axonal and synaptic transformations of melanopsin retinal ganglion cell output
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project concerns how a mammalian photoreceptor generates, transforms, and transmits information that
drives a range of processes such as the regulation of sleep, circadian rhythms, and mood. The proposed
experiments will characterize how specific properties of this photoreceptor make it especially well-positioned to
collect environmental information and drive downstream circuits. The knowledge gained in this project is likely
to generalize to other neurons and has the potential to inform how we design artificial lighting for health and
treat ocular diseases.

application_id: 11244302
activity_code: F32
title: Characterizing migratory cell differentiation in developing zebrafish skin
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Mucus cells and ionocytes are crucial to the function of many organs and are conserved across animals including
humans and zebrafish. Impairment of mucus cell and ionocyte function can cause human disease, including
contributing to cystic fibrosis, but despite the importance of these cell types, it is poorly understood how they are
distributed throughout the mature tissue during development. In this proposal, I will use zebrafish skin as a model
system to dissect the mechanisms by which mucus cell and ionocyte precursors simultaneously migrate through
an epithelial tissue and differentiate into their mature forms, revealing potential avenues for further study of
human mucosal tissue diseases.

application_id: 11190956
activity_code: K99
title: RNA and genome regionalization in giant single cells: implications for cellular patterning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
A cell must know what genes to transcribe and where to send these messages in order to carry out specific
cellular functions. This proposal uses an emerging model system, Stentor coeruleus, a giant single cell with a
clear axis and spatial complexity comparable to an animal embryo to understand how RNA and genome
regionalization contribute to cellular patterning and function. This work will further our understanding of how
cells form patterns and enable us to study what happens when patterns are broken in development and
disease.

application_id: 11243894
activity_code: F31
title: Multimodal AI for aortic disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
The aorta undergoes complex structural remodeling with aging and disease, driven by genetic and environmental
factors, which plays a critical role in cardiovascular health. However, current approaches provide limited insights
into the progression of these changes and their implications for disease risk. The proposed research leverages
generative modeling and multimodal learning to simulate age- and disease-associated aortic remodeling while
developing an organ-specific, genetically-informed foundation model that integrates imaging, text, and genetic
data for advanced diagnostic and prognostic applications in cardiovascular disease.
