title: Associations between neurophysiological discrimination and clinically observable sensory reactivity features in infants
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 This research is relevant to public health because it will uncover biomarkers of clinically observable
sensory features that significantly impact children with neurodevelopmental conditions such as autism. This
research will shed light on the origins of sensory processing difficulties and provide valuable insights into early
brain development for at-risk populations. Further, these empirical findings will strengthen conceptual models
of brain-behavior linkages related to sensory processing deficits, which in turn will inform more effective
targeted interventions for infants at increased likelihood for neurodevelopmental conditions, ultimately
improving developmental outcomes.

application_id: 11135340
activity_code: K01
title: Exploring Food Insecurity and Pilot Testing a Diabetes Nutrition Education Program and Healthy Food Access Service for American Indian and Alaska Native Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
org_name: COLORADO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
American Indian and Alaska Native (AI/AN) adults are almost twice as likely to have type 2 diabetes (T2D) than
non-Hispanic white adults. Healthy nutrition habits are a cornerstone to effective diabetes self-management;
however, barriers to healthful eating, such as lack of access to culturally-responsive diabetes nutrition
education, lack of access to fresh food, or inability to afford healthy food (known as food insecurity), are
common among AI/ANs with T2D. The proposed research is relevant to public health because the
development of effective interventions to target AI/ANs with T2D who experience food insecurity is
fundamental in reducing disproportionate diabetes health disparities among AI/AN communities.

phr: Project Narrative Male infertility is a highly prevalent yet poorly understood condition that clinically manifests in combination with poor somatic health, especially in terms of elevated cancer risks for affected males and their families. title: Developing Systemic in vivo Massively Parallel Reporter Assay to Elucidate the Genetic Etiology of Problematic Alcohol Use
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative
Although problematic alcohol use (PAU) is a major public health concern, there are only three approved

application_id: 11165145
activity_code: F32
title: The role of protein phase separation in the circadian clock
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Circadian clocks regulate many aspects of physiology and, in humans, impact metabolic efficiency,
growth regulation and psychiatric function. Core circadian clock components across species are large, highly
disordered proteins proposed to phase separate to regulate clock timing, and, as this process is not well
understood, I aim to determine how disordered regions of core clock proteins contribute to their ability to phase
separate and how condensed states alter partner interactions, enzymatic activites and entrainment mechanisms.
These studies will provide a deeper understanding of the molecular foundations that enable the circadian clock
to function as an effective cellular timekeeper and how perturbations to this system lead to cellular dysfunction
and disease.

application_id: 10928746
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding Changes in Hippocampal Representations by Measuring Memories with Natural Language Processing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OREGON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The hippocampus plays a key role not only in encoding new episodic memories, but in separating neural representations for highly similar—and potentially interfering—memories. The proposed research will leverage Natural Language Processing algorithms and advanced computational and neuroimaging methods to test whether separation of hippocampal representations predicts distortions in how events are actually remembered (i.e., the content of episodic memories). The project will lead to fundamental new insight into how the hippocampus resolves interference between similar memories and will establish exciting new approaches for investigating these mechanisms.

application_id: 10764244
activity_code: F32
title: Defining Structural and Molecular Mechanisms of The Human Multifunctional Mitochondrial Protease, LONP1
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Integrated quality control (QC) systems are required to sense and manage mitochondrial stress, and their dysregulation is associated with a number of diseases including neurodegenerative disease, aging, and cancer. The human ATP-dependent AAA+ protease, LONP1, has emerged as a master regulator of diverse mitochondrial functions, is an integral component of mitochondrial QC systems, and alterations in its activity are implicated in mitochondrial diseases, aging, and cancer. This proposal will provide structural and mechanistic models describing the allosteric regulation and control of LONP1's proteolytic activity and determine the molecular mechanism and conformational state required for LONP1's single-stranded mtDNA binding activity.

application_id: 11176275
activity_code: F32
title: Lower Leg Heat Therapy in Older, Hypertensive Women to Improve Blood Pressure and Cognition
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Hypertension remains a major public health issue in the United States and contributes to the development of
cardiovascular diseases (CVD) and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD). Women older than 60
are at a particularly greater risk for the development of hypertension yet have a proportionally lower rate of blood
pressure control, drastically increasing the likelihood for complications related to CVD and ADRD. Accordingly,
the proposed work will investigate the impact of a home-based, lower leg heat therapy on blood pressure control
in older, hypertensive women, while simultaneously assessing the autonomic cardiovascular mechanisms
related to blood pressure regulation and their impact on cerebrovascular function and cognitive health.

application_id: 11240952
activity_code: F31
title: 129Xe Hyperpolarized Chemical Exchange Saturation Transfer (HyperCEST) MRI for Tracking Immunotherapy Delivery in the Lungs
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TX MD ANDERSON CAN CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The current inability to track the delivery of immunotherapy to the lungs is a critically unmet clinical need, as
doctors do not have the ability to verify that immunotherapy cells are reaching the desired target site and
interacting as intended. This project aims to address this unmet clinical need through the development of a novel
translational imaging methodology for tracking the delivery of natural killer cell immunotherapy to the lungs using
hyperpolarized chemical exchange saturation transfer (hyperCEST) MRI. My innovative approach combines
emerging, FDA approved and clinically relevant, hyperpolarized 129-Xenon gas and clinically translatable, FDA
approved perfluorocarbon nanodroplets as contrast agents for labeling immunotherapy cells.

application_id: 11170461
activity_code: F32
title: Determining pathways from food insecurity to parent and child well-being
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: Food insecurity is associated with maladaptive outcomes in every domain of child development, with long-
lasting life and health consequences, but how it undermines child development is unknown. By using
ecological momentary assessment to determine the directionality and timing between food insecurity and
parent and child well-being, this research will identify how food insecurity works within a family to disrupt child
development. Gaining insight into these processes will help shape the most promising set of solutions for this
highly significant public health threat to millions of children’s immediate and longer-term health and
development.

application_id: 10907540
activity_code: K08
title: Examining the Role of a Novel Long Noncoding RNA, linc02454, in Resistance of Glioblastoma to Temozolomide
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) are a novel class of RNAs representing up to 80% of the genome, though only a handful of lncRNAs have been demonstrated with function in the most common and deadly brain tumor, glioblastoma (GBM). I have identified a novel lncRNA, linc02454, upregulated by GBM chemotherapy, that modulates the chemosensitivity of GBM. I plan to characterize the manner by which chemotherapy increases linc02454, and the manner by which linc02454 affects treatment sensitivity, to better understand the role of lncRNAs following adjuvant therapy, while acquiring the training in transcriptional regulation necessary to develop as a surgeon-scientist.

title: Determining factors of inflammation that support the expansion and function of long-lived effector cell CD8 T cells in a mature memory compartment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE
Traditional SPF mouse models do not accurately reflect the immune compartment makeup observed in
humans, particularly the T cell compartment. This proposal utilizes pet store mouse cohousing to normalize
mouse pathogen exposure with the goal to explore the maintenance and function of long-lived effector CD8 T
cells. These studies will help better understand the factors that promote T cell maintenance and function in a
model with normal levels of pathogen exposure.

title: Integrative Multi-Omics Approach to Identify Cell-Type and Brain-Region Specific Genetic Drivers of Alcohol Use Disorder
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS

application_id: 11145044
activity_code: K01
title: The Effect of the Home Environment on Health and Wellbeing of Low-income Older Adults
org_name: HEBREW REHABILITATION CENTER FOR AGED
phr: A substantial number of low-income older adults are frequently exposed to a hot home ambient temperature, either because they cannot afford air conditioning, or they do not possess sufficient motor and/or cognitive ability to properly use it. The proposed research aims to understand the impact of the summertime variations in home thermal environment on health and wellbeing of low-income community-dwelling older adults. This is an urgently-needed line of research that is expected to inform public health policy and lead to development of clinical and housing interventions that are tuned to the unique needs of each older adult living within their own home.

phr: Narrative
Aging is a major risk factor for the onset of chronic diseases and thus slowing aging holds the potential to

title: Understanding the neurocomputational mechanisms underlying speech sensorimotor adaptation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
Sensorimotor adaptation, learning from sensory feedback, is a fundamental skill that supports accurate
movement control in speech and is impaired in many neurogenic disorders (including cerebellar degeneration,
Parkinson’s disease, and adults who stutter). However, why such damage to such disparate neural structures
leads to similar impairments in learning is not well understood, potentially because adaptation in speech may
comprised of multiple learning processes, as has been shown in other types of movement such as reaching. The
proposed studies expand a well-studied computational mechanism of sensorimotor adaptation to speech and
test its neural basis across multiple motor domains, to providing a critical first step towards developing more
sensitive measures that can d...

application_id: 10791766
activity_code: F30
title: Epigenetic regulation of neural stem cell biology by Tet DNA dioxygenases
org_name: ALBERT EINSTEIN COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Dysregulation of epigenetic gene regulatory mechanisms, including DNA methylation, has been linked to human neurodevelopmental disorders, intellectual disability, and craniofacial abnormalities. The TET family of DNA demethylases have also been implicated in these disorders. This proposal investigates how TET enzymes regulate neural stem cell biology and embryonic neurodevelopment. Findings from this study will enhance our understanding of the epigenetic regulation of neurodevelopment and could facilitate the identification of new therapeutic targets to improve treatment of neurodevelopmental disorders.

application_id: 11184407
activity_code: K99
title: Endometrial immune microenvironment dysfunction in endometriosis pathobiology
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Endometriosis is a debilitating chronic disease characterized by an abnormal endometrial immune
microenvironment, affecting 10% of reproductive-age women worldwide. The goal of this proposal is to gain a
better understanding of the contribution of uterine natural killer cells, the dominant cytolytic lymphocyte in the
endometrium, to endometriosis pathobiology and to identify non-invasive, mechanistically-driven diagnostic
biomarkers in menstrual blood. Ultimately, completion of this work will contribute to significantly reducing the long
diagnostic delay associated with endometriosis by unraveling fundamental immune mechanisms and expanding
the pool of potential therapeutic targets, thereby mitigating the burden of illness and disability associated with
this condition.

application_id: 11242303
activity_code: F31
title: An EMA Investigation of Impaired Control Over Alcohol among Heavy Drinking College Students
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
College student heavy drinking is a longstanding national public health concern in the United States. This study
examines the impact of impaired control over alcohol (drinking more or for longer than intended) on heavy
drinking and related problems, leading up to, during, and immediately following a drinking event. The results
have the potential to significantly impact public health by providing a more nuanced understanding of an early
indicator of alcohol use disorder that could be targeted to reduce alcohol consumption and risk of long-term
consequences in college students who drink heavily.

application_id: 11243180
activity_code: F31
title: Characterization of the role of STK38 kinase in Wnt signaling and heart development
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Prevalence to Public Health
Project Narrative:
This research proposes to shed light on STK38's role as a positive kinase regulator of Wnt signaling, an
evolutionarily conserved signaling cascade crucial for numerous developmental processes, including
cardiogenesis. Using rigorous biochemical, cell biological and chemical approaches, I aim to unravel the
mechanism underlying its function. By integrating Wnt signaling modulation into cardiogenesis studies using
induced pluripotent stem cells, I will elucidate the functional significance of STK38 in heart development,
presenting a compelling case for its potential as a therapeutic target in addressing congenital heart anomalies.

application_id: 11244337
activity_code: F31
title: Roles for Ets1 in thymic selection and CD8αα IEL development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This research delves into the critical processes of thymic selection and the function of CD8αα intraepithelial
lymphocytes (IELs), shedding light on fundamental aspects of immune system regulation. This proposal
focuses on the regulation of these processes by Ets1, a transcription factor identified as a risk allele for several
autoimmune disorders. Understanding the role of Ets1 in thymic selection and IEL function will provide insights
into autoimmune diseases and intestinal pathologies, potentially paving the way for the development of novel
therapeutic strategies to improve public health outcomes.

title: Access to Strained Rings and Heterocycles: Applications in the Synthesis of Bacterial Metabolites and Chemical Building Blocks
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative/Abstract
Heterocycles and strained rings are valuable motifs in pharmaceuticals and natural products, thus highlighting
the need for unique ways to access them. The K99 phase will establish the first total syntheses of azetidine-
containing metabolites that affect biofilm and pigment production in the pathogenic bacterium P. aeruginosa; the
R00 phase will center on development of distinctive methods to access multifunctional chemical building blocks.
This project is relevant to the NIH’s mission because 1) in the short-term, it will expand fundamental knowledge
in the synthesis of bioactive natural products and pharmaceutically relevant motifs, and 2) in the long-term,
findings from biological studies of molecules in the K99 phase could lead to new anti-virulence treatmen...

application_id: 10929342
activity_code: F31
title: Interpreting Bone Morphogenetic Protein Gradients in Vertebrate Development
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY NEW YORK STONY BROOK
phr: Project Narrative The proper development of vertebrates requires the deployment of morphogen signaling gradients and the ability of cells exposed to the morphogen to interpret different levels of signal. This proposal will identify mechanisms by which cells interpret the Bone Morphogenetic Protein (BMP) morphogen gradient, which when disrupted can lead to disorders such as cardiac defects, undifferentiated mesoderm, lack of germ cells, eye abnormalities, gastrointestinal diseases, Sjögren’s syndrome, and various cancers. By understanding how cells interpret levels of BMP signaling, my research will shed light on the mechanisms of BMP-derived diseases and congenital syndromes and provide important insight into how cells interpret other types of morphogen gradients.

application_id: 11245148
activity_code: F31
title: Microbiota mechanisms promoting Clostridioides difficile colon persistence
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Infection caused by C. difficile and subsequent recurrence of infection following failed clearance of the pathogen
are urgent public health crises that affect hundreds of thousands of patients per year. Despite its known burden
on healthcare systems and patients alike, the molecular mechanisms that contribute to C. difficile persistence
are unknown. Through completion of this project, we plan to target these current challenges through the
discovery of one or more mechanisms by which the local microbiome governs the persistence of C. difficile in
the host.

activity_code: K08
title: Cannabis Toxicology in Relation to Neurocognitive Outcomes in Adolescents
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE While personal cannabis use and secondhand cannabis smoke exposure is prevalent among youth, the influence of these on adolescent cognitive development remains understudied. Cannabis product complexity and limitations of self-report may obscure important relationships between delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and neurocognitive development/outcomes. Here we propose using quantifiable toxicological information of personal and secondhand cannabis exposure from hair to predict cognitive functioning over time in adolescents.

application_id: 10929368
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of cardiac small-conductance calcium-activated potassium channels by PIP2
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Atrial Fibrillation (AF) is the most common type of cardiac arrhythmia, affecting over 40 million people globally. Small conductance calcium-activated potassium channels (SK channels) retain a unique role in the integration of the intracellular Ca2+ and membrane potentials within the heart. Our approaches integrating patch-clamp, TIRF microscopy, super-resolution immunofluorescence imaging, optogenetics and genetic manipulations may elucidate the mechanism of SK2 channels regulation by PIP2, and present a potential, novel therapeutic target for atrial arrhythmias.

application_id: 10929375
activity_code: F32
title: Causal mechanisms of anesthetic induction and emergence in human cortical organoids
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative The mechanisms underlying the loss and recovery of consciousness from general anesthesia remain poorly understood. This project aims to use human cerebral organoids, which are brain-like structures derived from human pluripotent stem cells, to isolate the molecular mechanisms that cause induction and emergence from anesthesia. Completion of this project may not only aid in the development of methods aimed at actively inducing recovery of consciousness from anesthesia, but may also lay the foundation for using human cerebral organoids in the search for interventions for pathological states of unconsciousness, such as coma and vegetative states, for which there currently no treatments or animal models.

application_id: 11060855
activity_code: R25
title: CBEC-KEMRI BIOETHICS TRAINING INITIATIVE
org_name: KENYA MEDICAL RESEARCH INSTITUTE (KEMRI)
phr: Narrative
The proposed program will establish the first blended Masters and Postgraduate Diploma
training in bioethics in Kenya at 2 local universities. The program will also build capacity of
both Kenya and Pakistan-based faculty to support these graduate-level training programs by
offering focused trainings in bioethics pedagogy, mentoring, and scientific writing including
grantsmanship and manuscript development. Rapid capacity building initiatives will involve
training stakeholders from both countries in basic and emerging issues in research and
public health ethics through workshops and certificate courses, with a focus on enhancing
human subject protection, especially in resource-limited settings.

application_id: 11244011
activity_code: F31
title: Role of Dopamine in Alcohol-Enhanced Alzheimer’s Disease Progression
org_name: WAKE FOREST UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer's disease (AD) is worsened by heavy alcohol use, and both AD and alcohol are known to reduce the
function of the neurotransmitter dopamine, which is involved in both the memory problems and the psychiatric
symptoms of AD, including apathy and depression. This study will explore how the dopamine system changes
over time in a mouse model of AD after alcohol exposure, the role of a protein - synaptogyrin-3 (Syngr-3) - in
regulating dopamine system function, and the potential of Syngr-3 as a therapeutic target to slow the
progression of AD symptoms, especially when alcohol is involved.

individuals with motor disabilities, through directly accessing the neural signals driving movement. However,
multiple factors necessitate electrodes being surgically implanted in less desirable cortical regions, due to brain
damage (e.g., stroke), neurovasculature or surgically inaccessible cortex, thus comprising access to the required
neural signals and hindering the clinical translation of BCI technologies to populations that would most benefit
from it. To bridge this gap, in this proposal, we will investigate the amount of whole-body movement information
that can be extracted in a single region of human motor cortex and quantify the degree to which we can modify

application_id: 11041091
activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating the pathophysiology and molecular mechanisms of renal insulin resistance
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2D) is one of the most common chronic diseases in the United States and is
characterized by multi-organ insulin resistance and dysregulated glucose homeostasis. This project will employ
novel methodologies to mechanistically understand renal insulin resistance, its pathophysiology, and the role of
the kidney in perturbed whole-body glucose homeostasis in diabetes. This proposal is relevant to public health
because it has the potential to inform the development of novel therapies for T2D.

In response to the worst overdose crisis in US history and calls for a more effective police response to
behavioral health problems, the growing practice of diverting people with opioid use disorder to treatment
instead of arrest can improve the health outcomes of police encounters with people who use drugs. To better
understand the processes that lead to a police officer’s decision to divert a person to treatment, this project will
1) develop and validate an instrument to measure police stigma toward people who use drugs, 2) use the
Theory of Planned Behavior to identify other key variables that determine police officers’ intentions to divert,
and 3) determine the officer-perceived barriers and facilitators of effective diversion to treatment. Executing

application_id: 11191365
activity_code: F32
title: Defining the role of premature infant microbial dysbiosis in mediating immune development and response to infection
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Narrative
The gut microbiome is significantly altered in premature infants, who are at higher risk for adverse outcomes to
respiratory infections such as respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). Our studies will investigate whether altered state
of the premature microbiome affects the ability of the immune system to effectively deal with infection, and to
define the contributions of the altered gut microbiome to the outcome of RSV infection in preterm infants.
Identifying bacteria and metabolites that might be used to “normalize” the respiratory immune response to RSV
infection will have the potential to improve the outcome of RSV infection in vulnerable premature infants.

application_id: 11256710
activity_code: F31
title: Enhancing proteasomal activity and tau clearance via USP14 reduction in Alzheimer's Disease mouse models
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative dementia that lacks reliable therapies to markedly
slow disease pathology and its symptoms. Pathological tau protein deposition and propagation to various brain
regions correlates strongly with cognitive decline in AD such that strategies to reduce tau burden may be an
effective treatment. The research proposed in this fellowship aims to evaluate a novel in vivo approach for
proteasomal activation to degrade tau, mitigate tau propagation, and alleviate neuronal loss as a potential
therapy for AD.

application_id: 11087705
activity_code: R25
title: R25 Summer Research Program for Undergraduate Students in PharmacoNeuroimmunology (PNI)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: In general, drug use is a significant public health issue. I received the first grant in 1988 examining the potential effect of drugs of abuse on the development and severity of such diseases as AIDS, Tuberculosis and other infectious diseases. We now recognize drugs of abuse serve as cofactors for infectious diseases as well as neurological and neurogenerative diseases. The public health concern is broad encompassing both illicit drug use and the misuse of prescription medications. Prevention and treatment will come from advances at the intersection of the fields of pharmacology, neurosciences, immunology and psychology (hence PNI). Research and training of individuals needs to be fostered as such intersections.

application_id: 10792616
activity_code: K99
title: High-throughput closed-loop direct aberration sensing and correction for multiphoton imaging in live animals
org_name: CORNELL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This research will enable us to study real-time biological processes at the cellular level noninvasively in deep regions (~2 mm) of living systems. The proposed new method for aberration sensing and correction in highly scattering tissues will improve the quality of the excitation focus in multiphoton microscopy, thus, will enhance the signal strength and spatial resolution in deep-tissue imaging. In the long term, this research will allow scientists to investigate the underpinnings of brain functions and complex behaviors, as well as the mechanism of immune response in live animals.

title: BU Summer Institute for Research Education in Biostatistics and Data Science
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: The Boston University Summer Institute for Research Education in Biostatistics and Data Science (BU SIBS)
is a five-week in-residence program, followed by a period of continued remote research, designed to build
skills and to interest undergraduates, recent graduates and early graduate students in the many exciting
opportunities in the field of Biostatistics. The trainees’ future role in public health will be enhanced due to
their early exposure to the foundations of biostatistical analysis, epidemiological analysis, infectious disease
modeling, design and analysis of clinical trials and statistical genetics analysis within a data science
framework.

application_id: 11096844
activity_code: R25
title: Increasing Global AMPERE (Access for Medical Physicists to Education and Research Excellence)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: The AMPERE (Access for Medical Physicists to Education and Research Excellence) program advances U.S. public health by strengthening the pipeline of medical physicists trained to develop and implement innovations in cancer care. By engaging early-career professionals in global research experiences with partners in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), AMPERE enhances American leadership in cancer research, fosters the development of cost-effective technologies, and supports the U.S. goal of reducing cancer mortality. These collaborations provide real-world experience that prepares trainees to improve access to high-quality radiation-based cancer treatments at home and abroad, ultimately expanding the impact of American science and innovation on global health challenges.

application_id: 11014029
activity_code: T32
title: Cardiovascular Innovation Program in Surgery & Engineering
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE An interdisciplinary approach is required to better prepare future surgeons and engineers for the challenges and opportunities in cardiovascular research. The CVISE T32 training program synergizes the training of post- doctoral surgeon-scientists and pre-doctoral graduate engineering students to focus on key modern topics spanning the field of cardiovascular disease diagnosis, treatment, and prevention. Graduates from this highly institutional supported program will acquire the skillsets necessary to transition into independent investigators who are capable of collaboratively investigating cardiovascular diseases, and developing innovations that will have a lasting impact on healthcare and society.

activity_code: F31
title: Segmentation and integration of experience in hippocampal neuronal representations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our brains rely both on the segmentation of experience into distinct memories and on the integration of those episodic memories across space and time to inform our perception of the world and help us make decisions. Event boundaries are conceptual or physical boundaries such as reward locations or environment changes that can segment experience into distinct episodes. This project proposes a novel experimental design and set of analyses focused on illuminating how the hippocampus forms neural representations of segmented episodes through place cells, the firing of place-cell sequences during theta oscillations (“theta sequences”), and multi-neuronal replays during sharp-wave ripples, and how these representations and patterns are altered upon introduction of new event bou...

application_id: 11292090
activity_code: K08
title: Identification of Pathogenic T cells in Axial Spondyloarthritis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Spondyloarthritis is a complex disease with a multitude of manifestations, spanning the joint, skin, gut, and eye. Given the inherent immune restrictions of the eye, we theorize that sampling the human eye during periods of ocular inflammation will enrich for pathogenic immune cells in spondyloarthritis, specifically T cells. We propose to use single-cell RNA sequencing to identify putative pathogenic T cells in the human eye and to assess whether dysregulation of these T cells track with disease activity in other anatomic sites.
